          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
DIARY NO. 48396 AND 31001 OF 2025
1. Delay condoned.
2. After hearing learned counsel for the applicants, we do not
find any ground to entertain these miscellaneous applications,
since the application has not been filed to correct some clerical
or arithmetic error, nor has the applicant shown that the judgment
dated 30.01.2025 in Civil Appeal No. 1298 of 2025 has become non-
executable due to subsequent events. Guidance in this regard may be
taken from the reportable judgment of this Court in Miscellaneous
Application No. 1276/2026 titled as ‘ Fakir Mamad Suleman Sameja &
Ors. v. Adani Ports and Special Economic Zones Ltd. & Ors. ’
pronounced on 12.05.2026 (2026 INSC 483). 
3. Accordingly, the Miscellaneous Applications are dismissed.
Pending  applications,  if  any,  including  application  for
invervention/impleadment shall stand disposed of.
Diary No(s). 39011/2025

--- Page 378 ---
5
4. Delay condoned.
5. The instant miscellaneous application seeking clarification
of the judgment dated 30.01.2025 in Civil Appeal No. 1298 of 2025.
6. After hearing, we find that the Applicants are persons who
had participated in the recruitment process pursuant to several
orders of the High Court extending the timeline for application in
the recruitment process which was the subject matter of dispute in
Civil Appeal No. 1298 of 2025. In our judgment dated 30.01.2025 in
Civil Appeal No. 1298 of 2025, we have found that the final
judgment of the High Court warranted interference and as such, the
High Court’s final order was set aside.
7. In  such  circumstances,  the  application  made  by  the
Applicants during the extended period of application submissions
has been rejected by the State. The Applicants’ plea that they were
otherwise eligible in terms of the judgment dated 30.01.2025, but
had applied during the timeline extended by the High Court,
therefore, cannot be agitated by means of this miscellaneous
application.
8. This  miscellaneous  application  has  not  been  filed  to
correct some clerical or arithmetic error, nor have the applicants
shown that the judgment dated 30.01.2025 in Civil Appeal No. 1298
of  2025  has  become  non-executable  due  to  subsequent  events.
Guidance in this regard may be taken from the reportable judgment

--- Page 379 ---
6
of this Court in Miscellaneous Application No. 1276/2026 titled as
‘Fakir Mamad Suleman Sameja & Ors. v. Adani Ports and Special
Economic Zones Ltd. & Ors. ’ pronounced on 12.05.2026 (2026 INSC
483)
9. Accordingly, the Miscellaneous Application is dismissed.
Pending  applications,  if  any,  including  application  for
intervention/impleadment shall stand disposed of.
SLP(C) No.25455 AND 25459 OF 2025
10. After arguing, learned senior counsel appearing for the
Petitioners  seek  permission  to  withdraw  these  special  leave
petitions and submits that they may be permitted to apply for
review before the High Court.
11. Learned counsel for the State is having no objection for
withdrawal, however, he raised an objection that no liberty should
be given to the Petitioners. At present, we are not commenting on
the same.
12. As prayed by the Petitioners, these special leave petitions
are permitted to be withdrawn. They can apply for review, if
permissible under law.
CONTEMPT PETITION (CIVIL) DIARY NO. 43106 OF 2025
IN CIVIL APPEAL NO. 1297 OF 2025
CONTEMPT PETITION (CIVIL) DIARY NO. 59613 OF 2025
IN CIVIL APPEAL NO. 1297 OF 2025

--- Page 380 ---
7
CONTEMPT PETITION (CIVIL) DIARY NO. 64804 OF 2025
IN CIVIL APPEAL NO. 1297 OF 2025
13. The instant contempt petitions have been filed alleging
non-compliance of judgment dated 30.01.2025 passed in Civil
Appeal No. 1297 of 2025.
14. Contempt Petition (C)Diary No. 59613 of 2025 has been filed
inter alia contending that the candidates who were in reserved
category having higher merit and were required to be migrated
under the unreserved category as per the advertisement, have
wrongly been put in the reserved category merit list.
15. For appreciating the scope of the contempt petitions within
the purview of the directions, it is necessary to refer to the
directions issued in the judgment passed in the aforementioned
Civil Appeal:
“(i)  The  impugned  judgment  of  the  High  Court
permitting the candidates of CTET and STET holders
of neighbouring States to participate in ongoing
selection process to the post of Assistant Teacher
of Primary and Upper Primary schools pursuant to
Advertisement No. 13 stands set-aside;
(ii) The JTET holders who were possessing the
requisite qualification under the 2022 Recruitment
Rules,  prior  to  the  2024  Amendment  and
participated shall be eligible for appointment and
their  result  be  declared  forthwith  and  the
appointments be made strictly on merit;
(iii) We make it clear that CTET holders or STET
holders, who have applied after the judgment of
the High Court or after amendment in the rules or
advertisement  would  not  be  eligible  for  the

--- Page 381 ---
8
recruitment in furtherance to the Advertisement
No. 13/2023.”
 
16. It  has  been  brought  to  our  notice  that  under  the
Advertisement, Paragraph 19 governs the preparation of the merit
list based on the Main Examination with a stipulation that marks
obtained in the mother tongue subject shall only be qualifying
wherein the candidates ought to obtain 30% minimum marks in this
paper; only after obtaining such marks, the other papers shall
be checked. It was clarified that the marks obtained in the
mother tongue would not be included in preparation of the merit
list. Clause (v) of Paragraph 19 further indicates the manner in
which the merit list is to be prepared.
17. Clauses (iv) to (vii) of Paragraph 19 of the Advertisement
are relevant, therefore, reproduced as thus:
“(iv) In case reserved category candidate comes in
merit list prepared for unreserved posts on same
criteria  basis,  calculation  of  such  candidate
shall  be  done  against  admissible  posts  of
unreserved category and their reservation category
shall also remain the same against their names. In
this regard, updated instructions received from
State Government shall be followed.
(v)  Candidates  obtaining  less  than  following
minimum qualifying marks in examination shall not
be included in merit list:
i. Unreserved/Economically Weaker Section (EWS)
-40 (forty) percent
ii. Scheduled Caste/Scheduled Tribe and Women -
32(thirty-two) percent

--- Page 382 ---
9
iii. Most Backward Class (Schedule-1) - 34
(thirty four)percent
iv. Backward Class Schedule-2 - 36.5 (thirty-
six and half) percent
v. Primitive Tribe - 30 (thirty) percent
(vi) Based on above sub-clauses, general merit
list shall be prepared and thereafter reservation
category-wise selection list shall be formed with
respect to vacancies.
(vii)  Candidates  obtaining  less  than  following
minimum qualifying marks in examination shall not
be included in merit list.”
18. As per Paragraph 19(iv), reserved candidates who come in
the merit list prepared for unreserved posts shall be included
merit wise if their marks are above the unreserved candidates.
Therefore, the migration of a candidate of a reserved category
who  secured  higher  marks  is  permitted  to  the  unreserved
category.
19. In view of clause (v) of Paragraph 19, it is clear that the
minimum qualifying marks in the examination make the candidates
eligible for inclusion of their names in the merit list. In the
Unreserved  /  Economically  Weaker  Section  (EWS),  Scheduled
Caste / Scheduled Tribe and Women, Most Backward Class, Backward
Class and Primitive Tribe categories, the respective percentage
of minimum qualifying marks has been specified.  It is further
clarified that in terms of the subclauses referred to in 19(v)
(i) to (v)(v), general merit list was required to be drawn,

--- Page 383 ---
10
thereafter the reservation category wise selection list shall be
formed  with  respect  to  the  vacancies.   It  has  also  been
clarified that the candidates obtaining less than the minimum
qualifying marks in examination shall not be included in the
merit list.
20. Paragraph  20  of  the  Advertisement  relates  to  the
verification  of the  candidates’ certificates  which is  also
relevant therefore, reproduced as under:
“20. Verification of Candidates’ Certificates
After forming draft merit list based on Clause
20 of the prospectus, certificates related to
eligibility/qualification of finally successful
candidates shall be checked by the Commission.
During  checking  of  certificates,  if
verification of claims mentioned in application
form of candidates of any category is not
possible  and  their  candidature  is  not
established for vacancy of that category, then
in  such  situation,  lower  ranked  candidates
shall be invited by the Commission for checking
of certificates in light of availability in
merit list against vacant posts in related
category.”
21. After perusal of the above Paragraphs 19(v) and 20 of the
Advertisement applying both clauses harmoniously, it is clear
that the merit list as per the existing vacancies ought to be
prepared and in case, on verification, any candidate is not
found eligible, the Commission is at liberty to call for the
candidates who are below in the category in the merit list.  It
would not mean that the persons who have scored below the

--- Page 384 ---
11
minimum  qualification  marks  as  prescribed  in  clause  v  of
Paragraph 19, can be included therein.
22. After perusal of the reply filed by the Commission, it
appears that contempt petitioners have not been included in the
merit list because they ‘have secured marks lesser than that of
the last selected candidate in their respective subjects’.  The
intent of the said reply, in prima facie perception is that the
selected  candidates  who  have  scored  above  the  percentage
prescribed category-wise in clause (v) of Paragraph 19 ought to
be put in the merit list.  Therefore, in terms of the conditions
specified in the Advertisement the merit list was required to be
redrawn, if necessary, and the Commission is required to answer
about the status of the contempt petitioners who are alleging
non-compliance of the directions whereby the list was required
to be prepared strictly in accordance with the merit.
 
23. It is needless to observe that in all the categories,
vacancies are available therefore, the plea of non-availability
of the vacancy is not germane to the extent if a candidate
secured marks above the minimum qualifying marks as prescribed
in clause (v) of Paragraph 19 of the Advertisement.
 
24. Simultaneously, the issue of migration observed in 19(iv)
is also required to be explained.

--- Page 385 ---
12
25. In this view of the matter, we direct the Commission to
file a fresh reply indicating the compliance in terms of the
observations made hereinabove in light of the main direction
within a period of four weeks from today.
26. We hope and trust that the Commission shall supply the list
of the  petitioners/intervenors/impleaders  who  fall within the
 purview of the directions as issued hereinabove and if some of
the persons do not fall within the same, reasons thereof be also
specified in the counter affidavit.
27. Rejoinder,  if  any,  may  be  filed  within  two  weeks
thereafter.
28. In view of the foregoing, we postpone the hearing of this
case for a period of six weeks.
29. List on 20th July, 2026.
(GULSHAN KUMAR ARORA)                           (NAND KISHOR)
   DEPUTY REGISTRAR                          ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

--- Page 386 ---

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
DIARY NO. 48396 AND 31001 OF 2025
1. Delay condoned.
2. After hearing learned counsel for the applicants, we do not
find any ground to entertain these miscellaneous applications,
since the application has not been filed to correct some clerical
or arithmetic error, nor has the applicant shown that the judgment
dated 30.01.2025 in Civil Appeal No. 1298 of 2025 has become non-
executable due to subsequent events. Guidance in this regard may be
taken from the reportable judgment of this Court in Miscellaneous
Application No. 1276/2026 titled as ‘ Fakir Mamad Suleman Sameja &
Ors. v. Adani Ports and Special Economic Zones Ltd. & Ors. ’
pronounced on 12.05.2026 (2026 INSC 483). 
3. Accordingly, the Miscellaneous Applications are dismissed.
Pending  applications,  if  any,  including  application  for
invervention/impleadment shall stand disposed of.
Diary No(s). 39011/2025

--- Page 390 ---
5
4. Delay condoned.
5. The instant miscellaneous application seeking clarification
of the judgment dated 30.01.2025 in Civil Appeal No. 1298 of 2025.
6. After hearing, we find that the Applicants are persons who
had participated in the recruitment process pursuant to several
orders of the High Court extending the timeline for application in
the recruitment process which was the subject matter of dispute in
Civil Appeal No. 1298 of 2025. In our judgment dated 30.01.2025 in
Civil Appeal No. 1298 of 2025, we have found that the final
judgment of the High Court warranted interference and as such, the
High Court’s final order was set aside.
7. In  such  circumstances,  the  application  made  by  the
Applicants during the extended period of application submissions
has been rejected by the State. The Applicants’ plea that they were
otherwise eligible in terms of the judgment dated 30.01.2025, but
had applied during the timeline extended by the High Court,
therefore, cannot be agitated by means of this miscellaneous
application.
8. This  miscellaneous  application  has  not  been  filed  to
correct some clerical or arithmetic error, nor have the applicants
shown that the judgment dated 30.01.2025 in Civil Appeal No. 1298
of  2025  has  become  non-executable  due  to  subsequent  events.
Guidance in this regard may be taken from the reportable judgment

--- Page 391 ---
6
of this Court in Miscellaneous Application No. 1276/2026 titled as
‘Fakir Mamad Suleman Sameja & Ors. v. Adani Ports and Special
Economic Zones Ltd. & Ors. ’ pronounced on 12.05.2026 (2026 INSC
483)
9. Accordingly, the Miscellaneous Application is dismissed.
Pending  applications,  if  any,  including  application  for
intervention/impleadment shall stand disposed of.
SLP(C) No.25455 AND 25459 OF 2025

Petitioners  seek  permission  to  withdraw  these  special  leave
petitions and submits that they may be permitted to apply for

CONTEMPT PETITION (CIVIL) DIARY NO. 64804 OF 2025
IN CIVIL APPEAL NO. 1297 OF 2025
13. The instant contempt petitions have been filed alleging
non-compliance of judgment dated 30.01.2025 passed in Civil
Appeal No. 1297 of 2025.
14. Contempt Petition (C)Diary No. 59613 of 2025 has been filed
inter alia contending that the candidates who were in reserved
category having higher merit and were required to be migrated
under the unreserved category as per the advertisement, have
wrongly been put in the reserved category merit list.
15. For appreciating the scope of the contempt petitions within
the purview of the directions, it is necessary to refer to the
directions issued in the judgment passed in the aforementioned
Civil Appeal:
“(i)  The  impugned  judgment  of  the  High  Court
permitting the candidates of CTET and STET holders
of neighbouring States to participate in ongoing
selection process to the post of Assistant Teacher
of Primary and Upper Primary schools pursuant to
Advertisement No. 13 stands set-aside;
(ii) The JTET holders who were possessing the
requisite qualification under the 2022 Recruitment
Rules,  prior  to  the  2024  Amendment  and
participated shall be eligible for appointment and
their  result  be  declared  forthwith  and  the
appointments be made strictly on merit;
(iii) We make it clear that CTET holders or STET
holders, who have applied after the judgment of
the High Court or after amendment in the rules or
advertisement  would  not  be  eligible  for  the

--- Page 393 ---
8
recruitment in furtherance to the Advertisement
No. 13/2023.”
 
16. It  has  been  brought  to  our  notice  that  under  the
Advertisement, Paragraph 19 governs the preparation of the merit
list based on the Main Examination with a stipulation that marks
obtained in the mother tongue subject shall only be qualifying
wherein the candidates ought to obtain 30% minimum marks in this
paper; only after obtaining such marks, the other papers shall
be checked. It was clarified that the marks obtained in the
mother tongue would not be included in preparation of the merit
list. Clause (v) of Paragraph 19 further indicates the manner in
which the merit list is to be prepared.
17. Clauses (iv) to (vii) of Paragraph 19 of the Advertisement
are relevant, therefore, reproduced as thus:
“(iv) In case reserved category candidate comes in
merit list prepared for unreserved posts on same
criteria  basis,  calculation  of  such  candidate
shall  be  done  against  admissible  posts  of
unreserved category and their reservation category
shall also remain the same against their names. In
this regard, updated instructions received from
State Government shall be followed.
(v)  Candidates  obtaining  less  than  following
minimum qualifying marks in examination shall not
be included in merit list:
i. Unreserved/Economically Weaker Section (EWS)
-40 (forty) percent
ii. Scheduled Caste/Scheduled Tribe and Women -
32(thirty-two) percent

--- Page 394 ---
9
iii. Most Backward Class (Schedule-1) - 34
(thirty four)percent
iv. Backward Class Schedule-2 - 36.5 (thirty-
six and half) percent
v. Primitive Tribe - 30 (thirty) percent
(vi) Based on above sub-clauses, general merit
list shall be prepared and thereafter reservation
category-wise selection list shall be formed with
respect to vacancies.
(vii)  Candidates  obtaining  less  than  following
minimum qualifying marks in examination shall not
be included in merit list.”
18. As per Paragraph 19(iv), reserved candidates who come in
the merit list prepared for unreserved posts shall be included
merit wise if their marks are above the unreserved candidates.
Therefore, the migration of a candidate of a reserved category
who  secured  higher  marks  is  permitted  to  the  unreserved
category.
19. In view of clause (v) of Paragraph 19, it is clear that the
minimum qualifying marks in the examination make the candidates
eligible for inclusion of their names in the merit list. In the
Unreserved  /  Economically  Weaker  Section  (EWS),  Scheduled
Caste / Scheduled Tribe and Women, Most Backward Class, Backward
Class and Primitive Tribe categories, the respective percentage
of minimum qualifying marks has been specified.  It is further
clarified that in terms of the subclauses referred to in 19(v)
(i) to (v)(v), general merit list was required to be drawn,

--- Page 395 ---
10
thereafter the reservation category wise selection list shall be
formed  with  respect  to  the  vacancies.   It  has  also  been
clarified that the candidates obtaining less than the minimum
qualifying marks in examination shall not be included in the
merit list.
20. Paragraph  20  of  the  Advertisement  relates  to  the
verification  of the  candidates’ certificates  which is  also
relevant therefore, reproduced as under:
“20. Verification of Candidates’ Certificates
After forming draft merit list based on Clause
20 of the prospectus, certificates related to
eligibility/qualification of finally successful
candidates shall be checked by the Commission.
During  checking  of  certificates,  if
verification of claims mentioned in application
form of candidates of any category is not
possible  and  their  candidature  is  not
established for vacancy of that category, then
in  such  situation,  lower  ranked  candidates
shall be invited by the Commission for checking
of certificates in light of availability in
merit list against vacant posts in related
category.”
21. After perusal of the above Paragraphs 19(v) and 20 of the
Advertisement applying both clauses harmoniously, it is clear
that the merit list as per the existing vacancies ought to be
prepared and in case, on verification, any candidate is not
found eligible, the Commission is at liberty to call for the
candidates who are below in the category in the merit list.  It
would not mean that the persons who have scored below the

--- Page 396 ---
11
minimum  qualification  marks  as  prescribed  in  clause  v  of
Paragraph 19, can be included therein.
22. After perusal of the reply filed by the Commission, it
appears that contempt petitioners have not been included in the
merit list because they ‘have secured marks lesser than that of
the last selected candidate in their respective subjects’.  The
intent of the said reply, in prima facie perception is that the
selected  candidates  who  have  scored  above  the  percentage
prescribed category-wise in clause (v) of Paragraph 19 ought to
be put in the merit list.  Therefore, in terms of the conditions
specified in the Advertisement the merit list was required to be
redrawn, if necessary, and the Commission is required to answer
about the status of the contempt petitioners who are alleging
non-compliance of the directions whereby the list was required
to be prepared strictly in accordance with the merit.
 
23. It is needless to observe that in all the categories,
vacancies are available therefore, the plea of non-availability
of the vacancy is not germane to the extent if a candidate
secured marks above the minimum qualifying marks as prescribed
in clause (v) of Paragraph 19 of the Advertisement.
 
24. Simultaneously, the issue of migration observed in 19(iv)
is also required to be explained.

--- Page 397 ---
12
25. In this view of the matter, we direct the Commission to
file a fresh reply indicating the compliance in terms of the
observations made hereinabove in light of the main direction
within a period of four weeks from today.
26. We hope and trust that the Commission shall supply the list
of the  petitioners/intervenors/impleaders  who  fall within the

the persons do not fall within the same, reasons thereof be also

case for a period of six weeks.

SLP(C)  No.16087/2017
ITEM NO.44               COURT NO.7               SECTION IX
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No.16087/2017

                             O R D E R
Heard Mr. S.D. Sanjay, learned Additional Solicitor
General (ASG), Convenor and Coordinator of the Committee
constituted by this Court, Ms. Mukta Gupta, learned Senior
Advocate, Mr. H.S. Phoolka, learned Senior Advocate and
Dr.P.M. Nair, former IPS Officer. 
2. Having regard to the fact that Ms. Aparna Bhat,
learned Senior Counsel who is active in the field of
issues relating to human trafficking, we add her as a
Member of the team constituted by this Court. She is also
present in Court and the Court has interacted with her.
3. We have been apprised that the Committee has already
met five times and certain specific proposals have been
deliberated and the same have been disclosed in the status
5

--- Page 415 ---
SLP (CRL.)  NO. 11263/2025
report filed by Mr. S.D. Sanjay, learned ASG, enclosing
the minutes of all the five meetings. The same is taken on
record.
4. Having  discussed  the  matter  at  length,  for  the
present, the following directions are issued:
(i) The Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of
India is directed to set into place an all India
grid connecting each and every police station in
the country on one platform, which shall have a
special  portal  dedicated  to  human  trafficking,
including  missing  children  and  women.  At  this
stage, a suggestion has come that the portal would
be more meaningful if linked with the portal of
Mission  Vatsalya  and  the  Crime  and  Criminal
Tracking  Network  System  (CCTNS).  We  find  the
suggestion to be useful. Accordingly, a direction
is issued to come up with a module integrating all
the three portals together.
(ii)  All  Police  Stations  are  directed  to
immediately  register  an  FIR  the  moment  any
information reaches them with regard to any person
missing,  without  waiting  for  any  preliminary
enquiry or leaving it to the guardians or family
members of the missing person to first search for
him/her. We add a note of caution that if the
6

--- Page 416 ---
SLP (CRL.)  NO. 11263/2025
missing person is restored to his/her family within
24 hours, the head of the police force of the
concerned district shall not forward a copy of the
FIR to the jurisdictional Court, and the matter may
stand closed at that level itself. However, this
shall not mean that the police machinery would
remain  inactive.  On  the  contrary,  the  police
machinery shall be activated immediately, at its
highest level, for tracing and locating the missing
person, as such prompt action is worth the exercise
even if the person is traced or returns within 24
hours, rather than waiting for the expiry of 24
hours before initiating action, by which time, it
may already be too late. Needless to overemphasise,
the initial few hours from the time any person goes
missing are the “golden hours” during which the
chances of safe recovery are at their maximum. 
(iii)  The  FIR  so  registered  shall  mandatorily
incorporate the relevant provisions of the Bharatiya
Nyaya  Sanhita,  2023  and  any  other  applicable
statutory  provisions  relating  to  kidnapping,
abduction or trafficking of a person or child.
(iv)  Immediately  upon  the  investigating
agency/police having sufficient reason to believe
that the case relates to trafficking, and without
7

--- Page 417 ---
SLP (CRL.)  NO. 11263/2025
waiting for the expiry of the four months’ period,
the matter shall be transferred to the specialised
unit, if constituted in the area, dealing with
offences relating to human trafficking, kidnapping,
abduction and allied offences.
(v) At his juncture, Dr. P.M. Nair has informed the
Court that Anti Human Trafficking Units (AHTUs)
have  been  constituted  on  paper  throughout  the
country, but many of them are either non-functional
on the ground or lack adequate powers, manpower and
resources to effectively deal with such offences.
Taking note of the seriousness and urgency of the
matter, a mandamus is issued to the Union of India
as well as to all States and Union Territories
(UTs), through their respective Home Secretaries
and Directors General of Police, to ensure that all
AHTUs are made fully functional and operational
within four weeks from today. 
(vi) Learned counsel appearing for the States/UTs
shall  communicate  the  present  order  to  the
concerned  authorities  for  strict  and  immediate
implementation.
(vii) The moment any person, including a child, is
recovered, it shall be ensured without any delay
that the person is restored to his / her family in
8

--- Page 418 ---
SLP (CRL.)  NO. 11263/2025
accordance with law, subject to due verification
that the person is being restored to the rightful
guardian.  Procedural  formalities  shall  not  be
pleaded as a defence in the event of inordinate
delay in restoration. However, in cases where it is
found that the victim was trafficked with the
involvement  or  connivance  of  the  family  or
guardians, the victim shall not be restored to such
family and immediate responsibility for care and
protection shall vest in the State authorities,
including the Child Welfare Committees (CWCs).
(viii) We endorse and direct that the moment any
missing person is recovered or rescued, he or she
shall immediately be taken for Aadhaar verification
or for issuance of an Aadhaar Card, as the case may
be. This direction is being issued in view of the
fact that for the purpose of procuring an Aadhaar
Card, finger prints and other biometric details are
captured, and the moment any attempt is made by the
same person to obtain another Aadhaar Card, the
system would reveal whether such person already
possesses  one,  thereby  greatly  facilitating
identification and restoration of the person to his
or her family. The verification of the Aadhaar Card
may  be  carried  out  by  any  readily  available
9

--- Page 419 ---
SLP (CRL.)  NO. 11263/2025
authorised agency so as to avoid any delay or loss
of valuable time.   
(ix) The concerned District Child Protection Units
(DCPUs) where the person is found or rescued, shall
discharge  their  statutory  obligations  with  due
diligence and utmost priority. The Juvenile Justice
Committees of the High Courts are requested to
monitor compliance in this regard. We also direct
that all agencies, including the District Legal
Services  Authorities  and  similar  institutions,
shall  fully  cooperate  and  coordinate  with  the
concerned authorities in this exercise.
(x) We further endorse and direct the authorities
concerned to examine the feasibility of issuing
Aadhaar Cards at the place of birth itself, subject
to voluntary consent of the parents/ guardians.
5. Before concluding, we deem it appropriate to address
an issue highlighted by the learned ASG concerning the
appointment  and  functioning  of  Nodal  Officers  by  the
States and UTs pursuant to earlier orders of this Court.
It has been brought to our notice that Nodal Officers are
often  appointed  in  a  casual  manner  and  on  several
occasions, they do not even attend the conferences or
meetings  convened  in  connection  with  the  present
10

--- Page 420 ---
SLP (CRL.)  NO. 11263/2025
proceedings, nor do they respond appropriately. It has
further  been  noticed  that  they  frequently  depute
subordinate officers to represent them at such meetings or
conferences. In many instances, Nodal Officers have also
been changed at very short intervals, thereby affecting
continuity and effective coordination. 
6. Keeping the aforesaid concerns in mind, we direct all
the Home Secretaries and Directors General of Police of
the  respective  States  and  UTs  to  designate,  for  the
present Committee, a Nodal Officer of proven integrity and
competence, preferably possessing experience in matters of
this nature. The said Nodal Officer shall be duty-bound to
promptly respond to and extend all assistance sought in
relation to the functioning of the Committee. Under no
circumstances shall such officer delegate or pass on the
said responsibility to any colleague or junior officer.
The Nodal Officer shall further ensure that all steps
taken, reports generated, or suggestions made by their
respective States or UTs in relation to the issues pending
before this Court are promptly communicated to Mr. S.D.
Sanjay, learned ASG and Convenor of the Committee, as well
as to the Secretary, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government
of India, the Departments of Home of all the States or
UTs, the Directors General of Police, and the Secretaries
of  the  Women  and  Child  Welfare  Departments  of  the
11

--- Page 421 ---
SLP (CRL.)  NO. 11263/2025
respective States and UTs.
7. Before the next date of hearing, an updated status
report, along with details of the action taken by the
Union of India, the States, the UTs, and the concerned
Departments, shall be placed on record by way of an
affidavit  personally  affirmed  by  the  respective  Nodal
Officers. A copy of the same shall also be forwarded
through e-mail to the Coordinator of the Committee prior
to its filing before this Court. 
8. List on 05.08.2026 at 2.00 p.m.
 (POOJA SHARMA)                                (ANJALI PANWAR)
    AR-CUM-PS                               ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
12

--- Page 422 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
EXTRAORDINARY APPELLATE JURISDICTION
PETITION FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (CRL.) NO.6836/2026
ADITYA JAIN                                        PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB & ANR.                             RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Our order dated 20.04.2026 reads thus:-
“1. Heard Mr.Sidharth Luthra, learned Senior counsel for the
petitioner, Mr. N Hariharan, learned Senior counsel for the
de-facto complainant-caveator and Mr.Mohit Siwach, learned
counsel for the State of Punjab.
2. Let the de-facto complainant be impleaded as a party
respondent  no.2  in  this  case.  Cause  title  be  amended
accordingly.
3. Issue notice to the respondents. 
4. Notices are accepted by Mr. Chritarth Palli, and Mr.Karan
Sharma, learned AOR for the respondents/caveator(s). 
5.  We  would  like  to  know  from  the  State  their  stand
especially with regard to the observation of the High Court
in  the  impugned  order  in  paragraph  9.  Paragraph  9  is
extracted hereinbelow:
“During the course of investigation, it has come on
record that fake bills were allegedly prepared by
using forged seals and signatures to falsely show
delivery of gold to the complainant. This clearly
indicates a deliberate and calculated intention on
the  part  of  the  petitioner  to  cheat  the
complainant. Once such allegations of forgery and
fabrication of documents come into picture, the
matter is no longer civil and takes the shape of a
criminal offence.” 
6. Till the next date of hearing in the event of arrest, the
petitioner  shall  be  released  on  bail  subject  to  the

--- Page 423 ---
2
satisfaction of the concerned Investigating Officer in FIR
No.61  dated  19.02.2026  registered  with  Police  Station
Division No.8, Ludhiana, Punjab, for the offences punishable
under  Sections  316(2),  318(4)  of  the  Bharatiya  Nyaya
Sanhita, 2023 (for short, ‘BNS’) as well as Sections 338,
336(3) & 340(2) of 3 BNS, added vide DDR No.37 dated
26.03.2026. 
7.  The  petitioner  shall  continue  to  cooperate  in  the
investigations.
8. Since there are civil and criminal disputes were pending,
the  parties  have  voluntarily  suggested  that  without
prejudice to the rights and contention, they are prepared to
sit across.
9. We appoint Hon’ble Mr.Justice Anupinder Singh Grewal,
retired Judge, Punjab & Haryana High Court to mediate the
dispute between the parties. The fee and other expenses
payable to the Mediator shall be settled after consultation
with the parties. 
10. List on 08th May, 2026.”
2. By our order dated 08.05.2026, we recorded that the mediation
between the parties was successful and the parties have been able
to resolve the dispute amicably.
3. Today, when the matter was taken up for further hearing, the
learned counsel for the petitioner produced a settlement, reduced
into writing, duly signed by the parties and the learned Mediator.
The settlement agreement reads thus:-
MEMORANDUM OF UNDERSTANDING-CUM-SETTLEMENT/
COMPROMISE-DEED
This memorandum of understanding/settlement/compromise-deed is
entered in between Jagdish Jewelers Private Limited (GST
No.04AABCJ6834B2ZZ), SCO No.2443-44, Sector 22-C, Chandigarh,
through its Directors Mr. Sagar Singla, Mr. Susham Singla and
Mr. Akash Singla (hereinafter referred to as "first party")
And

--- Page 424 ---
3
Mr. Aditya Jain, Mrs. Aakriti Jain wife of Mr. Aditya Jain,
Proprietors/Directors  of  SRK  Jewellers  (GST
No.03ABLPJ7962Q1ZX), Shop No. 15, First Floor, Mall Plaza,
Near  Fountain  Chowk,  Ludhiana:  Pvt.  Ltd.  (GST
Proprietors/Directors  of  Finegold  Jewellers
No.03AAFCF0462MIZR), First Floor. Shop No.14-A. Mall Plaza,
Near Fountain Chowk, Ludhiana and Proprietors/Directors of
Shree Rama Krishna Jewellers (GST No.03AKRPJ0192PIZQ), Lower
Ground Floor, Shop No.2-B. Mall Road, Ludhiana (hereinafter
referred to as "Second Party").
WHEREAS, on this 7 th day of May. 2026, at Chandigarh, this
settlement/compromise-deed  is  being  executed  between  the
parties, on the following terms and conditions: -
1. That a business dispute regarding dealing of Gold Bars as
well as Silver Bars and jewellery articles had arisen in-
between both parties.
2. That in pursuance of the business transactions, the second
party i.e. Mr. Aditya Jain had instituted two civil suits,
i.e. Civil Suits No.7322 of 2025 and Civil Suit No.7282 of
2025 before the Court of Civil Judge (Junior Division) at
Ludhiana.
3. The second party had also levelled allegation against the
first party, by filing complaint dated 08/12/2025 before the
Commissioner of Police at Ludhiana. Besides, the second party
had also filed complaint dated 02/02/2025, alleging that the
first party had threatened the second party and had wrongly
trespassed  into  his  property,  lastly  the  second  party
thereafter  on  03/01/2026  filed  another  complaint/
representation before the police authorities at Ludhiana.
4. That similarly, the first party had also filed a detailed
and  comprehensive  complaint  dated  16/12/2025  against  the
second party. Pursuant to the complaint filed by first party,
the  Second  party  instituted  two  separate  Criminal
Miscellaneous petitions before the Hon'ble High Court of
Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh i.e. CRM-M 1035 of 2026 as
well as CRM-M No.2133 of 2026.
5. That both the Criminal miscellaneous petitions i.e. CRM-M
1035 of 2026 as well as CRM-M No. 2133 of 2026, filed by the
second party, were disposed of vide orders dated 16/01/2026
and 22/01/2026, by the Hon'ble Punjab & Haryana High Court.
6. That thereafter the complaints filed by both the parties
were investigated by the police authorities, which culminated
into registration of an FIR No.61 dated 19/02/2026 registered
under sections 316(2), 318(4), 338, 336(3) & 340(2) of BNS
Act, registered against the second party.
7. That upon registration of FIR No.61 dated 19/02/2026, the
second  party  i.e.  Aditya  Jain  approached  the  Court  of
Additional Session Judge at Ludhiana, seeking Anticipatory

--- Page 425 ---
4
Bail, which was dismissed on 18.03.2026, by the court of
Additional Session Judge at Ludhiana. On 19.03.2026, the
second party, i.e. Aditya Jain, approached the Hon'ble High
Court seeking quashing of FIR No 61 dated 19/02/2026 by filing
CRM-M 15622 of 2026 which is pending before the Hon'ble High
Court of Punjab and Haryana as on date.
8. That in the meantime, aggrieved against the order of
dismissal of Anticipatory bail by the ADJ, Ludhiana, second
party i.e. Aditya Jain, approached the Hon'ble High Court of
Punjab & Haryana for grant of concession of anticipatory bail,
by filing CRM-M 17617 of 2026. However, that too met the same
fate and the bail application of the second party (Aditya
Jain) stood rejected by the Hon'ble Punjab and Haryana High
Court on 06.04.2026.
9.  That  aggrieved  against  the  order  of  rejection  of
Anticipatory bail by the Hon'ble Punjab & Haryana High Court
at  Chandigarh,  the  second  party  i.e.  Mr.  Aditya  Jain,
approached the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India by preferring
SLP (Crl.) No.6836/2026 seeking pre-arrest bail.
10. That vide order dated 20/04/2026. passed by the Hon'ble
Supreme Court of India, the Second Party (Aditya Jain) was
ordered to be released on ad interim bail in FIR No.61 dated
19/02/2026,  subject  to  the  satisfaction  of  concerned
investigating officer, that since both the parties i.e. the
First & Second party had voluntary suggested that since there
are both civil and criminal proceedings initiated by and
against each other, thus without prejudice to their rights and
contentions, both the parties were willing to sit across and
put a quietus to the dispute by way of an amicable settlement.
11. That it was in pursuance to the aforesaid proposal and
suggestion made by both the parties to this settlement, that
the Hon'ble Supreme Court had appointed Hon'ble Mr. Justice
Anupinder Singh Grewal (Retd.) as a Mediator to mediate the
dispute between the parties to this settlement.
12. That both the parties agreed that the undersigned would
act as their mediator/conciliator in the matter of mediation
and conciliation proceedings.
13. That meetings were held with the parties and with the
assistance of Mediator/Conciliator along with their relatives,
respectable and elders voluntarily arrived at an amicable
settlement/ solution resolving the above-mentioned disputes
and differences that had arisen.
14. That the parties hereto confirm and declare that they have
voluntarily and out of their own free will, without any
pressure from any quarter, arrived at this memorandum of
understanding/settlement/compromise- deed in the presence of
the Mediator/Conciliator which are as follows:-
(A) That at the time of signing of this compromise-deed,

--- Page 426 ---
5
it has been assured by the second party that their son,
namely, Arnav Jain has no concern whatsoever with either
of the firms maintained by the second party as on date.
(B) That the parties have mutually agreed to resolve
their dispute by way of an amicable resolution whereby
the  entire  dispute  has  been  settled  for  a  total
consideration of Rs.6,00,00,000/- (Rs. Six crores only),
which the second party has undertaken to pay to the first
party towards full and final settlement, including all
past and present business transactions, in between the
parties as on the date of signing this agreement. The
payment shall be made in the following manner:-
(i) The second party shall make the payment of

Jain undertakes to get this settlement-deed signed from Aditya
Jain as and when he is released from custody.

this settlement- deed entered in between both the parties is
in the shape of a compromise deed and duly enforceable in law
and thus both the parties shall be free to produce, place on
record  the  above  settlement-deed  before  any  competent

controversy between the parties. Further, the bail application
filed by any of the second party shall not be opposed by the
first party, who shall also extend full cooperation in the
quashing of the FIR.

and agents of both the parties shall remain bound by the terms
and conditions of the present settlement-deed.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
EXTRAORDINARY APPELLATE JURISDICTION
PETITION FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (CRL.) NO.6836/2026
ADITYA JAIN                                        PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB & ANR.                             RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Our order dated 20.04.2026 reads thus:-
“1. Heard Mr.Sidharth Luthra, learned Senior counsel for the
petitioner, Mr. N Hariharan, learned Senior counsel for the
de-facto complainant-caveator and Mr.Mohit Siwach, learned
counsel for the State of Punjab.
2. Let the de-facto complainant be impleaded as a party
respondent  no.2  in  this  case.  Cause  title  be  amended
accordingly.
3. Issue notice to the respondents. 
4. Notices are accepted by Mr. Chritarth Palli, and Mr.Karan
Sharma, learned AOR for the respondents/caveator(s). 
5.  We  would  like  to  know  from  the  State  their  stand
especially with regard to the observation of the High Court
in  the  impugned  order  in  paragraph  9.  Paragraph  9  is
extracted hereinbelow:
“During the course of investigation, it has come on
record that fake bills were allegedly prepared by
using forged seals and signatures to falsely show
delivery of gold to the complainant. This clearly
indicates a deliberate and calculated intention on
the  part  of  the  petitioner  to  cheat  the
complainant. Once such allegations of forgery and
fabrication of documents come into picture, the
matter is no longer civil and takes the shape of a
criminal offence.” 
6. Till the next date of hearing in the event of arrest, the
petitioner  shall  be  released  on  bail  subject  to  the

--- Page 434 ---
2
satisfaction of the concerned Investigating Officer in FIR
No.61  dated  19.02.2026  registered  with  Police  Station
Division No.8, Ludhiana, Punjab, for the offences punishable
under  Sections  316(2),  318(4)  of  the  Bharatiya  Nyaya
Sanhita, 2023 (for short, ‘BNS’) as well as Sections 338,
336(3) & 340(2) of 3 BNS, added vide DDR No.37 dated
26.03.2026. 
7.  The  petitioner  shall  continue  to  cooperate  in  the
investigations.
8. Since there are civil and criminal disputes were pending,
the  parties  have  voluntarily  suggested  that  without
prejudice to the rights and contention, they are prepared to
sit across.
9. We appoint Hon’ble Mr.Justice Anupinder Singh Grewal,
retired Judge, Punjab & Haryana High Court to mediate the
dispute between the parties. The fee and other expenses
payable to the Mediator shall be settled after consultation
with the parties. 
10. List on 08th May, 2026.”
2. By our order dated 08.05.2026, we recorded that the mediation
between the parties was successful and the parties have been able
to resolve the dispute amicably.
3. Today, when the matter was taken up for further hearing, the
learned counsel for the petitioner produced a settlement, reduced
into writing, duly signed by the parties and the learned Mediator.
The settlement agreement reads thus:-
MEMORANDUM OF UNDERSTANDING-CUM-SETTLEMENT/
COMPROMISE-DEED
This memorandum of understanding/settlement/compromise-deed is
entered in between Jagdish Jewelers Private Limited (GST
No.04AABCJ6834B2ZZ), SCO No.2443-44, Sector 22-C, Chandigarh,
through its Directors Mr. Sagar Singla, Mr. Susham Singla and
Mr. Akash Singla (hereinafter referred to as "first party")
And

--- Page 435 ---
3
Mr. Aditya Jain, Mrs. Aakriti Jain wife of Mr. Aditya Jain,
Proprietors/Directors  of  SRK  Jewellers  (GST
No.03ABLPJ7962Q1ZX), Shop No. 15, First Floor, Mall Plaza,
Near  Fountain  Chowk,  Ludhiana:  Pvt.  Ltd.  (GST
Proprietors/Directors  of  Finegold  Jewellers
No.03AAFCF0462MIZR), First Floor. Shop No.14-A. Mall Plaza,
Near Fountain Chowk, Ludhiana and Proprietors/Directors of
Shree Rama Krishna Jewellers (GST No.03AKRPJ0192PIZQ), Lower
Ground Floor, Shop No.2-B. Mall Road, Ludhiana (hereinafter
referred to as "Second Party").
WHEREAS, on this 7 th day of May. 2026, at Chandigarh, this
settlement/compromise-deed  is  being  executed  between  the
parties, on the following terms and conditions: -
1. That a business dispute regarding dealing of Gold Bars as
well as Silver Bars and jewellery articles had arisen in-
between both parties.
2. That in pursuance of the business transactions, the second
party i.e. Mr. Aditya Jain had instituted two civil suits,
i.e. Civil Suits No.7322 of 2025 and Civil Suit No.7282 of
2025 before the Court of Civil Judge (Junior Division) at
Ludhiana.
3. The second party had also levelled allegation against the
first party, by filing complaint dated 08/12/2025 before the
Commissioner of Police at Ludhiana. Besides, the second party
had also filed complaint dated 02/02/2025, alleging that the
first party had threatened the second party and had wrongly
trespassed  into  his  property,  lastly  the  second  party
thereafter  on  03/01/2026  filed  another  complaint/
representation before the police authorities at Ludhiana.
4. That similarly, the first party had also filed a detailed
and  comprehensive  complaint  dated  16/12/2025  against  the
second party. Pursuant to the complaint filed by first party,
the  Second  party  instituted  two  separate  Criminal
Miscellaneous petitions before the Hon'ble High Court of
Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh i.e. CRM-M 1035 of 2026 as
well as CRM-M No.2133 of 2026.
5. That both the Criminal miscellaneous petitions i.e. CRM-M
1035 of 2026 as well as CRM-M No. 2133 of 2026, filed by the
second party, were disposed of vide orders dated 16/01/2026
and 22/01/2026, by the Hon'ble Punjab & Haryana High Court.
6. That thereafter the complaints filed by both the parties
were investigated by the police authorities, which culminated
into registration of an FIR No.61 dated 19/02/2026 registered
under sections 316(2), 318(4), 338, 336(3) & 340(2) of BNS
Act, registered against the second party.
7. That upon registration of FIR No.61 dated 19/02/2026, the
second  party  i.e.  Aditya  Jain  approached  the  Court  of
Additional Session Judge at Ludhiana, seeking Anticipatory

--- Page 436 ---
4
Bail, which was dismissed on 18.03.2026, by the court of
Additional Session Judge at Ludhiana. On 19.03.2026, the
second party, i.e. Aditya Jain, approached the Hon'ble High
Court seeking quashing of FIR No 61 dated 19/02/2026 by filing
CRM-M 15622 of 2026 which is pending before the Hon'ble High
Court of Punjab and Haryana as on date.
8. That in the meantime, aggrieved against the order of
dismissal of Anticipatory bail by the ADJ, Ludhiana, second
party i.e. Aditya Jain, approached the Hon'ble High Court of
Punjab & Haryana for grant of concession of anticipatory bail,
by filing CRM-M 17617 of 2026. However, that too met the same
fate and the bail application of the second party (Aditya
Jain) stood rejected by the Hon'ble Punjab and Haryana High
Court on 06.04.2026.
9.  That  aggrieved  against  the  order  of  rejection  of
Anticipatory bail by the Hon'ble Punjab & Haryana High Court
at  Chandigarh,  the  second  party  i.e.  Mr.  Aditya  Jain,
approached the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India by preferring
SLP (Crl.) No.6836/2026 seeking pre-arrest bail.
10. That vide order dated 20/04/2026. passed by the Hon'ble
Supreme Court of India, the Second Party (Aditya Jain) was
ordered to be released on ad interim bail in FIR No.61 dated
19/02/2026,  subject  to  the  satisfaction  of  concerned
investigating officer, that since both the parties i.e. the
First & Second party had voluntary suggested that since there
are both civil and criminal proceedings initiated by and
against each other, thus without prejudice to their rights and
contentions, both the parties were willing to sit across and
put a quietus to the dispute by way of an amicable settlement.
11. That it was in pursuance to the aforesaid proposal and
suggestion made by both the parties to this settlement, that
the Hon'ble Supreme Court had appointed Hon'ble Mr. Justice
Anupinder Singh Grewal (Retd.) as a Mediator to mediate the
dispute between the parties to this settlement.
12. That both the parties agreed that the undersigned would
act as their mediator/conciliator in the matter of mediation
and conciliation proceedings.
13. That meetings were held with the parties and with the
assistance of Mediator/Conciliator along with their relatives,
respectable and elders voluntarily arrived at an amicable
settlement/ solution resolving the above-mentioned disputes
and differences that had arisen.
14. That the parties hereto confirm and declare that they have
voluntarily and out of their own free will, without any
pressure from any quarter, arrived at this memorandum of
understanding/settlement/compromise- deed in the presence of
the Mediator/Conciliator which are as follows:-
(A) That at the time of signing of this compromise-deed,

--- Page 437 ---
5
it has been assured by the second party that their son,
namely, Arnav Jain has no concern whatsoever with either
of the firms maintained by the second party as on date.
(B) That the parties have mutually agreed to resolve
their dispute by way of an amicable resolution whereby
the  entire  dispute  has  been  settled  for  a  total
consideration of Rs.6,00,00,000/- (Rs. Six crores only),
which the second party has undertaken to pay to the first
party towards full and final settlement, including all
past and present business transactions, in between the
parties as on the date of signing this agreement. The

(i) The second party shall make the payment of

(ii) The balance payment of Rs.4,00,00,000/- (Rs.

record  the  above  settlement-deed  before  any  competent
authority or court if the same is required to witness the
execution  of  settlement-deed  or  to  settle  any  pending
controversy between the parties. Further, the bail application
filed by any of the second party shall not be opposed by the
first party, who shall also extend full cooperation in the
quashing of the FIR.
21. That the predecessors, executants, successors, assignees
and agents of both the parties shall remain bound by the terms
and conditions of the present settlement-deed.

--- Page 440 ---
8
In witness whereof, this settlement-deed is entered into
between the parties out of their own free will and without any
undue influence, coercion or threat of any kind from any
quarter and without any misrepresentation by either of the
parties and after admitting contents of instant deed, the
parties to the deed have put the respective signatures.
FIRST PARTY SECOND PARTY
1. SUSHAM SINGLA 1. ADITYA JAIN
2. SAGAR SINGLA 2. AAKRITI JAIN
3. AKASH SINGLA
________________ _______________
(Witness No.1) (Witness No.2)
Manveen Shahi Sheetan Bansal,

Mobile No.9888870027 Mobile No.9815739218
(ANUPINDER SINGH GREWAL)
  MEDIATOR”
4. In accordance with the terms of the settlement, the petitioner
has to pay a total sum of Rs.6,00,00,000/- (Rupees Six Crore only)
to the complainant. Rs.2,00,00,000/- (Rupees Two Crore only) has
already been paid by the petitioner to the complainant. The balance
amount has to be paid in accordance with the schedule provided in
the settlement agreement.
5. Parties are directed to abide by all the terms and conditions
of the settlement. 
6. The original settlement agreement is ordered to be taken on
record and the same shall be kept with the record of this case.
7. In view of the aforesaid, the order passed by this Court dated
20.04.2026 is made absolute.

--- Page 444 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.                 OF 2026
(ARISING OUT OF SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026) 
MAHADEV AIAH                                                            …....APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
THE STATE OF KARNATAKA                          …RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER 
1. Heard. Leave granted. 
2. The  present  appeal  arises  out  of  the  Judgment  and  Order  dated
28.04.2025 passed by the High Court of Karnataka in Criminal Appeal
No.844 of 2013 affirming the judgment of conviction and sentence dated
24.07.2013 passed by the I Additional Sessions Judge, Mandya in S.C.
No.183/2012, whereunder the appellant was convicted for the offences
punishable under Sections 324 and 326 of the Indian Penal Code (for
short ‘IPC’) and sentenced to undergo imprisonment for a period of two
years with fine of Rs.2,000/- for the offence under Section 326 IPC and
fine of Rs.1,000/- with default sentence for the offence under Section 324
IPC. 
1                                                                        SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026

--- Page 445 ---
3. Briefly stated, the prosecution case was that the appellant/Mahadevaiah
being the Husband of the complainant/informant Sannathayamma, who is
his  wife,  assaulted  her  with  a  stick  on  06.07.2011  causing  injuries
including fracture to her hand. The Trial Court, upon appreciation of the
evidence of the injured witness, eyewitnesses and the medical evidence
including the wound certificate and X-ray report, came to the conclusion
that the prosecution had proved the offences under Sections 324 and 326
IPC against the appellant and acquitted the other accused persons while
convicting the present appellant. 
4. The High Court, while dismissing the appeal, held that the testimony of
the injured witness stood corroborated by the evidence of her daughter
and  the  medical  evidence.  The  High  Court  further  observed  that  the
injuries,  particularly  the  fracture,  clearly  attracted  the  offence  under
Section 326 IPC and that the sentence imposed by the Trial Court was
proportionate to the nature and gravity of the injuries sustained by the
complainant. 
5. During the pendency of the present proceedings, I.A. No.119646 of 2026
has been filed jointly by the appellant and respondent No.2/complainant
seeking permission to compound the offences. In the application, it has
been stated that with the intervention of elders, family members and well-
wishers, the disputes between the parties, who are husband and wife, have
been amicably resolved and they are now residing together peacefully. It
2                                                                        SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026

--- Page 446 ---
has been further stated that the complainant does not wish to pursue the
criminal proceedings any further and has no objection if the conviction
and sentence of the appellant are set aside. 
6. Learned counsel appearing for the parties submit that the compromise is
genuine, voluntary and without any coercion. The parties are personally
present before the Court and affirm the settlement arrived at between
them. 
7. This Court has, in a catena of decisions, permitted compounding and
accepted  post-conviction  settlements  in  appropriate  cases  in  order  to
secure complete justice between the parties. In Manoj & Anr. v. State of
Madhya Pradesh1, this Court accepted the compromise and acquitted the
appellants for the offence under Section 324 IPC. Similar views were
taken in Md. Abdul Sufan Laskar v. State of Assam 2, Mathura Singh v.
State of U.P .3  wherein this Court accepted the compromise and set aside
the conviction. In Hirabhai Jhaverbhai v. State of Gujarat 4, this Court
held that once the offence is compounded under Section 320 Cr.P.C., the
same shall have the effect of acquittal. In Padmalayan v. Sarasan 5, this
Court  permitted  post-conviction  compromise  for  the  offence  under
Section 324 IPC. In  Mohd. Rafi v. State of U.P . 6, this Court granted
permission to compound the offences under Sections 323 and 325 IPC in
1Criminal Appeal No.1530 of 2008.
2(2008) 9 SCC 333
3(2009) 13 SCC 420.
4(2010) 6 SCC 688.
5(2014) 13 SCC 798.
6Criminal Appeal No. 383 of 1998.
3                                                                        SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026

--- Page 447 ---
order to maintain cordial relations between the parties and acquitted the
appellant. 
8. This Court has also consistently held that in exercise of powers under
Article 142 of the Constitution of India, this Court can give quietus to
criminal  proceedings where  the  dispute  is personal  in  nature  and  the
parties have genuinely settled their disputes. In the light of the amicable
settlement and the complainant’s unequivocal consent, as evidenced by
the Interlocutory Application, this Court finds it appropriate to allow the
present  application  and  permit  the  compounding  of  offences.  While
noting the offense under Section 326 IPC is non-compoundable under the
provisions  of  the  Criminal  Procedure  Code,  1973,  in  the  exceptional
circumstances  of  this  case  prevailing  namely  appellant  and  the
complainant being husband and wife, including the voluntary settlement
between the parties, which would warrant the exercise of this Court’s
inherent powers to give effect to the compromise. 
9. Having regard to the nature of the dispute, the relationship between the
parties, the compromise arrived at between them, and in order to secure
complete  justice,  we  are  of  the  considered  view  that  the  application
seeking permission to compound deserves to be allowed, though learned
Counsel  appearing  for  the  Respondent  No.  1/State  has  vehemently
opposed the same.
10.Respondent-complainant, who is present virtually and she is identified by
Shri Shashi Kumar, learned Advocate (Roll No. KAR2857/99). She has
4                                                                        SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026

--- Page 448 ---
stated in categorical terms that she has entered into the settlement without
any force, threat or coercion. 
11.In view of the same, we see no impediment to accept the same settlement.
Accordingly  I.A.  No.119646/2026  is  partly  allowed.  The  parties  are
permitted to compound the offences. Consequently the judgement and
order  dated  28.04.2025  passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Karnataka  in
Criminal  Appeal  No.844/2013  and  the  judgement  of  conviction  and
sentence dated 24.07.2013 passed by the First Additional and Sessions
Judge, Mandya in SC No.183/2013 is modified and it is held that the
period  of  sentence  already  undergone  would  stand  substituted  to  the
sentence imposed by the Sessions Court. The appellant is ordered to be
released forthwith and a mail be sent to the Registry of this Court within
three days from today by the jail superintendent, Mandya.   
12.The appellant is acquitted of all the charges. Bail bonds, if any, shall
stand discharged. 
5                                                                        SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026

--- Page 449 ---
13. The appeal is accordingly allowed.
14.   Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
....................................J.
(ARA VIND KUMAR)
..............................................J.
(PRASANNA B. V ARALE)
New Delhi;
May 27, 2026
6                                                                        SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026

--- Page 450 ---

--- Page 460 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
 CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION   
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 2108 OF 2025
SWAPANA S. JERPOTH                                 Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
RAJESHWAR KASHALA                                  Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. The petitioner-wife has filed the transfer petition under
Section 25 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 before this
Court seeking transfer of case bearing F.C.O.P No. 204 of 2025
titled as “ Shri Rajeshwar Kashala  vs Smt. Swapana S. Jerpoth ”
filed under Section 13(1)(ia) of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955
pending before the Court of Principal Judge, Family Court,
Secunderabad to the Court Family Court, Nagpur, Maharashtra.
2. During the pendency of aforesaid transfer petition, vide
order dated 29.10.2025 the parties were referred to the Supreme
Court Mediation Centre. By virtue of the mediation, the parties
have arrived at a settlement and they have decided to part ways
on certain terms and conditions which are delineated in the
settlement agreement dated 19.01.2026 which has been signed by
1

--- Page 461 ---
both the parties, counsel for the parties and the Mediator, copy
of which has been filed by the Supreme Court Mediation Centre. 
3. Learned counsel for the respondent has also filed an
application being IA No. 149170 of 2026 with the following
prayers:
“a)  Allow  the  present  Application  filed  under
Article 142(1) of the Constitution of India by
granting divorce between the parties on the ground
of irretrievable break down of marriage;
b)  Quash  the  Domestic  Violence  Application
No. CRI.MA/2314/2025 filed JMFC (Special Court for
DV)  Nagpur,  Court  Maharashtra  and  Maintenance
Petition bearing No. E/250/2025 before Principal
Judge, Family Court, Nagpur, Maharashtra;
c) The FIR bearing No.421/2025 at Sadar Police
Station, Nagpur, Maharashtra and the proceedings
emanating therefrom be quashed and set aside in
consonance with the terms of the settlement deed
dated 19.01.2026;
d) Pass such other or further order (s) as deem fit
and proper in view of the facts and circumstances of
the present case."
4. We have taken the Interlocutory Application being IA No.
149170 of 2026 and Settlement Agreement dated 19.01.2026 on
record.

7. Having considered the matter in detail and having perused
the Settlement Agreement, we are of the view that this is a fit
case for exercising power under Article 142 of the Constitution
of  India  to  dissolve  the  marriage  between  the  parties.
Consequently,  the  marriage  between  the  parties  which  was
solemnized on 04.04.2024  at Lamba Celebrations, Kamptee Road,
Nagpur, Maharashtra stands dissolved by a decree of divorce by
mutual consent.

recording the consent terms.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.                 OF 2026
(ARISING OUT OF SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026) 
MAHADEV AIAH                                                            …....APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
THE STATE OF KARNATAKA                          …RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER 
1. Heard. Leave granted. 
2. The  present  appeal  arises  out  of  the  Judgment  and  Order  dated
28.04.2025 passed by the High Court of Karnataka in Criminal Appeal
No.844 of 2013 affirming the judgment of conviction and sentence dated
24.07.2013 passed by the I Additional Sessions Judge, Mandya in S.C.
No.183/2012, whereunder the appellant was convicted for the offences
punishable under Sections 324 and 326 of the Indian Penal Code (for
short ‘IPC’) and sentenced to undergo imprisonment for a period of two
years with fine of Rs.2,000/- for the offence under Section 326 IPC and
fine of Rs.1,000/- with default sentence for the offence under Section 324
IPC. 
1                                                                        SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026

--- Page 469 ---
3. Briefly stated, the prosecution case was that the appellant/Mahadevaiah
being the Husband of the complainant/informant Sannathayamma, who is
his  wife,  assaulted  her  with  a  stick  on  06.07.2011  causing  injuries
including fracture to her hand. The Trial Court, upon appreciation of the
evidence of the injured witness, eyewitnesses and the medical evidence
including the wound certificate and X-ray report, came to the conclusion
that the prosecution had proved the offences under Sections 324 and 326
IPC against the appellant and acquitted the other accused persons while
convicting the present appellant. 
4. The High Court, while dismissing the appeal, held that the testimony of
the injured witness stood corroborated by the evidence of her daughter
and  the  medical  evidence.  The  High  Court  further  observed  that  the
injuries,  particularly  the  fracture,  clearly  attracted  the  offence  under
Section 326 IPC and that the sentence imposed by the Trial Court was
proportionate to the nature and gravity of the injuries sustained by the
complainant. 
5. During the pendency of the present proceedings, I.A. No.119646 of 2026
has been filed jointly by the appellant and respondent No.2/complainant
seeking permission to compound the offences. In the application, it has
been stated that with the intervention of elders, family members and well-
wishers, the disputes between the parties, who are husband and wife, have
been amicably resolved and they are now residing together peacefully. It
2                                                                        SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026

--- Page 470 ---
has been further stated that the complainant does not wish to pursue the
criminal proceedings any further and has no objection if the conviction
and sentence of the appellant are set aside. 
6. Learned counsel appearing for the parties submit that the compromise is
genuine, voluntary and without any coercion. The parties are personally
present before the Court and affirm the settlement arrived at between
them. 
7. This Court has, in a catena of decisions, permitted compounding and
accepted  post-conviction  settlements  in  appropriate  cases  in  order  to
secure complete justice between the parties. In Manoj & Anr. v. State of
Madhya Pradesh1, this Court accepted the compromise and acquitted the
appellants for the offence under Section 324 IPC. Similar views were
taken in Md. Abdul Sufan Laskar v. State of Assam 2, Mathura Singh v.
State of U.P .3  wherein this Court accepted the compromise and set aside
the conviction. In Hirabhai Jhaverbhai v. State of Gujarat 4, this Court
held that once the offence is compounded under Section 320 Cr.P.C., the
same shall have the effect of acquittal. In Padmalayan v. Sarasan 5, this
Court  permitted  post-conviction  compromise  for  the  offence  under
Section 324 IPC. In  Mohd. Rafi v. State of U.P . 6, this Court granted
permission to compound the offences under Sections 323 and 325 IPC in
1Criminal Appeal No.1530 of 2008.
2(2008) 9 SCC 333
3(2009) 13 SCC 420.
4(2010) 6 SCC 688.
5(2014) 13 SCC 798.
6Criminal Appeal No. 383 of 1998.
3                                                                        SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026

--- Page 471 ---
order to maintain cordial relations between the parties and acquitted the
appellant. 
8. This Court has also consistently held that in exercise of powers under
Article 142 of the Constitution of India, this Court can give quietus to
criminal  proceedings where  the  dispute  is personal  in  nature  and  the
parties have genuinely settled their disputes. In the light of the amicable
settlement and the complainant’s unequivocal consent, as evidenced by
the Interlocutory Application, this Court finds it appropriate to allow the
present  application  and  permit  the  compounding  of  offences.  While
noting the offense under Section 326 IPC is non-compoundable under the
provisions  of  the  Criminal  Procedure  Code,  1973,  in  the  exceptional
circumstances  of  this  case  prevailing  namely  appellant  and  the
complainant being husband and wife, including the voluntary settlement
between the parties, which would warrant the exercise of this Court’s
inherent powers to give effect to the compromise. 
9. Having regard to the nature of the dispute, the relationship between the
parties, the compromise arrived at between them, and in order to secure
complete  justice,  we  are  of  the  considered  view  that  the  application
seeking permission to compound deserves to be allowed, though learned
Counsel  appearing  for  the  Respondent  No.  1/State  has  vehemently
opposed the same.
10.Respondent-complainant, who is present virtually and she is identified by
Shri Shashi Kumar, learned Advocate (Roll No. KAR2857/99). She has
4                                                                        SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026

--- Page 472 ---
stated in categorical terms that she has entered into the settlement without
any force, threat or coercion. 
11.In view of the same, we see no impediment to accept the same settlement.
Accordingly  I.A.  No.119646/2026  is  partly  allowed.  The  parties  are
permitted to compound the offences. Consequently the judgement and
order  dated  28.04.2025  passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Karnataka  in
Criminal  Appeal  No.844/2013  and  the  judgement  of  conviction  and
sentence dated 24.07.2013 passed by the First Additional and Sessions
Judge, Mandya in SC No.183/2013 is modified and it is held that the
period  of  sentence  already  undergone  would  stand  substituted  to  the
sentence imposed by the Sessions Court. The appellant is ordered to be
released forthwith and a mail be sent to the Registry of this Court within
three days from today by the jail superintendent, Mandya.   
12.The appellant is acquitted of all the charges. Bail bonds, if any, shall
stand discharged. 
5                                                                        SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026

--- Page 473 ---
13. The appeal is accordingly allowed.
14.   Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
....................................J.
(ARA VIND KUMAR)
..............................................J.
(PRASANNA B. V ARALE)
New Delhi;
May 27, 2026
6                                                                        SLP (Crl.) NO. 8566/2026

--- Page 474 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

O R D E R 
1. The petitioner-wife has filed the transfer petition under
Section 25 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 before this
Court seeking transfer of case bearing F.C.O.P No. 204 of 2025
titled as “ Shri Rajeshwar Kashala  vs Smt. Swapana S. Jerpoth ”
filed under Section 13(1)(ia) of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955
pending before the Court of Principal Judge, Family Court,
Secunderabad to the Court Family Court, Nagpur, Maharashtra.
2. During the pendency of aforesaid transfer petition, vide
order dated 29.10.2025 the parties were referred to the Supreme
Court Mediation Centre. By virtue of the mediation, the parties
have arrived at a settlement and they have decided to part ways
on certain terms and conditions which are delineated in the
settlement agreement dated 19.01.2026 which has been signed by
1

--- Page 477 ---
both the parties, counsel for the parties and the Mediator, copy
of which has been filed by the Supreme Court Mediation Centre.

application being IA No. 149170 of 2026 with the following
prayers:
“a)  Allow  the  present  Application  filed  under
Article 142(1) of the Constitution of India by
granting divorce between the parties on the ground
of irretrievable break down of marriage;
b)  Quash  the  Domestic  Violence  Application
No. CRI.MA/2314/2025 filed JMFC (Special Court for
DV)  Nagpur,  Court  Maharashtra  and  Maintenance
Petition bearing No. E/250/2025 before Principal
Judge, Family Court, Nagpur, Maharashtra;
c) The FIR bearing No.421/2025 at Sadar Police
Station, Nagpur, Maharashtra and the proceedings
emanating therefrom be quashed and set aside in
consonance with the terms of the settlement deed
dated 19.01.2026;
d) Pass such other or further order (s) as deem fit
and proper in view of the facts and circumstances of
the present case."

149170 of 2026 and Settlement Agreement dated 19.01.2026 on
record.

take due steps to withdraw Maintenance Petition
bearing No. E/250/2025 Under Section 144 BNSS, 2023
before  Hon'ble  Principal  Judge,  Family  Court,
Nagpur, Maharashtra on or before 2nd  February
2026.
IX The Petitioner/wife further undertakes to co-
operate in quashing of the FIR filed bearing No.
421/2025  at  Sadar  Police  Station  Nagpur,
Maharashtra against the Respondent and / or family
4

--- Page 480 ---
members before the Hon'ble High Court in Quashing
Petitions  bearing  Nos.  No.  APL/1508/2025  and
APL/1689/2025 after the payment of last instalment
in case the said FIR and subsequent proceedings are
pending.
AND/OR
D.  In  case  this  Hon'ble  Court  does  not  grant
permission under Article 142 of Constitution of
India,  both parties  have agreed  to file  first
motion before the Competent Court at Nagpur at
their own cost within 15 days from the rejection of
Joint Application Under 142 of the Constitution of
India by this Hon'ble Court.
E. The Subsequent proceedings including moving of
second motion uptil grant of decree of divorce
shall be as per the procedure laid down by law and
on the basis of the present settlement agreement.
6. The parties undertake not to interfere in each
others life in future and not to malign each other
and/or their family members via social Media and or
internet. Any prior content posted earlier shall be
removed by both the parties.
7. That the Petitioner and the Respondent have
agreed that none of them will initiate any other
legal action or complaint against each other or
against the family members of each other in respect
of their matrimonial dispute in future.
8. That subject to the aforesaid terms, the parties
have resolved all the disputes amicably in relation
to the marriage and have been left with no claims
against  each  other  or  their  respective  family
members
9.  That by  signing this  Agreement the  parties
hereto solemnly state and affirm that they have no
further  claims  or  demands  against  each  other
including maintenance, or any movable or immovable
property and all the disputes and differences have
been amicably settled by the parties hereto through
the process of mediation.
10. The parties undertake to abide by the terms and
conditions  set  out  in  the  above  mentioned
Settlement Agreement, which have been arrived with
free will of the parties without any coercion,
duress or collusion and parties undertake not to
raise any dispute whatsoever henceforth.
5

--- Page 481 ---
11. The contents of this settlement - agreement
have been explained to all The parties through
their respective counsels and they have understood
the same."
6. The parties namely petitioner-wife is present virtually and
the respondent-husband is present physically. Learned counsel
for the respondent-husband has handed over a Demand Draft of Rs.
9,51,000/- (balance amount) to the counsel representing the
petitioner-wife towards full and final satisfaction of all her
claims. Learned counsel for the respondent-husband has also
indicated that the almirah and cot will be dispatched to the
address given in the settlement agreement.  
7. Having considered the matter in detail and having perused
the Settlement Agreement, we are of the view that this is a fit
case for exercising power under Article 142 of the Constitution
of  India  to  dissolve  the  marriage  between  the  parties.
Consequently,  the  marriage  between  the  parties  which  was
solemnized on 04.04.2024  at Lamba Celebrations, Kamptee Road,
Nagpur, Maharashtra stands dissolved by a decree of divorce by
mutual consent. 
8. In view of the above, we direct that;
A) Domestic Violence Application No.  CRI.MA/2314/2025  pending
before the JMFC (Special Court for DV) Nagpur Court Maharashtra
and Maintenance Petition bearing No. E/250/2025 pending before
the Principal Judge, Family Court, Nagpur, Maharashtra stand
quashed;
B) The FIR bearing No.421/2025 registered at Sadar Police
Station,  Nagpur,  Maharashtra  and  the  proceedings  emanating
therefrom  stand quashed/disposed  of  in terms  of our  order
recording the consent terms.
6

--- Page 482 ---
9. Copy of this order shall be sent to the respective Courts
as mentioned in paragraph nos. 8(A) to (B) for follow up action.
10. In view of the above, Transfer Petition and Interlocutory
Application being IA No. 149170 of 2026 are disposed of. The
Registry will draw a decree in the aforesaid terms.
11. It is further directed that the parties shall abide by the
terms of this settlement implicitly. 
12. Pending applications(s), if any, are disposed of.

“105.  It is in the backdrop of the aforesaid
constitutional  principles,  coupled  with  the
peculiar mitigating circumstances noticed in the
present case, that we deem it appropriate to
facilitate the right of A1 and A2 to seek pardon
by permitting them to file appropriate petitions
before His Excellency, the Hon’ble Governor of
Tamil  Nadu.   We  would  only  request  the
constitutional authority to consider the same,
which we hope and trust shall be done by taking
note  of  the  relevant  circumstances  mentioned
above. 
106. Accordingly, we grant eight weeks’ time from
the date of this judgment to A1 and A2 to file
appropriate petitions seeking invocation of the
power  of  pardon  under  Article  161  of  the
Constitution of India. Till such petitions are
duly considered and decided, A1 and A2 shall not
be arrested and the sentence imposed upon them
shall remain suspended.  
107.  Save  and  except  A1  and  A2,  all  the
respondents are directed to surrender before the
Trial Court within two weeks for serving the
sentences. Trial Court shall be at liberty to

compliance.”
The appeals are allowed in terms of the signed
reportable judgment.
