IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 4580 of 2026)
RAJ PRATAP @ SONU … APPELLANT
VERSUS
STATE OF U.P. … RESPONDENT
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 6625 of 2026)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. After  hearing  learned  counsel  for  the
parties, we find that the appellants have already
served more than half of the sentence as awarded
for the purpose of charge under Section 304 first
part read with Sections 34, 504 and 506(2) of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860 and Section 25 of the Arms
Act.  Considering the aforesaid, subject to final
outcome of the criminal appeal, without expressing
any opinion on the merits of the case,  we deem it
appropriate  to  suspend  the  substantive  jail
sentence of the appellants.
1SLP (Crl) No.4580/2026

--- Page 493 ---
3. Accordingly, we direct that substantive jail
sentence of the appellants shall remain suspended
and the appellants be released on bail on such
terms and conditions as may be deemed fit and
imposed by the trial court.
4. We clarify and direct that after the release,
in case the appellants are found indulged in any
criminal case, the State Government would be at
liberty to take recourse as permissible seeking
cancellation of suspension of sentence.
5. Accordingly, the appeals stand disposed of.
Pending applications, if any, shall also stand
disposed of.

ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
7

--- Page 506 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO(S).               OF 2026  
(Arising out of SLP(C)  No(s).  19620/2024)
PARMESHWAR SINGH                              APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF JHARKHAND & ORS.                 RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant was allotted 40 decimals of land in Bokaro
Industrial Area by the Bokaro Industrial Area Development
Authority, Jharkhand (for short, ‘BIADA’) by an allotment
letter dated 16.02.1989. Thereafter, a lease deed was executed
and registered on 08.09.1989.  In the meantime, the appellant
was to ensure the payment of rent.  After nearly 10 years, the
respondent-authority  sent  a  letter  dated  30.09.1999  for
cancellation of the allotment  inter-alia alleging that the
appellant has violated the conditions mentioned in the lease
deed.  Challenging  the  same,  the  appellant  filed  a  writ
petition, which came to be dismissed for non-prosecution.

--- Page 507 ---
2
Thereafter, the appellant filed an appeal before the Competent
Authority, though belatedly. During the pendency of the appeal,
he once again approached the High Court, challenging a re-
allotment letter issued in favour of a third person. In the
said matter, the High Court directed the Competent Authority to
dispose of the aforementioned appeal. The Competent Authority
has, in turn, dismissed the appeal. In the next round of
litigation, the appellant filed a writ petition challenging the
order passed by the Competent Authority, dated 05.05.2022.  The
said order was confirmed by the High Court, holding that there
is no material to show that industrial activity was going on in
the leasehold area; hence, the plea of violation of principles
of natural justice is not tenable on law or fact. The finding
of the learned Single Judge in the order dated 27.07.2023 reads
thus:-
“XXXXX
7. From the submissions advanced it is beyond any
dispute  that  the  petitioner  was  a  lessee  of  an
industrial estate allotted by BIADA vide letter issued
in the year 1989. Allotment of such plot in the
industrial area is a special right created under Bihar
Industrial Area Development Act, 1974 for a specific
purpose to encourage and develop industry. The very
object of the Act being development and promotion of
industries,  its  allotment  was  premised  on  the
construction  and  carrying  of  industrial  activity.
There is no material to show that industrial activity

--- Page 508 ---
3
was  going  on  in  the  lease-hold  area  despite  the
allotment of the plot more than three decades ago.
Petitioner preferred the appeal wherein after hearing
the petitioner the appeal was dismissed. This Court
cannot  enter  in  the  question  whether  there  was
industrial activity in the lease hold area or not. The
plea of violation of principles of natural justice is
not tenable on law or fact.
I do not find any merit in the instant petition which
appears  to  be  a  subterfuge  to  prolong  illegal
occupation in the allotted industrial plot.
Writ petition stands dismissed.”
3. An  appeal  preferred  by  the  appellant  against  the
aforementioned order also came to be dismissed on 09.07.2024.
Aggrieved by the same, the present appeal has been preferred. 
4. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that clause xiv
of the lease deed has no relevance to the case of the
appellant. In any case, one has to rely on Section 6(2)(a) of
the Bihar Industrial Area Development Authority Act, 1974 (for
short, ‘1974 Act’). In such view of the matter, the impugned
order requires interference by this Court.
5. Learned counsel for the respondent(s) submits that the
appeal of the appellant was filed belatedly, and clause xiv of
the lease deed itself states that the lease may be terminated
and the lessee evicted if the terms of the deed are violated. 
6. We have heard the learned counsel for the parties. 
7. Clause xiv of the lease deed reads thus:-

--- Page 509 ---
4
“xiv) That the Lessees shall use the land for the
specified purpose within a period of six months from
the date of the lease failing which the lease may be
terminated  and  the  Lessee  evicted  from  the  lands
without notice. In case extension is required it can be
granted within the discretion of the Lessor.”
8. Section 6(2)(a) of the 1974 Act, reads thus:-
“(2)(a) In case necessary effective steps are not
taken  within  the  fixed  period  to  establish  the
industry,  the  authority  shall  in  such  condition,
cancel the allotment of allotted plot/shed and also
forfeit the amount deposited in this connection. The
authority shall, before cancelling the allotment allow
one month time to the allottee to put up his case, the
allottee on being dissatisfied with the order of the
Authority may file an appeal to the State Government
within one month and the State Government shall, after
due consideration dispose it of within two months from
the date of receipt of the appeal.”
9. The act clearly specifies that an aggrieved person is
entitled to be heard.
10. We do not find any conflict between the terms of the
lease as against the provisions of the Act. Clause xiv of the
lease deed allows for termination of the lease and eviction of
the lessee if the land is not used for the specified purpose
within a period of 6 months. If that situation arises, then

--- Page 510 ---
5
there is no need to give any notice.
11. However, it will not apply to a case where the lessee has
used the land within six months but has not been able to
complete the entire activity. In other words, Clause xiv of the
lease deed merely says that initiation of the activity should
be done within six months. Meaning that if the land in question
is laid barren without being utilized, then it would amount to
an automatic termination. There is a certain difference between
termination and cancellation. What has been done in the present
case is the cancellation. Further, the cancellation of the
lease deed has civil consequences for the appellant.
12. The learned counsel for the respondents contended that
the appeal was belatedly filed and hence the plea of the
appellant cannot be considered. But we are dealing with a case
where cancellation has been made without the issuance of
notice, and therefore, the law of limitation will not arise.
13. In such view of the matter, we are inclined to set aside
the order passed by the High Court.
14. Accordingly, the impugned order stands set aside, and as
a consequence, the cancellation of allotment is set aside. 
15. The  matter  stands  remitted  back  to  the  respondent-
authorities for fresh consideration.
16. This  order  shall  act  as  a  show-cause  notice.  The
appellant shall give an explanation to the respondent-authority
within a period of four weeks from the receipt of a copy of
this order. Thereafter, the final order shall be passed by the
respondent(s)-authorities within a period of six weeks.

--- Page 511 ---
6
17. The appeal is disposed of, in the above terms.
18. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
………………………………………………………….J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
 ……………………………………………………...J.
    [NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH]
NEW DELHI;
MAY 27, 2026.

--- Page 512 ---
7
ITEM NO.67               COURT NO.5               SECTION XVII-B
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  19620/2024
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated  09-07-2024 in
LPA No. 461/2023 in WP(C)No.2449/2022 passed by the High Court of
Jharkhand at Ranchi]
PARMESHWAR SINGH                                   Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF JHARKHAND & ORS.                      Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION and I.R. 
 
Date : 27-05-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE M.M. SUNDRESH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Aditya Kumar Choudhary, Adv.
                   Mr. Sandeep Pandey, Adv.

                   Mr. Rajesh Singh Chauhan, AOR

                   Ms. Tulika Mukherjee, AOR

                   Mr. Venkat Narayan, Adv.                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appeal is disposed of in terms of the signed order,
which is placed on the file.
3. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand also stand
disposed of.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (POONAM VAID)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 4580 of 2026)
RAJ PRATAP @ SONU … APPELLANT
VERSUS
STATE OF U.P. … RESPONDENT
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 6625 of 2026)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. After  hearing  learned  counsel  for  the
parties, we find that the appellants have already
served more than half of the sentence as awarded
for the purpose of charge under Section 304 first
part read with Sections 34, 504 and 506(2) of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860 and Section 25 of the Arms
Act.  Considering the aforesaid, subject to final
outcome of the criminal appeal, without expressing
any opinion on the merits of the case,  we deem it
appropriate  to  suspend  the  substantive  jail
sentence of the appellants.
1SLP (Crl) No.4580/2026

--- Page 528 ---
3. Accordingly, we direct that substantive jail
sentence of the appellants shall remain suspended
and the appellants be released on bail on such
terms and conditions as may be deemed fit and
imposed by the trial court.
4. We clarify and direct that after the release,
in case the appellants are found indulged in any
criminal case, the State Government would be at
liberty to take recourse as permissible seeking
cancellation of suspension of sentence.
5. Accordingly, the appeals stand disposed of.
Pending applications, if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
…………………………………………………,J.
[J.K. MAHESHWARI]
………………………………………………………,J.
[ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
New Delhi;
April 15, 2026.

Corrected
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
 
CIVIL APPEAL NO(s). 3675-3676  OF 2012  
 
SMT.MEENAKSHAMMA                                       APPELLANT(S)
                              VERSUS 
C.N. GOPALKRISHNA RAJU & ORS.                        RESPONDENT(S)
 
O R D E R  
 
1. These appeals arise out of the common judgment and order
dated 15.09.2010 passed by the High Court of Karnataka at
Bangalore in R.F.A. No.1587 of 2003 and R.F.A. No.202 of
2004, whereby the High Court partly modified the judgment
and decree dated 11.09.2003 passed by the Trial Court in
O.S. No.3329 of 1994.
2. The suit was instituted by the plaintiff seeking partition
and separate possession of her share in the properties
belonging to the joint family of late Shri S. Narayana Raju,
who died intestate on 15.05.1992. He was survived by his
wife, Smt. Chandramma (Defendant No.1), four sons (Defendant
Nos.2  to  5),  and  three  daughters  (the  plaintiff  and
Defendant Nos.6 and 7).
1

--- Page 535 ---
3. The suit properties were described under five schedules.
Schedule A consisted of three immovable properties situated
in Bangalore. Schedule B consisted of three commercial
properties standing in the name of Defendant No.1, Smt.
Chandramma.  Schedule  C  consisted  of  six  residential
properties standing in the names of Defendant Nos.3 to 5.
Schedule D related to a bank locker and Schedule E consisted
of certain movable properties such as gold, silver and cash.
4. The plaintiff also sought a declaration that the Release
Deed  dated  24.05.1993,  executed  by  her  in  respect  of
Schedule A properties, was null and void on the ground of
fraud, coercion and undue influence.
5. The Trial Court held that the properties comprised in
Schedules A, B and C were joint family properties. It,
however, partly decreed the suit by granting the plaintiff a
share only in Schedules B and C, while declining relief in
respect of Schedules D and E on the ground that the same
were not proved to be in existence.
6. Aggrieved thereby, appeals were preferred before the High
Court. The High Court, while affirming the rejection of the
claim in respect of Schedules D and E, modified the decree
by holding that the plaintiff was not entitled to any share
in Schedule C properties. The High Court further upheld the
validity of the Release Deed dated 24.05.1993 and re-
determined the shares in Schedules A and B in view of the
2

--- Page 536 ---
death  of  Defendant  No.1,  Smt.  Chandramma,  during  the
pendency of the proceedings. 
7. The controversy before us is now substantially confined to
the claim of the plaintiff in respect of the properties
described in Schedule C.
8. Having heard learned counsel for the parties and upon
careful consideration of the material on record, we find
that the properties included in Schedule C were acquired by
Defendant Nos.3 to 5 in their individual capacity. The
evidence on record establishes that these properties were
purchased from their own earnings, including properties
allotted by the Karnataka Housing Board on a lease-cum-sale
basis. No evidence has been led to show the existence of a
joint  family  nucleus  or  that  joint  family  funds  were
utilised for such acquisitions.
9. We also find no error in the conclusion of the High Court
with regard to the validity of the Release Deed dated
24.05.1993. The plaintiff, despite being well-educated, did
not step into the witness box to substantiate her plea of
fraud or coercion, and instead examined her husband as a
power of attorney holder, who admittedly had no personal
knowledge of the execution of the document. The burden of
proof having not been discharged, the finding of the High
Court warrants no interference.
10.  In view of the above, we find no legal infirmity in the
impugned judgment. The High Court has rightly excluded
3

--- Page 537 ---
Schedule C properties from partition and correctly affirmed
the rejection of the claim in respect of Schedules D and E.
11.  Consequently, the appeals are dismissed. The judgment and
decree passed by the High Court of Karnataka are affirmed.
All pending applications, if any, stand disposed of.
 
…………………………………………………………………. J. 
 (SANJAY KAROL) 
…………………………………………………………………. J. 
 (AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH) 
 
New Delhi 
28th January, 2026 
 
 
 
4

--- Page 538 ---
REVISED*
ITEM NO.101               COURT NO.11               SECTION XII-B

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP(C) NO.16718/2025)
SHEETAL                                         APPELLANT
VERSUS
RAKESH MITTAL                                   RESPONDENT
 O R D E R
Leave granted.
2. The  legality  and  correctness  of  the  judgment  dated
24.02.2025, passed by the Division Bench of the High Court of
Punjab  and  Haryana,  is  assailed  in  this  appeal  by  the
appellant-wife.
3. Learned senior counsel appearing for the appellant-wife
and learned counsel for the respondent-husband submitted that
during  the  pendency  of  this  appeal,  the  parties  have
negotiated a settlement. Also, a joint application has been
filed under Article 142 of the Constitution of India under
which, the parties have resolved to part ways on certain
terms and conditions stated in the joint application which
has  also  appended  with  the  Settlement  agreement  dated
08.04.2026 arrived at between the parties before the Supreme
Court Mediation Centre.
1

--- Page 541 ---
4. Learned Senior Counsel for the appellant-wife submitted
that the appellant has accepted the terms and conditions of
the Settlement Agreement. She, therefore, does not press the
appeal on merits however the appeal may be disposed of in
terms of the Settlement Agreement. 
5. In that regard, learned senior counsel appearing for the
appellant-wife and learned counsel for the respondent-husband
submitted that the minor son Jatin alias Jayant would remain
with  the  appellant-mother;  that  a  total  sum  of
Rs.1,00,00,000/-(Rupees One Crore only) is the permanent
alimony that is being paid by the respondent-husband to the
appellant-wife towards the maintenance and living expenses of
the appellant as well as the minor son; that already a sum of
Rs.30,00,000/-(Rupees Thirty Lakhs only) has been received by
the appellant and the balance amount of Rs.70,00,000/-(Rupees
Seventy Lakhs Only) is being paid by the respondent to the
appellant by way of a demand draft today. 
6. Learned  senior  counsel  and  learned  counsel  for  the
respective parties also submitted that following are two
pending cases and in view of the settlement arrived at
between the parties, the said cases may also be quashed:
2

--- Page 542 ---
“(i) Application bearing No.APP/57/2024 pending before
Principal Judge (family court), Gurugram, Haryana u/s
127 of the Cr. P.C.
(ii) Application  bearing  No.APP/103/2025,  under
Section 379/215, BNSS pending before the Principal
Judge, Family Court at Gurugram, Haryana.”
7. The other terms and conditions of the settlement are
also delineated in the Settlement Agreement dated 8.04.2026.
Hence,  the  settlement  agreement  as  well  as  the  joint
application may be taken on record and the appeal may be
disposed  of  in  terms  of  the  said  application  and  the
Settlement Agreement.
8. The parties are also present before this Court. They
have been identified by their respective counsel. 
9. When we queried the parties about their decision to part
ways on the terms and conditions delineated in the settlement
agreement, they stated that they have indeed decided on their
own free volition and without there being any undue influence
from any side to part ways; that they have been living
separately since the year 2010. The appellant wife stated
that she would not press the appeal insofar as the impugned
judgment of dissolution of the marriage granted by the High
Court  is  concerned;  that  she  has  received  a  sum  of
Rs.30,00,000/- plus Rs.70,00,000/- totally Rs.1,00,00,000/-
towards permanent alimony as well as towards the upbringing
3

--- Page 543 ---
of the minor son who resides with her. Learned counsel for
the  respondent  has  handed  over  a  demand  draft  bearing
No.791483  dated  16.05.2026  for  a  sum  of  Rs.70,00,000/-
(Rupees Seventy Lakhs Only) issued by the State Bank of
India, Narnaul Branch, Haryana. The receipt of the said
demand draft is acknowledged by the learned senior counsel
for the appellant as well as by the appellant-wife. The
aforesaid payment is in full and final settlement of all her
claims of the appellant herein.  The right, title and
interest of the minor son in the ancestral or separate
properties of the respondent are left open to be claimed by
him at an appropriate stage and time. In the circumstances,
they stated that this Court may pass orders in the matter.
10. In the above backdrop, we have taken on record the
Settlement Agreement dated 08.04.2026 and also the Joint
Application filed under Article 142 of the Constitution of
India. On perusal of the same, we find that there is no legal
impediment in accepting the terms of the Settlement Agreement
and the prayers made in the Joint Application. Consequently,
by accepting the terms of the settlement, we allow the Joint
Application and direct the parties to abide by the terms of
the Settlement Agreement dated 08.04.2026.
4

--- Page 544 ---
11. The settlement agreement dated 08.04.2026 shall be read as
part and parcel of this order.
12. In view of the above, the impugned judgment of the
Division Bench of the High Court would not call for any
interference.
13. We are informed by learned senior counsel and learned
counsel for the respective parties that the learned Mediator
has  made  great  endeavours  to  bring  about  an  amicable
settlement  between  the  parties.  We  place  on  record  our
appreciation of the efforts of the learned Mediator. 
14. The appeal is disposed of in the aforesaid terms.
15. The Registry to draw-up a decree in the aforesaid terms.
16. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
…………………………………………….J.
                                           (B.V. NAGARATHNA)  
 
………………………………………………J.
                                            (UJJAL BHUYAN)    
NEW DELHI; 
May 20, 2026

--- Page 546 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.          /2026
[@ SLP (CRL.) NO.8556/2026]
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.          /2026
[@ SLP (CRL.) NO.8461/2026]
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.          /2026
[@ SLP (CRL.) NO.8368/2026]
NEHRU IRULANDI                                APPELLANT(s)
                          VERSUS
STATE OF TAMIL NADU & ORS.                   RESPONDENT(s)
O R D E R
1. Exemption applications are allowed.
2. Leave granted.
3. These appeals arise from the common order passed by
the  High  Court  dated  24.02.2026  in  Crl.  O.P.  (MD)
No.2054/2026  by  which  the  High  Court  while  granting
anticipatory bail to the appellants-herein in connection
with Crime No.6/2026, registered with Perunazhi Police
Station,  Madurai,  for  the  offence  punishable  under
Sections 189(2), 329(3), 324(4), 115(2), 351(2) of the
Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 respectively (for short,
“the BNS, 2023”) imposed a condition that the appellants
and other co-accused shall not interfere in the disputed

--- Page 547 ---
2
property until the conclusion of the Civil Suit instituted
by the complainant.
4. While granting anticipatory bail as prayed for, the
High Court imposed the following conditions:-
“[a] the petitioners and the sureties shall affix
their photographs and left thumb impression in
the surety bond and the Magistrate may obtain a
copy of their Aadhaar card or bank pass book to
ensure their identity;
[b] the petitioners 1 and 2 shall report before
the respondent police daily at 10.30 a.m., for a
period of two weeks and thereafter, as and when
required  for  interrogation;  Further,  the
petitioners 3 and 4 shall report before the
respondent  police  as  and  when  required  for
interrogation. The petitioners are directed not
to interfere in the disputed property until the
suit is over.
[c] the petitioners shall not tamper with the
evidence or witness either during investigation
or trial.
[d] the petitioners shall not abscond either
during investigation or trial.
[e] On breach of any of the aforesaid conditions,
the learned Magistrate/Trial Court is entitled to
take appropriate action against the petitioners
in accordance with law as if the conditions have
been imposed and the petitioners are released on
bail  by  the  learned  Magistrate/Trial  Court
himself as laid down by the Hon’ble Supreme Court
in P.K.Shaji vs. State of Kerala [(2005)AIR SCW
5560].
[f] If the accused thereafter absconds, a fresh
FIR can be registered under Section 269 of BNS.”

--- Page 548 ---
3
5. We take notice of the fact that the appellants are
the  defendants  in  the  Civil  Suit  instituted  by  the
complainant.  The  Civil  Suit  has  been  instituted  for
permanent injunction.
6. Prima-facie,  it  appears  that  the  case  of  the
appellant, namely, Nehru Irulandi as one of the defendants
is that he has purchased the suit property.
7. Be that as it may, the Suit is of the year 2019. The
plaintiff has not prayed for any injunction pending the
Suit.
8. We are informed that an application was preferred
praying for injunction under Order 39 Rules 1 and 2 of the
CPC respectively, however, the same has not been taken up
for hearing.
9. We are of the view that while granting anticipatory
bail, High Court should not have imposed a condition which
tantamount to granting of some relief in the Suit itself,
which otherwise the Civil Court has not granted.
10. In view of the aforesaid, the condition no.9(b) is
set-aside to the extent it has directed not to interfere
in the disputed property until the Suit is over. Rest of
the conditions shall be abided by the appellants.
11. If the complainant as plaintiff has any apprehension
as regards the breach of peace, interference with the suit
property, etc., he may pray for appropriate relief before
the Civil Court or any appropriate forum in accordance
with law.

--- Page 549 ---
4
12. In view of the aforesaid, the appeals are allowed in
part.
13. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
……………………………………………J.
[J.B. PARDIWALA]
……………………………………………J.
[K.V. VISWANATHAN]
NEW DELHI;
27th MAY, 2026.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Learned Counsel for the State of Karnataka has pointed out
that inadvertently in the order dated 12.03.2026, it could not be
mentioned that the State of Karnataka has also complied with the
direction of appointment of regular Director General of Police
(DGP) on recommendations of the UPSC.
2. The submission is taken on record.
I.A. No.159602/2026, along with I.A. Nos.84684 and 85514/2026
3. IA No.159602/2026 is taken on board.
4. Learned counsel for the applicant(s) seeks and is permitted to
withdraw IA No. 85514/2026 & IA No. 84684/2026. The same are,
accordingly, dismissed as withdrawn.
5. I.A. No.159602/2026, thus, stands allowed.

1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.2984/2026
(@Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No.6811/2026)
PUSHPENDRA                                         Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P. & ORS.                               Respondent(s)
 O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. This appeal arises from the Order passed by the High Court of
Judicature at Allahabad dated 6-1-2026 in Criminal Miscellaneous
Writ Petition No.25468/2025, by which the application preferred by
the Respondent Nos.3 and 4 respectively before us – original
accused persons praying for quashing of the First Information
Report lodged by the appellant before us, came to be allowed and
thereby the FIR was ordered to be quashed. 
3. We take notice of the fact that the appellant lodged the First
Information Report bearing No. 0512 of 2025 with Police Station-
Soro Thana, District-Kasganj, UP for the offence punishable under
Sections 109 (1), 127 (2), 191 (2), 308 (5), 351 (3) and 352 of the
Bhartiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (for short, “BNS, 2023) respectively. 
4. The First Information Report reads thus:-
“12. First Information Contents:
Respectfully, it is submitted that Ayendra son of Natthu
Singh, Chandrakesh son of Ramrahees, and Vrijesh son of Nem
Singh from my village were forcibly dumping soil on my land. I
stopped them from dumping the soil. Taking offense to this, on
06.09.2025,  Ayendra,  Chandrakesh,  Vrijesh,  residents  of
Village Kumaria, along with Kuldeep Pandey and Aashish Pandey,

--- Page 559 ---
2
residents of Village Garhi Harnader, Police Station Dolna,
came to my house and said, "Either let us dump the soil, or
give us 1 lakh rupees." When I refused to give the money, all
the above-mentioned people forcibly made me sit in their
vehicle and took me outside the village, where they held me
hostage in the vehicle. Out of fear, I gave them 20,000/- that
I had on me, and they told me to arrange for the remaining
80,000/-. When I refused to give any more money, Kuldeep₹
Pandey and Aashish Pandey said to Aryendra, "Kill him, shoot
the scoundrel." Then Aryendra, with the intention of killing
me, fired at me from the pistol he had in his hand, and I had
a narrow escape. Hearing the sound of the gunshot, my brother
Avanish son of Ganga Singh, Pappu son of Ghanendra, and many
other people ran towards the spot. Seeing them approach, (the
accused) threatened to kill me, and then all of them fled with
the vehicle.
The above-mentioned people are of a criminal nature. They form
a group called the 'Kisan Union Swaraj Gaddi' and extort money
by threatening people. Therefore, I humbly request that you
register my report and take legal action against the above-
mentioned people. I shall be highly obliged. Date: 30.09.2025
SD Pushpendra Applicant Pushpendra Son of Netrapal Singh
Resident of Village Kumaraua, P.S. Soron, Kasganj, Mobile No.
9720838592 Note: I Head Constable 515 Shyamveer Singh, certify
that the copy of the application has been transcribed by me,
with word to word that may contains technical errors having
been copied exactly.”
5. The accused persons went before the High Court and prayed that
the FIR, referred to above, be quashed as the same was lodged after
a period of 24 days from the date of the incident.
6. The High Court accepted the contention canvassed on behalf of
the accused persons as regards the delay in lodging the FIR and
thought fit to quash the FIR.
7. In such circumstances, referred to above, the appellant –
original complainant is here before us with the present appeal.
8. We heard Mr. Mohit Saroha, the learned counsel appearing for
the appellant – complainant, Ms. Ruchira Goel, the learned counsel
appearing for the State of U.P. and Mr. Akshay Dev, the learned
counsel appearing for the Respondent Nos.3 and 4 respectively –
original accused persons.

--- Page 560 ---
3
9. Having regard to the nature of allegations levelled in the
FIR, the High Court should not have quashed the FIR merely on the
ground that there was a delay of 24 days in lodging the same. There
could be many good reasons for the First Informant not to immedi -
ately reach the Police Station and lodge the FIR.  However, delay
with other attending circumstances emerging from the record of the
case rendering the entire case put up by the prosecution inherently
improbable, may at times become a good ground to quash the FIR and
consequential proceedings.
10. As pointed out by the learned counsel appearing for the appel -
lant, the accused persons have criminal antecedents. 
11. Delay is one of the factors which the Trial Court may take
into consideration while appreciating the overall evidence that may
come on record in the course of the trial. Delay by itself without
anything more cannot be a ground to quash the FIR. 
12. The learned counsel appearing for the Respondent Nos.3 and 4
respectively vehemently submitted that the FIR is politically
motivated. This contention also cannot be a ground to quash the FIR
at the nascent stage. At the time of considering the case for
quashing of the FIR, the High Court is expected to look into the
nature of the crime, the nature of allegations levelled etc. In
other words, what is important for the High Court is to see whether
the FIR discloses commission of any cognizable offence or not. 
13. We take notice of the fact that the High Court has not said a
word or discussed anything insofar as the nature of the allegations
levelled in the FIR is concerned.  It is expected of the High Court
to give a fair idea in its order as regards the contents of the FIR

--- Page 561 ---
4
and what type of allegations have been levelled in the FIR. The en -
tire FIR may not be reproduced but at least when the matter travels
upto the highest Court, then if something is observed in the im -
pugned Order as regards the contents of the FIR, nature of the al -
legations, that would be helpful to understand whether there has
been proper application of mind at the end of the High Court.
14. In the overall view of the matter, we are convinced that the
impugned Order passed by the High Court is not sustainable in law.
15. In the result, this Appeal succeeds and is hereby allowed. 
16. The impugned Order passed by the High Court is quashed and the
FIR No.0512/2025 is restored. The police shall now proceed further
in accordance with law.
17. Pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.
…………………………………………J     
(J.B. PARDIWALA)
…………………………………………J     
(S.V.N. BHATTI)
NEW DELHI
29TH MAY, 2026.

--- Page 564 ---
 
1 
 
   IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
 
CIVIL APPEAL NO(s).        OF 2026  
(Arising out of SLP (C) No(s).17799-802 of 2025) 
 
 
 
  LIFE INSURANCE CORPORATION OF INDIA & ORS.       APPELLANT(s) 
 
 
                               VERSUS 
 
 
  B.D.G. PRASAD ALIAS GANESH & ORS.                RESPONDENT(s) 
 
 
 
 O R D E R 
 
 Leave granted. 
2. These appeals arise out of the impugned common judgment and 
order dated 10.02.2025 passed by the High Court for the State of 
Telangana at Hyderabad in Writ Appeal (W.A.) Nos. 1426 & 1432 of 
2024 and W.A. Nos. 126 & 159 of 2025. 
3. Mindful of the fact that the livelihoods and future of the 
employees (Respondents herein) are at stake, we proceed to 
adjudicate the present matter. The challenge of securing 
employment for youth in the current landscape is significant, and 
should these appeals be allowed, the Respondents—who are temporary 
Class-IV employees would face immediate displacement  from their 
jobs. Consequently, the resolution of th ese appeals directly 

--- Page 565 ---
 
2 
 
determine the future of these individuals. 
4. The central question for our consideration is whether, even 
if we are inclined to extend equitable relief, with our open hearts 
and extended hands, such a gesture can be allowed to result in 
regularization, that stands in clear violation of the 
prescriptions of law. We must determine if the High Court erred 
in directing the Appellant-Corporation to absorb the Respondents 
into permanent service , despite their failure to meet the 
mandatory eligibility criteria prescribed by a scheme previously 
approved by this Court. 
5. Pursuant to the order dated 18.01.2011 passed by this Court 
in Civil Appeal Nos. 953 -968 of 2005 (titled D.V. Anil Kumar & 
Ors. vs. LIC of India), the Appellant-Corporation was directed to 
fill vacancies for Class -IV employees through a one -time 
absorption scheme. This scheme specifically required candidates 
to have rendered at least five years of continuous service as of 
the cut-off date of 18.01.2011. 
6. In compliance with the aforementioned order, an employment 
notice was issued on 20.05.2011. While the Respondents 
participated in the selection process, the Appellant -Corporation 
determined they were ineligible for having failed to meet the 
requisite criteria, specifically the duration of service. 
Aggrieved, the Respondents approached the High Court for the State 

--- Page 566 ---
 
3 
 
of Telangana via Writ Petition No. 2288 of 2013. The learned Single 
Judge, by order dated 14.06.2022, allowed the petition and 
directed the Appellant-Corporation as follows: 
"In view thereof, this Court is of the opinion that 
employment certificate and experience certificates 
are given by the office/respondent in respect of 
these four petitioners and therefore, the 
respondents cannot deny the opportunity of 
employment to the p etitioners when they have 
qualified in the written examination and also 
interview and medical test. In view thereof, the 
respondents are directed to absorb the petitioners 
into the services as sub-staff subject to employment 
certificate given to each of th e petitioners being 
not cancelled by the respondent corporation." . 
 
7. It is a matter of record that the Respondents' candidatures 
were officially cancelled by the Appellant -Corporation on 
15.02.2012 following internal investigations. Subsequently, fresh 
cancellation orders were issued on 30.08.2022 after the Appellant-
Corporation reconsidered the Respondents' representations 
following the order passed by the High Court in a subsequent 
proceeding, which was in the year 2022. 
8. Be that as it may, following subsequent litigation including 
Review Petitions and Contempt Cases, the matter was carried before 
the Division Bench via Writ Appeal (W.A.) Nos. 1426 & 1432 of 2024 
and 126 & 159 of 2025. These appeals were decided in terms of the 
impugned common judgment dated 10.02.2025 passed by the High Court 
for the State of Telangana at Hyderabad. 

--- Page 567 ---
 
4 
 
9. It is not in dispute that, as per the scheme approved by this 
Court, a candidate was required to fulfil specific conditions of 
eligibility, most notably rendering five years of continuous 
service as on the cut -off date of 18.01.2011.  Apparently, none 
of the respondents qualified the same. 
10. The Respondents placed reliance upon temporary employment 
certificates issued by various Branch Managers; however, the 
Appellant-Corporation repelled these, contending that they were 
bogus, falsified, or issued without following recruitment 
instructions. Internal vigilance investigations revealed that 
several candidates had not completed the mandatory five -year 
tenure. 
11. Within this spectrum of facts, we hold the findings returned 
by the learned Single Judge —and subsequently modified by the 
Division Bench —regarding the Respondents' fulfilment of 
eligibility criteria, to be problematic. In this backdrop, it is 
necessary to quash and set aside the impugned common judgment 
dated 10.02.2025.  Noticeably, post -cancellation of initial 
appointments, respondents continued to work only under interim 
orders passed by the Court(s) in several petitions /appeals 
preferred by either pa rty, and all this , despite the present 
Appellant-Corporation, contesting the respondents' claim on 
merits. 

--- Page 568 ---
 
5 
 
12. However, we are not inclined to let the matter rest here, as 
the private Respondents have served the institution for a 
considerable period , even though by virtue of interim orders 
passed in various proceedings, and at this belated stage, equity 
demands that their service not be terminated. There is no evidence 
of misconduct by the Respondents during their service s all 
throughout.  
13. As such, we dispose of the present appeals with the following 
directions: 
(A) While we quash and set aside the impugned judgment, we further 
direct that the Respondents be allowed to continue to discharge 
their duties on the very same post and same status, as they are 
officiating today, but with no further promotional benefits. 
(B) They shall be allowed to continue in service until the 
prescribed age of superannuation. 
One respondent, P. Muthaiah, having already superannuated & 
passed away, no recovery of dues shall be made from his legal 
heirs. 
(C) In the absence of a legally sustainable claim qua 
regularization of the Respondents in their current posts, we 
direct the payment of an ex-gratia lump sum amount of                
 

--- Page 569 ---
 
6 
 
Rs. 5 lakhs to each one of the serving Respondents, as also to 
the widow/ legal representative  of Sh. P. Muthaiah , who stood 
superannuated, payable within a period of four weeks, to be 
remitted directly into their respective bank accounts. 
(D) All claims of the respondents shall stand settled , and no 
amount paid in excess, if any, shall be recovered from them.  
14. The appeals stand allowed and disposed of in the aforesaid 
terms. 
 
 
..........................J. 
(SANJAY KAROL) 
 
 
 
  
 
                       .............................J. 
   (AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH) 
 
 
NEW DELHI 
May 29, 2026

Criminal Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.5692/2026 6

--- Page 576 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION(S)(CRIMINAL)  NO(S).  943/2025
DEEPAK NAGPAL                                   PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
M/S ANABOND LIMITED                            RESPONDENT(S)
WITH
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO(S). 1160/2025
O R D E R
1. The petitioner (common in both the petitions) has
filed these transfer petitions for transfer of cases
bearing C.C. No.1625/2024 & STC No.1624/2024 titled as
“M/s Anabond Limited vs. Deepak Nagpal"   filed by the
respondent-Company  under  Sections  138  &  142  of  the
Negotiable  Instruments  Act,  1881,  from  the  Court  of
Metropolitan  Magistrate(Fast  Track  Judge)  Saidapet,
Chennai, Tamil Nadu to the competent Court of First-Class
Judicial Magistrate, Jabalpur, Madhya Pradesh. 
2. As  per  order  dated  24.03.2026  in  T.P.(Crl.)
No.1160/2025  by  mutual  consent,  the  parties  were
relegated  to  take  recourse  of  mediation  before  the
Supreme Court Mediation Centre.  As per the report dated
15.04.2026 received from the Mediation Centre, mediation
between the parties remained unsuccessful.
3. Having heard learned counsel for the parties ,  the

--- Page 577 ---
2
petitioner is the proprietor of M/s Deepak Motors and is
carrying on business at Jabalpur, Madhya Pradesh. The
petitioner has placed on record documents to indicate
that his business is situated at Jabalpur and that the
transactions  were  conducted  in  connection  with  such
business.
4. It is not in dispute that the cheque which forms the
subject matter of the complaint was drawn on Karnataka
Bank, Jabalpur Branch. The petitioner is residing and
carrying on business at Jabalpur and has asserted that
the relevant records pertaining to the transactions as
well as the witnesses are available at Jabalpur. 
5.  The  respondent  opposes  the  transfer  petition  and
relies upon the averments made in the complaint that the
cheque was presented through its bank account maintained
at HDFC Bank, Radhakrishnan Salai, Mylapore, Chennai and,
therefore, the Court at Chennai possesses jurisdiction
under Section 142(2) of the Negotiable Instruments Act,
1881.
6. In our view, the power of transfer vested in this
Court by virtue of Section 446 of the Bharatiya Nagarik
Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 is of a wider amplitude and is
intended to secure the ends of justice whenever the
circumstances of the case so demand.

--- Page 578 ---
3
7. On a consideration of the material placed before us,
we find that the dispute has a substantial nexus with
Jabalpur. The petitioner resides there; his proprietary
concern is situated there; the cheque in question was
drawn on a bank located there; and the banking records
concerning the drawer's account are available there. The
petitioner has also placed material to indicate that the
commercial  transactions  giving  rise  to  the  present
dispute were undertaken through his business established
at Jabalpur. 
8.  Significantly,  apart  from  the  location  of  the
respondent's corporate office and the presentation of the
cheque through its Chennai bank account, no substantial
part of the underlying transaction is shown to have taken
place  at  Chennai.  The  record  indicates  that  the
transactions  were  principally  connected  with  the
petitioner's business transactions outside Chennai and
that  the  drawer's  bank  as  well  as  the  petitioner's
business records are located at Jabalpur. 
9.  Moreover,  the  petitioner  is  required  to  contest
criminal proceedings nearly 1400 kilometers away from his
place of residence and business. While distance by itself
may  not  constitute  a  ground  for  transfer,  it  is  a
relevant consideration when viewed cumulatively with the

--- Page 579 ---
4
location  of  witnesses,  documentary  evidence  and  the
overall nexus of the dispute with a particular place.
10. The principle of balance of convenience is another
factor  which  persuade  us  to  exercise  the  power  of
transfer.  While  the  petitioner  would  be  required  to
undertake repeated travel from Jabalpur to Chennai for
the purpose of defending the complaint, the respondent,
being a corporate body, can adequately prosecute the
proceedings through its authorized officials and learned
counsel  at  the  transferee  forum.  The  comparative
inconvenience that would be caused to the petitioner by
retaining the proceedings at Chennai is substantially
greater than any inconvenience that may be occasioned to
the respondent by transfer of the matter. Viewed thus,
the balance of convenience clearly lies in favour of
directing  transfer  of  the  complaint  proceedings  to
Jabalpur. 
11. The object of the power of transfer is not merely to
determine convenience in a narrow sense, but to ensure
that the administration of criminal justice is fair,
practical and conducive to effective adjudication. Having
regard to the cumulative effect of the circumstances
noticed above, we are satisfied that the ends of justice
would be better served if the complaint proceedings are

--- Page 580 ---
5
tried at Jabalpur. 
12. Therefore, we deem it appropriate to allow these
transfer  petitions  and  accordingly,  order  that  the
subject  cases  be  transferred  from  the  Court  of
Metropolitan  Magistrate  (Fast  Track  Judge)  Saidapet,
Chennai, Tamil Nadu to the competent Court of First-Class
Judicial Magistrate, Jabalpur, Madhya Pradesh.
13. It is on the discretion of the transferee Court to
allow  the  presence  of  the  representative  of  the
respondent-company, virtually when necessarily required.
14. On intimation by email, the records of the cases be
transferred to the transferee Court without any delay and
immediate compliance be made.
15. The transfer petitions are, accordingly, allowed.
………………………………………………………….J.
[J.K. MAHESHWARI]
 ……………………………………………………...J.
    [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
NEW DELHI;
MAY 25, 2026.

--- Page 581 ---
6
ITEM NO.66               COURT NO.3               SECTION XVI-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
TRANSFER PETITION(S)(CRIMINAL)  NO(S).  943/2025
DEEPAK NAGPAL                                      PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
M/S ANABOND LIMITED                                RESPONDENT(S)
IA No. 246777/2025 - EX-PARTE STAY
 
WITH
T.P.(Crl.) No. 1160/2025 (XVI-A)
Mediation report received
IA No. 282780/2025 - STAY APPLICATION
 
Date : 25-05-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.K. MAHESHWARI
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. B S Rajesh Agrajit, Adv.
                   Mr. Siddharth Goswami, Adv. (through VC)
                   Mr. Narendra Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Narednra Kumar, Adv.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.      OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (C) No. 10397 of 2025)
HIDAYATHULLA K.A. … APPELLANT
VERSUS
SHALINI RAJANEESH & ORS. … RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The  appellant  being  aggrieved  by  dismissal  of  the
contempt petition alleging non-compliance of the order dated
22.4.2022 passed by the High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru
in Writ Petition Nos. 795 of 2021 and 14084 of 2020, has filed
the present appeal.
2. Before the High Court in the writ petition, the challenge
was to the order dated 03.09.2020 of the Karnataka State
Administrative Tribunal passed in Original Application No.
2139 of 2010 and the order dated 17.07.2019 passed by Central
Administrative Tribunal in OA No. 170/0410 of 2019.  It is
necessary to note that before the KSAT, the appellant sought
seniority and other benefits and before the CAT, he sought

--- Page 583 ---
2
promotion as an IAS.  The High Court of Karnataka, vide
detailed  order  dated  22.04.2022  issued  the  following
directions :
“(I) Writ Petition No. 14084/2020 is partly
allowed. 
(II)  Order  dated  03.09.2020  passed  in
Application No. 2139/2010 by the Karnataka
State Administrative Tribunal is set aside.
(III) The Respondent-State is directed to
create a supernumerary post and appoint the
petitioner  to  the  post  of  Assistant
Commissioner, Revenue Department, KAS Group
A(Junior Scale) from the earliest point
available in the recruitment notification
dated 15.12.1999 for such appointment and
grant all notional benefits including the
seniority  from  the  date  of  such
appointment.
(IV) In view of the observation made above,
Writ  Petition  No.  795/2021  and  Writ
Petition No. 10411/2021 and Writ Petition
No. 16349/2021 are disposed of.
(V) Under the facts and circumstances of
the case parties to bear their cost.”
3. The  appellant  filed  contempt  petition  alleging  non-
compliance of the aforesaid directions, which was dismissed
vide impugned order. The High Court while dismissing the
contempt petition, observed on the issue of entitlement that
appellant he has not served the actual continuous service as

--- Page 584 ---
3
required.  On entertaining the present special leave petition,
this Court vide order dated 15.04.2026 referring to the
directions of the High Court in paragraph 3, observed as under
:
“3) After hearing learned counsel for the
parties, in our view, when a direction has
been  issued  by  the  Court,  including
creation of supernumerary post to extend
the benefit, the pretext of not having a
continuous regular service for the purpose
of extending the notional benefit is of no
relevance. Therefore, the High Court was
not  justified  to  dismiss  the  contempt
petition.”
4. In furtherance thereto, now, the compliance affidavit has
been filed wherein referencing a letter dated 05.05.2026 from
the office of the Chief Secretary to Government, Government of
Karnataka to the Secretary to Government of India, Ministry of
Personnel, Public Grievances & Pensions, it is stated by the
respondents that compliance has been made.  Learned counsel
representing the appellant has vociferously urged that the
letter does not indicate the actual compliance.  In reference
to  The  Indian  Administrative  Service  (Appointment  and
Promotion)  Regulations,  1955,  it  is  urged  that  without
constitution  of  the  Committee  and  taking  recourse  as
contemplated in Regulation 5(1), 5(2) and 6 and thereafter
following a procedure as contemplated in Regulation 7(1) and

--- Page 585 ---
4
9, compliance as stated cannot be treated as full compliance.
5. Having considered the submissions as made before us and
looking to the nature of the directions, at present, it is
suffice  to  say  that  the  case  of  the  appellant  may  be
considered by the authorities following the Regulations in
terms of the letter dated 05.05.2026 and taking recourse by
the Secretary to the Government of India, Department of
Personnel, Public Grievances & Pensions and needful be done
within a period of three months.  In case, any adverse order
is passed against the appellant, he is at liberty to take
recourse of law afresh in appropriate forum.
6. With the aforesaid observations, modifying the order of
the High Court, the appeal stands disposed of.  Pending
applications, if any, shall also stand disposed of.

1. The  petitioner-wife  has  instituted  the  present  Transfer
Petition seeking transfer of Matrimonial Case No.532 of 2025 that
is  pending  before  the  learned  Principal  Judge,  Family  Court,
Mysuru,  Karnataka  to  the  Court  of  learned  Principal  Judge,
Family Court, Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh.
2. By  an  order  dated  17.12.2025,  the  dispute  between  the
parties  was  referred  to  the  Supreme  Court  Mediation  Centre.
During the course of said proceedings, the petitioner-wife and the
respondent-husband  have  entered  into  a  Memorandum  of
Settlement dated 14.03.2026 whereby they have resolved to have
their marriage dissolved by mutual consent. The relevant terms
Transfer Petition (Civil) No.1908/2025 Page 1

--- Page 589 ---
of the settlement as stated in the Memorandum of Settlement
dated 14.03.2026 are as under:
“1. That the Parties have resolved all their matrimonial and other
ancillary disputes amicably and there are no claim(s) against each
other. 
2. That the second party (husband) has returned the jewellery,
clothes/goods of the First Party (Wife), the receipt of which is being
acknowledged by the first Party with the affirmation that all her
claims with regards to her jewellery, clothes/goods, are realized and
therefore, remains no future claim of first party and second party
with regards to all these things.
3. That the parties to this settlement would not take up any
future claim against each other with respect to all articles or any
other things henceforth. 
4. That the First party would not make any claim towards her
past,  present  or  future  maintenance  in  any  manner  or  in  any
circumstances  whatsoever  from  the  Second  Party  or  his  family
members.  That  the  Second  party  (husband)  had  returned  on
19.01.2026  back  the  jewellery,  clothes/goods  of  the  First  Party
(Wife).  A  copy  of  the  items  returned  on  19.01.2026  is  annexed
hereto and marked as Annexure-A. 
5. That  the  First  Party  had  unequivocally  signified  her
willingness  to  handover  the  Golf  Club  Membership  card  in  her
possession to the Second Party in original and accordingly, the said
Golf Club Membership card was duly handed over by the First Party
to the Second Party at the time of filing of the Petition under Section
13-B of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 before the Family Court,
Lucknow, the receipt whereof stands acknowledged by the Second
Party.

instrument  that  any article/goods/jewellery,  etc.,  the possession
whereof  remained  with  the  Second  Party  has  been  returned  on
14.03.2026 in the sitting of the present mediation proceedings. A
list of items returned on 14.03.2026 in annexed hereto and marked
as Annexure-B.

one  time  maintenance  and  alimony,  permanent  alimony  past,
present and future maintenance of the Second Party to which the
First Party accords her unequivocal acceptance and signifies her

First Party.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Special Leave Petition (C) No. 17507 of 2026
SMT. SHAGUN RANA … PETITIONER
VERSUS
SHIVAM TANWAR  … RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. The present special leave petition has been preferred
by the wife challenging the order dated 03.04.2026 of the
High Court of Judicature at Allahabad, whereby the High
Court has refused to grant the following reliefs as
prayed – 
(i)  Direct  the  Principal  Judge  Family  Court,
Ghaziabad to decide the Divorce Petition under
Section 13B(1) HMA Petition No. 159 of 2026 in
between (Smt. Sagun Rana Vs. Shrui Shivam Tanvar)
under  section  13B  Hindu  Marriage  Act,  pending
before Principal Judge Family Court Ghaziabad since
21.01.2026, expeditiously as possible with a short
and stipulated period and it is also further prayed
that court may exempt the provision of second
motion under section 13B HMA. 
(ii)  Direct  the  Principal  Judge  Family  Court,
Ghaziabad  to  ensure  the  presence  of  present
petitioner through video conference from Canada to
apply under the Rules for Video Conferencing for
Courts in the State of Uttar Pradesh, 2020 for
video conference facility in judicial proceedings.
(iii) Issue any other writ, order or direction
which this Hon’ble court may deem fit and proper

--- Page 595 ---
2
under the fact and circumstances of the case.
(iv) Award the cost of the petition in favour of
the petitioner.
2. It is the case of the wife that the marriage between
parties was solemnized on 08.01.2024 as per Hindu rites
and religious customs. Eventually, disputes arose and
despite sincere efforts to reconcile, the cohabitation
together  became  intolerable.  The  parties  have  been
residing separately since June 2024 and over the said
period, they have established no matrimonial relationship
between them. 
3. Given the circumstances, the parties mutually filed a
joint petition seeking divorce by mutual consent being
Petition No. 159 of 2026, pending before Principal Judge,
Family  Court,  Ghaziabad.  During  the  pendency  of  the
petition, the parties also filed a joint application
seeking waiver of the statutory cooling-off period. The
Principal  Judge,  Family  Court,  Ghaziabad,  vide  order
dated 11.03.2026 transferred the said application for
adjudication before the Ld. Additional Principal Judge.
Aggrieved, the wife preferred writ petition under Article
227 of the Constitution of India seeking reliefs as

--- Page 596 ---
3
mentioned in paragraph 1.
4. We heard learned counsel for the petitioner-wife and
also the wife who joined through video conferencing. On a
query put to the wife, she stated that it is case of
irretrievable breakdown of marriage, which is not refuted
by the counsel for the respondent-husband. Considering
the aforesaid, we issued notice in the matter and granted
liberty to the parties to file joint application seeking
invocation  of  powers  under  Article  142  of  the
Constitution of India for dissolution of marriage by
grant of decree of divorce by mutual consent. 
5. In compliance, the parties have filed I.A. No. 165321
of 20261 praying as under – 
i. Dissolve the marriage between the parties by way
of Decree of Divorce by Mutual Consent by invoking
inherent  powers  under  Article  142  of  the
Constitution of India; and
ii. Pass such other and further order it may deem
fir and proper under the circumstances of the case.
6. We have perused the application and relevant records.
It is evident that the marriage between the parties was
solemnized on 08.01.2024 and that they have been living
separately since June 2024. The parties have settled all
their disputes and differences amicably. There remains no
1 Joint application under Article 142 of Constitution of India for dissolution of
Marriage by decree of divorce by way of mutual consent, dated 23.05.2026.

--- Page 597 ---
4
possibility  of  reconciliation  or  resumption  of
cohabitation.  Continuation  of  the  matrimonial
relationship would serve no useful purpose and would only
prolong the agony of the parties.  
7. The  petitioner-wife  joined  virtually  while
respondent-husband  appeared  personally.  We  have
interacted with both of them. During interaction it is
informed by them that the possibility of union is not
there and they wish to have a decree of divorce by mutual
consent for which joint application for dissolution of
marriage by decree of divorce by way of mutual consent
has been filed by them. We are satisfied that this is a
fit case for exercise of powers under Article 142 of the
Constitution of India. Accordingly, I.A. No. 165321 of
2026 is allowed. The marriage solemnized between the
parties on 08.01.2024 is hereby dissolved by a decree of
divorce by mutual consent in exercise of powers under
Article 142 of the Constitution of India.
8. The pending Petition No. 159 of 2026 before the
Principal  Judge,  Family  Court,  Ghaziabad  shall  stand
disposed of in view of the present order. All pending
applications, if any, shall also stand disposed of.

--- Page 598 ---
5
9. Accordingly, the present petition stands disposed-of.
…………………………………………………,J.
 [J.K. MAHESHWARI]
…………………………………………………,J.
               [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
New Delhi;
May 27, 2026.

Leave granted.

appellant-wife.

during  the  pendency  of  this  appeal,  the  parties  have
negotiated a settlement. Also, a joint application has been
filed under Article 142 of the Constitution of India under
which, the parties have resolved to part ways on certain
terms and conditions stated in the joint application which
has  also  appended  with  the  Settlement  agreement  dated
08.04.2026 arrived at between the parties before the Supreme
Court Mediation Centre.
1

--- Page 601 ---
4. Learned Senior Counsel for the appellant-wife submitted
that the appellant has accepted the terms and conditions of
the Settlement Agreement. She, therefore, does not press the
appeal on merits however the appeal may be disposed of in
terms of the Settlement Agreement. 
5. In that regard, learned senior counsel appearing for the
appellant-wife and learned counsel for the respondent-husband
submitted that the minor son Jatin alias Jayant would remain
with  the  appellant-mother;  that  a  total  sum  of
Rs.1,00,00,000/-(Rupees One Crore only) is the permanent

appellant-wife towards the maintenance and living expenses of
the appellant as well as the minor son; that already a sum of
Rs.30,00,000/-(Rupees Thirty Lakhs only) has been received by
the appellant and the balance amount of Rs.70,00,000/-(Rupees

appellant by way of a demand draft today. 
6. Learned  senior  counsel  and  learned  counsel  for  the

pending cases and in view of the settlement arrived at
between the parties, the said cases may also be quashed:
2

--- Page 602 ---
“(i) Application bearing No.APP/57/2024 pending before

Section 379/215, BNSS pending before the Principal
Judge, Family Court at Gurugram, Haryana.”
7. The other terms and conditions of the settlement are
also delineated in the Settlement Agreement dated 8.04.2026.
Hence,  the  settlement  agreement  as  well  as  the  joint
application may be taken on record and the appeal may be
disposed  of  in  terms  of  the  said  application  and  the
Settlement Agreement.
8. The parties are also present before this Court. They
have been identified by their respective counsel. 
9. When we queried the parties about their decision to part
ways on the terms and conditions delineated in the settlement
agreement, they stated that they have indeed decided on their
own free volition and without there being any undue influence
from any side to part ways; that they have been living
separately since the year 2010. The appellant wife stated
that she would not press the appeal insofar as the impugned
judgment of dissolution of the marriage granted by the High
Court  is  concerned;  that  she  has  received  a  sum  of
Rs.30,00,000/- plus Rs.70,00,000/- totally Rs.1,00,00,000/-
towards permanent alimony as well as towards the upbringing
3

--- Page 603 ---
of the minor son who resides with her. Learned counsel for
the  respondent  has  handed  over  a  demand  draft  bearing
No.791483  dated  16.05.2026  for  a  sum  of  Rs.70,00,000/-
(Rupees Seventy Lakhs Only) issued by the State Bank of
India, Narnaul Branch, Haryana. The receipt of the said
demand draft is acknowledged by the learned senior counsel
for the appellant as well as by the appellant-wife. The
aforesaid payment is in full and final settlement of all her
claims of the appellant herein.  The right, title and
interest of the minor son in the ancestral or separate
properties of the respondent are left open to be claimed by
him at an appropriate stage and time. In the circumstances,
they stated that this Court may pass orders in the matter.
10. In the above backdrop, we have taken on record the
Settlement Agreement dated 08.04.2026 and also the Joint
Application filed under Article 142 of the Constitution of
India. On perusal of the same, we find that there is no legal
impediment in accepting the terms of the Settlement Agreement
and the prayers made in the Joint Application. Consequently,
by accepting the terms of the settlement, we allow the Joint
Application and direct the parties to abide by the terms of
the Settlement Agreement dated 08.04.2026.
4

--- Page 604 ---
11. The settlement agreement dated 08.04.2026 shall be read as
part and parcel of this order.
12. In view of the above, the impugned judgment of the
Division Bench of the High Court would not call for any
interference.
13. We are informed by learned senior counsel and learned
counsel for the respective parties that the learned Mediator
has  made  great  endeavours  to  bring  about  an  amicable
settlement  between  the  parties.  We  place  on  record  our
appreciation of the efforts of the learned Mediator. 
14. The appeal is disposed of in the aforesaid terms.
15. The Registry to draw-up a decree in the aforesaid terms.
16. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
…………………………………………….J.
                                           (B.V. NAGARATHNA)

--- Page 606 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.          /2026
[@ SLP (CRL.) NO.8556/2026]
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.          /2026
[@ SLP (CRL.) NO.8461/2026]
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.          /2026
[@ SLP (CRL.) NO.8368/2026]
NEHRU IRULANDI                                APPELLANT(s)
                          VERSUS
STATE OF TAMIL NADU & ORS.                   RESPONDENT(s)
O R D E R
1. Exemption applications are allowed.
2. Leave granted.
3. These appeals arise from the common order passed by
the  High  Court  dated  24.02.2026  in  Crl.  O.P.  (MD)
No.2054/2026  by  which  the  High  Court  while  granting
anticipatory bail to the appellants-herein in connection
with Crime No.6/2026, registered with Perunazhi Police
Station,  Madurai,  for  the  offence  punishable  under
Sections 189(2), 329(3), 324(4), 115(2), 351(2) of the
Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 respectively (for short,
“the BNS, 2023”) imposed a condition that the appellants
and other co-accused shall not interfere in the disputed

--- Page 607 ---
2
property until the conclusion of the Civil Suit instituted
by the complainant.
4. While granting anticipatory bail as prayed for, the
High Court imposed the following conditions:-
“[a] the petitioners and the sureties shall affix
their photographs and left thumb impression in
the surety bond and the Magistrate may obtain a
copy of their Aadhaar card or bank pass book to
ensure their identity;
[b] the petitioners 1 and 2 shall report before
the respondent police daily at 10.30 a.m., for a
period of two weeks and thereafter, as and when
required  for  interrogation;  Further,  the
petitioners 3 and 4 shall report before the
respondent  police  as  and  when  required  for
interrogation. The petitioners are directed not
to interfere in the disputed property until the
suit is over.
[c] the petitioners shall not tamper with the
evidence or witness either during investigation
or trial.
[d] the petitioners shall not abscond either
during investigation or trial.
[e] On breach of any of the aforesaid conditions,
the learned Magistrate/Trial Court is entitled to
take appropriate action against the petitioners
in accordance with law as if the conditions have
been imposed and the petitioners are released on
bail  by  the  learned  Magistrate/Trial  Court
himself as laid down by the Hon’ble Supreme Court
in P.K.Shaji vs. State of Kerala [(2005)AIR SCW
5560].
[f] If the accused thereafter absconds, a fresh
FIR can be registered under Section 269 of BNS.”

--- Page 608 ---
3
5. We take notice of the fact that the appellants are
the  defendants  in  the  Civil  Suit  instituted  by  the
complainant.  The  Civil  Suit  has  been  instituted  for
permanent injunction.
6. Prima-facie,  it  appears  that  the  case  of  the
appellant, namely, Nehru Irulandi as one of the defendants
is that he has purchased the suit property.
7. Be that as it may, the Suit is of the year 2019. The
plaintiff has not prayed for any injunction pending the
Suit.
8. We are informed that an application was preferred
praying for injunction under Order 39 Rules 1 and 2 of the
CPC respectively, however, the same has not been taken up
for hearing.
9. We are of the view that while granting anticipatory
bail, High Court should not have imposed a condition which
tantamount to granting of some relief in the Suit itself,
which otherwise the Civil Court has not granted.
10. In view of the aforesaid, the condition no.9(b) is
set-aside to the extent it has directed not to interfere
in the disputed property until the Suit is over. Rest of
the conditions shall be abided by the appellants.
11. If the complainant as plaintiff has any apprehension
as regards the breach of peace, interference with the suit
property, etc., he may pray for appropriate relief before
the Civil Court or any appropriate forum in accordance
with law.

--- Page 609 ---
4
12. In view of the aforesaid, the appeals are allowed in
part.
13. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

[J.B. PARDIWALA]
……………………………………………J.

27th MAY, 2026.

1. The  petitioner-wife  has  instituted  the  present  Transfer

Mysuru,  Karnataka  to  the  Court  of  learned  Principal  Judge,
Family Court, Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh.

parties  was  referred  to  the  Supreme  Court  Mediation  Centre.
During the course of said proceedings, the petitioner-wife and the
respondent-husband  have  entered  into  a  Memorandum  of
Settlement dated 14.03.2026 whereby they have resolved to have
their marriage dissolved by mutual consent. The relevant terms
Transfer Petition (Civil) No.1908/2025 Page 1

--- Page 613 ---
of the settlement as stated in the Memorandum of Settlement
dated 14.03.2026 are as under:
“1. That the Parties have resolved all their matrimonial and other
ancillary disputes amicably and there are no claim(s) against each
other. 
2. That the second party (husband) has returned the jewellery,
clothes/goods of the First Party (Wife), the receipt of which is being
acknowledged by the first Party with the affirmation that all her
claims with regards to her jewellery, clothes/goods, are realized and
therefore, remains no future claim of first party and second party
with regards to all these things.

future claim against each other with respect to all articles or any
other things henceforth.

(Wife).  A  copy  of  the  items  returned  on  19.01.2026  is  annexed

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Special Leave Petition (C) No. 17507 of 2026
SMT. SHAGUN RANA … PETITIONER
VERSUS
SHIVAM TANWAR  … RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. The present special leave petition has been preferred
by the wife challenging the order dated 03.04.2026 of the
High Court of Judicature at Allahabad, whereby the High
Court has refused to grant the following reliefs as
prayed – 
(i)  Direct  the  Principal  Judge  Family  Court,
Ghaziabad to decide the Divorce Petition under
Section 13B(1) HMA Petition No. 159 of 2026 in
between (Smt. Sagun Rana Vs. Shrui Shivam Tanvar)
under  section  13B  Hindu  Marriage  Act,  pending
before Principal Judge Family Court Ghaziabad since
21.01.2026, expeditiously as possible with a short
and stipulated period and it is also further prayed
that court may exempt the provision of second
motion under section 13B HMA. 
(ii)  Direct  the  Principal  Judge  Family  Court,
Ghaziabad  to  ensure  the  presence  of  present
petitioner through video conference from Canada to
apply under the Rules for Video Conferencing for
Courts in the State of Uttar Pradesh, 2020 for
video conference facility in judicial proceedings.
(iii) Issue any other writ, order or direction
which this Hon’ble court may deem fit and proper

--- Page 619 ---
2
under the fact and circumstances of the case.
(iv) Award the cost of the petition in favour of
the petitioner.
2. It is the case of the wife that the marriage between
parties was solemnized on 08.01.2024 as per Hindu rites
and religious customs. Eventually, disputes arose and
despite sincere efforts to reconcile, the cohabitation
together  became  intolerable.  The  parties  have  been
residing separately since June 2024 and over the said
period, they have established no matrimonial relationship
between them. 
3. Given the circumstances, the parties mutually filed a
joint petition seeking divorce by mutual consent being
Petition No. 159 of 2026, pending before Principal Judge,
Family  Court,  Ghaziabad.  During  the  pendency  of  the
petition, the parties also filed a joint application
seeking waiver of the statutory cooling-off period. The
Principal  Judge,  Family  Court,  Ghaziabad,  vide  order
dated 11.03.2026 transferred the said application for
adjudication before the Ld. Additional Principal Judge.
Aggrieved, the wife preferred writ petition under Article
227 of the Constitution of India seeking reliefs as

--- Page 620 ---
3
mentioned in paragraph 1.
4. We heard learned counsel for the petitioner-wife and
also the wife who joined through video conferencing. On a
query put to the wife, she stated that it is case of
irretrievable breakdown of marriage, which is not refuted
by the counsel for the respondent-husband. Considering
the aforesaid, we issued notice in the matter and granted
liberty to the parties to file joint application seeking
invocation  of  powers  under  Article  142  of  the
Constitution of India for dissolution of marriage by
grant of decree of divorce by mutual consent. 
5. In compliance, the parties have filed I.A. No. 165321
of 20261 praying as under – 
i. Dissolve the marriage between the parties by way
of Decree of Divorce by Mutual Consent by invoking
inherent  powers  under  Article  142  of  the
Constitution of India; and
ii. Pass such other and further order it may deem
fir and proper under the circumstances of the case.
6. We have perused the application and relevant records.
It is evident that the marriage between the parties was
solemnized on 08.01.2024 and that they have been living
separately since June 2024. The parties have settled all
their disputes and differences amicably. There remains no
1 Joint application under Article 142 of Constitution of India for dissolution of
Marriage by decree of divorce by way of mutual consent, dated 23.05.2026.

--- Page 621 ---
4
possibility  of  reconciliation  or  resumption  of
cohabitation.  Continuation  of  the  matrimonial
relationship would serve no useful purpose and would only
prolong the agony of the parties.  
7. The  petitioner-wife  joined  virtually  while
respondent-husband  appeared  personally.  We  have
interacted with both of them. During interaction it is
informed by them that the possibility of union is not
there and they wish to have a decree of divorce by mutual
consent for which joint application for dissolution of
marriage by decree of divorce by way of mutual consent
has been filed by them. We are satisfied that this is a
fit case for exercise of powers under Article 142 of the
Constitution of India. Accordingly, I.A. No. 165321 of
2026 is allowed. The marriage solemnized between the
parties on 08.01.2024 is hereby dissolved by a decree of
divorce by mutual consent in exercise of powers under
Article 142 of the Constitution of India.
8. The pending Petition No. 159 of 2026 before the
Principal  Judge,  Family  Court,  Ghaziabad  shall  stand
disposed of in view of the present order. All pending
applications, if any, shall also stand disposed of.

--- Page 622 ---
5
9. Accordingly, the present petition stands disposed-of.
…………………………………………………,J.
 [J.K. MAHESHWARI]
…………………………………………………,J.
               [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
New Delhi;
May 27, 2026.

--- Page 624 ---
