IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION(S)(CRIMINAL)  NO(S).  943/2025
DEEPAK NAGPAL                                   PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
M/S ANABOND LIMITED                            RESPONDENT(S)
WITH
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO(S). 1160/2025
O R D E R
1. The petitioner (common in both the petitions) has
filed these transfer petitions for transfer of cases
bearing C.C. No.1625/2024 & STC No.1624/2024 titled as
“M/s Anabond Limited vs. Deepak Nagpal"   filed by the
respondent-Company  under  Sections  138  &  142  of  the
Negotiable  Instruments  Act,  1881,  from  the  Court  of
Metropolitan  Magistrate(Fast  Track  Judge)  Saidapet,
Chennai, Tamil Nadu to the competent Court of First-Class
Judicial Magistrate, Jabalpur, Madhya Pradesh. 
2. As  per  order  dated  24.03.2026  in  T.P.(Crl.)
No.1160/2025  by  mutual  consent,  the  parties  were
relegated  to  take  recourse  of  mediation  before  the
Supreme Court Mediation Centre.  As per the report dated
15.04.2026 received from the Mediation Centre, mediation
between the parties remained unsuccessful.
3. Having heard learned counsel for the parties ,  the

--- Page 625 ---
2
petitioner is the proprietor of M/s Deepak Motors and is
carrying on business at Jabalpur, Madhya Pradesh. The
petitioner has placed on record documents to indicate
that his business is situated at Jabalpur and that the
transactions  were  conducted  in  connection  with  such
business.
4. It is not in dispute that the cheque which forms the
subject matter of the complaint was drawn on Karnataka
Bank, Jabalpur Branch. The petitioner is residing and
carrying on business at Jabalpur and has asserted that
the relevant records pertaining to the transactions as
well as the witnesses are available at Jabalpur. 
5.  The  respondent  opposes  the  transfer  petition  and
relies upon the averments made in the complaint that the
cheque was presented through its bank account maintained
at HDFC Bank, Radhakrishnan Salai, Mylapore, Chennai and,
therefore, the Court at Chennai possesses jurisdiction
under Section 142(2) of the Negotiable Instruments Act,
1881.
6. In our view, the power of transfer vested in this
Court by virtue of Section 446 of the Bharatiya Nagarik
Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 is of a wider amplitude and is
intended to secure the ends of justice whenever the
circumstances of the case so demand.

--- Page 626 ---
3
7. On a consideration of the material placed before us,
we find that the dispute has a substantial nexus with
Jabalpur. The petitioner resides there; his proprietary
concern is situated there; the cheque in question was
drawn on a bank located there; and the banking records
concerning the drawer's account are available there. The
petitioner has also placed material to indicate that the
commercial  transactions  giving  rise  to  the  present
dispute were undertaken through his business established
at Jabalpur. 
8.  Significantly,  apart  from  the  location  of  the
respondent's corporate office and the presentation of the
cheque through its Chennai bank account, no substantial
part of the underlying transaction is shown to have taken
place  at  Chennai.  The  record  indicates  that  the
transactions  were  principally  connected  with  the
petitioner's business transactions outside Chennai and
that  the  drawer's  bank  as  well  as  the  petitioner's
business records are located at Jabalpur. 
9.  Moreover,  the  petitioner  is  required  to  contest
criminal proceedings nearly 1400 kilometers away from his
place of residence and business. While distance by itself
may  not  constitute  a  ground  for  transfer,  it  is  a
relevant consideration when viewed cumulatively with the

--- Page 627 ---
4
location  of  witnesses,  documentary  evidence  and  the
overall nexus of the dispute with a particular place.
10. The principle of balance of convenience is another
factor  which  persuade  us  to  exercise  the  power  of
transfer.  While  the  petitioner  would  be  required  to
undertake repeated travel from Jabalpur to Chennai for
the purpose of defending the complaint, the respondent,
being a corporate body, can adequately prosecute the
proceedings through its authorized officials and learned
counsel  at  the  transferee  forum.  The  comparative
inconvenience that would be caused to the petitioner by
retaining the proceedings at Chennai is substantially
greater than any inconvenience that may be occasioned to
the respondent by transfer of the matter. Viewed thus,
the balance of convenience clearly lies in favour of
directing  transfer  of  the  complaint  proceedings  to
Jabalpur. 
11. The object of the power of transfer is not merely to
determine convenience in a narrow sense, but to ensure
that the administration of criminal justice is fair,
practical and conducive to effective adjudication. Having
regard to the cumulative effect of the circumstances
noticed above, we are satisfied that the ends of justice
would be better served if the complaint proceedings are

--- Page 628 ---
5
tried at Jabalpur. 
12. Therefore, we deem it appropriate to allow these
transfer  petitions  and  accordingly,  order  that  the
subject  cases  be  transferred  from  the  Court  of
Metropolitan  Magistrate  (Fast  Track  Judge)  Saidapet,
Chennai, Tamil Nadu to the competent Court of First-Class
Judicial Magistrate, Jabalpur, Madhya Pradesh.
13. It is on the discretion of the transferee Court to
allow  the  presence  of  the  representative  of  the
respondent-company, virtually when necessarily required.
14. On intimation by email, the records of the cases be
transferred to the transferee Court without any delay and
immediate compliance be made.
15. The transfer petitions are, accordingly, allowed.
………………………………………………………….J.
[J.K. MAHESHWARI]
 ……………………………………………………...J.
    [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
NEW DELHI;
MAY 25, 2026.

--- Page 629 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.      OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (C) No. 10397 of 2025)
HIDAYATHULLA K.A. … APPELLANT
VERSUS
SHALINI RAJANEESH & ORS. … RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The  appellant  being  aggrieved  by  dismissal  of  the
contempt petition alleging non-compliance of the order dated
22.4.2022 passed by the High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru
in Writ Petition Nos. 795 of 2021 and 14084 of 2020, has filed
the present appeal.
2. Before the High Court in the writ petition, the challenge
was to the order dated 03.09.2020 of the Karnataka State
Administrative Tribunal passed in Original Application No.
2139 of 2010 and the order dated 17.07.2019 passed by Central
Administrative Tribunal in OA No. 170/0410 of 2019.  It is
necessary to note that before the KSAT, the appellant sought
seniority and other benefits and before the CAT, he sought

--- Page 631 ---
2
promotion as an IAS.  The High Court of Karnataka, vide
detailed  order  dated  22.04.2022  issued  the  following
directions :
“(I) Writ Petition No. 14084/2020 is partly
allowed. 
(II)  Order  dated  03.09.2020  passed  in
Application No. 2139/2010 by the Karnataka
State Administrative Tribunal is set aside.
(III) The Respondent-State is directed to
create a supernumerary post and appoint the
petitioner  to  the  post  of  Assistant
Commissioner, Revenue Department, KAS Group
A(Junior Scale) from the earliest point
available in the recruitment notification
dated 15.12.1999 for such appointment and
grant all notional benefits including the
seniority  from  the  date  of  such
appointment.
(IV) In view of the observation made above,
Writ  Petition  No.  795/2021  and  Writ
Petition No. 10411/2021 and Writ Petition
No. 16349/2021 are disposed of.
(V) Under the facts and circumstances of
the case parties to bear their cost.”
3. The  appellant  filed  contempt  petition  alleging  non-
compliance of the aforesaid directions, which was dismissed
vide impugned order. The High Court while dismissing the
contempt petition, observed on the issue of entitlement that
appellant he has not served the actual continuous service as

--- Page 632 ---
3
required.  On entertaining the present special leave petition,
this Court vide order dated 15.04.2026 referring to the
directions of the High Court in paragraph 3, observed as under
:
“3) After hearing learned counsel for the
parties, in our view, when a direction has
been  issued  by  the  Court,  including
creation of supernumerary post to extend
the benefit, the pretext of not having a
continuous regular service for the purpose
of extending the notional benefit is of no
relevance. Therefore, the High Court was
not  justified  to  dismiss  the  contempt
petition.”
4. In furtherance thereto, now, the compliance affidavit has
been filed wherein referencing a letter dated 05.05.2026 from
the office of the Chief Secretary to Government, Government of
Karnataka to the Secretary to Government of India, Ministry of
Personnel, Public Grievances & Pensions, it is stated by the
respondents that compliance has been made.  Learned counsel
representing the appellant has vociferously urged that the
letter does not indicate the actual compliance.  In reference
to  The  Indian  Administrative  Service  (Appointment  and
Promotion)  Regulations,  1955,  it  is  urged  that  without
constitution  of  the  Committee  and  taking  recourse  as
contemplated in Regulation 5(1), 5(2) and 6 and thereafter
following a procedure as contemplated in Regulation 7(1) and

--- Page 633 ---
4
9, compliance as stated cannot be treated as full compliance.
5. Having considered the submissions as made before us and
looking to the nature of the directions, at present, it is
suffice  to  say  that  the  case  of  the  appellant  may  be
considered by the authorities following the Regulations in
terms of the letter dated 05.05.2026 and taking recourse by
the Secretary to the Government of India, Department of
Personnel, Public Grievances & Pensions and needful be done
within a period of three months.  In case, any adverse order
is passed against the appellant, he is at liberty to take
recourse of law afresh in appropriate forum.
6. With the aforesaid observations, modifying the order of
the High Court, the appeal stands disposed of.  Pending
applications, if any, shall also stand disposed of.

1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.2984/2026
(@Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No.6811/2026)
PUSHPENDRA                                         Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P. & ORS.                               Respondent(s)
 O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. This appeal arises from the Order passed by the High Court of
Judicature at Allahabad dated 6-1-2026 in Criminal Miscellaneous
Writ Petition No.25468/2025, by which the application preferred by
the Respondent Nos.3 and 4 respectively before us – original
accused persons praying for quashing of the First Information
Report lodged by the appellant before us, came to be allowed and
thereby the FIR was ordered to be quashed. 
3. We take notice of the fact that the appellant lodged the First
Information Report bearing No. 0512 of 2025 with Police Station-
Soro Thana, District-Kasganj, UP for the offence punishable under
Sections 109 (1), 127 (2), 191 (2), 308 (5), 351 (3) and 352 of the
Bhartiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (for short, “BNS, 2023) respectively. 
4. The First Information Report reads thus:-
“12. First Information Contents:
Respectfully, it is submitted that Ayendra son of Natthu
Singh, Chandrakesh son of Ramrahees, and Vrijesh son of Nem
Singh from my village were forcibly dumping soil on my land. I
stopped them from dumping the soil. Taking offense to this, on
06.09.2025,  Ayendra,  Chandrakesh,  Vrijesh,  residents  of
Village Kumaria, along with Kuldeep Pandey and Aashish Pandey,

--- Page 637 ---
2
residents of Village Garhi Harnader, Police Station Dolna,
came to my house and said, "Either let us dump the soil, or
give us 1 lakh rupees." When I refused to give the money, all
the above-mentioned people forcibly made me sit in their
vehicle and took me outside the village, where they held me
hostage in the vehicle. Out of fear, I gave them 20,000/- that
I had on me, and they told me to arrange for the remaining
80,000/-. When I refused to give any more money, Kuldeep₹
Pandey and Aashish Pandey said to Aryendra, "Kill him, shoot
the scoundrel." Then Aryendra, with the intention of killing
me, fired at me from the pistol he had in his hand, and I had
a narrow escape. Hearing the sound of the gunshot, my brother
Avanish son of Ganga Singh, Pappu son of Ghanendra, and many
other people ran towards the spot. Seeing them approach, (the
accused) threatened to kill me, and then all of them fled with
the vehicle.
The above-mentioned people are of a criminal nature. They form
a group called the 'Kisan Union Swaraj Gaddi' and extort money
by threatening people. Therefore, I humbly request that you
register my report and take legal action against the above-
mentioned people. I shall be highly obliged. Date: 30.09.2025
SD Pushpendra Applicant Pushpendra Son of Netrapal Singh
Resident of Village Kumaraua, P.S. Soron, Kasganj, Mobile No.
9720838592 Note: I Head Constable 515 Shyamveer Singh, certify
that the copy of the application has been transcribed by me,
with word to word that may contains technical errors having
been copied exactly.”
5. The accused persons went before the High Court and prayed that
the FIR, referred to above, be quashed as the same was lodged after
a period of 24 days from the date of the incident.
6. The High Court accepted the contention canvassed on behalf of
the accused persons as regards the delay in lodging the FIR and
thought fit to quash the FIR.
7. In such circumstances, referred to above, the appellant –
original complainant is here before us with the present appeal.
8. We heard Mr. Mohit Saroha, the learned counsel appearing for
the appellant – complainant, Ms. Ruchira Goel, the learned counsel
appearing for the State of U.P. and Mr. Akshay Dev, the learned
counsel appearing for the Respondent Nos.3 and 4 respectively –
original accused persons.

--- Page 638 ---
3
9. Having regard to the nature of allegations levelled in the
FIR, the High Court should not have quashed the FIR merely on the
ground that there was a delay of 24 days in lodging the same. There
could be many good reasons for the First Informant not to immedi -
ately reach the Police Station and lodge the FIR.  However, delay
with other attending circumstances emerging from the record of the
case rendering the entire case put up by the prosecution inherently
improbable, may at times become a good ground to quash the FIR and
consequential proceedings.
10. As pointed out by the learned counsel appearing for the appel -
lant, the accused persons have criminal antecedents. 
11. Delay is one of the factors which the Trial Court may take
into consideration while appreciating the overall evidence that may
come on record in the course of the trial. Delay by itself without
anything more cannot be a ground to quash the FIR. 
12. The learned counsel appearing for the Respondent Nos.3 and 4
respectively vehemently submitted that the FIR is politically
motivated. This contention also cannot be a ground to quash the FIR
at the nascent stage. At the time of considering the case for
quashing of the FIR, the High Court is expected to look into the
nature of the crime, the nature of allegations levelled etc. In
other words, what is important for the High Court is to see whether
the FIR discloses commission of any cognizable offence or not. 
13. We take notice of the fact that the High Court has not said a
word or discussed anything insofar as the nature of the allegations
levelled in the FIR is concerned.  It is expected of the High Court
to give a fair idea in its order as regards the contents of the FIR

--- Page 639 ---
4
and what type of allegations have been levelled in the FIR. The en -
tire FIR may not be reproduced but at least when the matter travels
upto the highest Court, then if something is observed in the im -
pugned Order as regards the contents of the FIR, nature of the al -
legations, that would be helpful to understand whether there has
been proper application of mind at the end of the High Court.
14. In the overall view of the matter, we are convinced that the
impugned Order passed by the High Court is not sustainable in law.
15. In the result, this Appeal succeeds and is hereby allowed. 
16. The impugned Order passed by the High Court is quashed and the
FIR No.0512/2025 is restored. The police shall now proceed further
in accordance with law.
17. Pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.
…………………………………………J     
(J.B. PARDIWALA)
…………………………………………J     
(S.V.N. BHATTI)
NEW DELHI
29TH MAY, 2026.

--- Page 640 ---
5

 (VISHAL ANAND)                                    (POOJA SHARMA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                                 COURT MASTER (NSH)
(Signed Order is placed on the file)

--- Page 642 ---
 
1 
 
   IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
 
CIVIL APPEAL NO(s).        OF 2026  
(Arising out of SLP (C) No(s).17799-802 of 2025) 
 
 
 
  LIFE INSURANCE CORPORATION OF INDIA & ORS.       APPELLANT(s) 
 
 
                               VERSUS 
 
 
  B.D.G. PRASAD ALIAS GANESH & ORS.                RESPONDENT(s) 
 
 
 
 O R D E R 
 
 Leave granted. 
2. These appeals arise out of the impugned common judgment and 
order dated 10.02.2025 passed by the High Court for the State of 
Telangana at Hyderabad in Writ Appeal (W.A.) Nos. 1426 & 1432 of 
2024 and W.A. Nos. 126 & 159 of 2025. 
3. Mindful of the fact that the livelihoods and future of the 
employees (Respondents herein) are at stake, we proceed to 
adjudicate the present matter. The challenge of securing 
employment for youth in the current landscape is significant, and 
should these appeals be allowed, the Respondents—who are temporary 
Class-IV employees would face immediate displacement  from their 
jobs. Consequently, the resolution of th ese appeals directly 

--- Page 643 ---
 
2 
 
determine the future of these individuals. 
4. The central question for our consideration is whether, even 
if we are inclined to extend equitable relief, with our open hearts 
and extended hands, such a gesture can be allowed to result in 
regularization, that stands in clear violation of the 
prescriptions of law. We must determine if the High Court erred 
in directing the Appellant-Corporation to absorb the Respondents 
into permanent service , despite their failure to meet the 
mandatory eligibility criteria prescribed by a scheme previously 
approved by this Court. 
5. Pursuant to the order dated 18.01.2011 passed by this Court 
in Civil Appeal Nos. 953 -968 of 2005 (titled D.V. Anil Kumar & 
Ors. vs. LIC of India), the Appellant-Corporation was directed to 
fill vacancies for Class -IV employees through a one -time 
absorption scheme. This scheme specifically required candidates 
to have rendered at least five years of continuous service as of 
the cut-off date of 18.01.2011. 
6. In compliance with the aforementioned order, an employment 
notice was issued on 20.05.2011. While the Respondents 
participated in the selection process, the Appellant -Corporation 
determined they were ineligible for having failed to meet the 
requisite criteria, specifically the duration of service. 
Aggrieved, the Respondents approached the High Court for the State 

--- Page 644 ---
 
3 
 
of Telangana via Writ Petition No. 2288 of 2013. The learned Single 
Judge, by order dated 14.06.2022, allowed the petition and 
directed the Appellant-Corporation as follows: 
"In view thereof, this Court is of the opinion that 
employment certificate and experience certificates 
are given by the office/respondent in respect of 
these four petitioners and therefore, the 
respondents cannot deny the opportunity of 
employment to the p etitioners when they have 
qualified in the written examination and also 
interview and medical test. In view thereof, the 
respondents are directed to absorb the petitioners 
into the services as sub-staff subject to employment 
certificate given to each of th e petitioners being 
not cancelled by the respondent corporation." . 
 
7. It is a matter of record that the Respondents' candidatures 
were officially cancelled by the Appellant -Corporation on 
15.02.2012 following internal investigations. Subsequently, fresh 
cancellation orders were issued on 30.08.2022 after the Appellant-
Corporation reconsidered the Respondents' representations 
following the order passed by the High Court in a subsequent 
proceeding, which was in the year 2022. 
8. Be that as it may, following subsequent litigation including 
Review Petitions and Contempt Cases, the matter was carried before 
the Division Bench via Writ Appeal (W.A.) Nos. 1426 & 1432 of 2024 
and 126 & 159 of 2025. These appeals were decided in terms of the 
impugned common judgment dated 10.02.2025 passed by the High Court 
for the State of Telangana at Hyderabad. 

--- Page 645 ---
 
4 
 
9. It is not in dispute that, as per the scheme approved by this 
Court, a candidate was required to fulfil specific conditions of 
eligibility, most notably rendering five years of continuous 
service as on the cut -off date of 18.01.2011.  Apparently, none 
of the respondents qualified the same. 
10. The Respondents placed reliance upon temporary employment 
certificates issued by various Branch Managers; however, the 
Appellant-Corporation repelled these, contending that they were 
bogus, falsified, or issued without following recruitment 
instructions. Internal vigilance investigations revealed that 
several candidates had not completed the mandatory five -year 
tenure. 
11. Within this spectrum of facts, we hold the findings returned 
by the learned Single Judge —and subsequently modified by the 
Division Bench —regarding the Respondents' fulfilment of 
eligibility criteria, to be problematic. In this backdrop, it is 
necessary to quash and set aside the impugned common judgment 
dated 10.02.2025.  Noticeably, post -cancellation of initial 
appointments, respondents continued to work only under interim 
orders passed by the Court(s) in several petitions /appeals 
preferred by either pa rty, and all this , despite the present 
Appellant-Corporation, contesting the respondents' claim on 
merits. 

--- Page 646 ---
 
5 
 
12. However, we are not inclined to let the matter rest here, as 
the private Respondents have served the institution for a 
considerable period , even though by virtue of interim orders 
passed in various proceedings, and at this belated stage, equity 
demands that their service not be terminated. There is no evidence 
of misconduct by the Respondents during their service s all 
throughout.  
13. As such, we dispose of the present appeals with the following 
directions: 
(A) While we quash and set aside the impugned judgment, we further 
direct that the Respondents be allowed to continue to discharge 
their duties on the very same post and same status, as they are 
officiating today, but with no further promotional benefits. 
(B) They shall be allowed to continue in service until the 
prescribed age of superannuation. 
One respondent, P. Muthaiah, having already superannuated & 
passed away, no recovery of dues shall be made from his legal 
heirs. 
(C) In the absence of a legally sustainable claim qua 
regularization of the Respondents in their current posts, we 
direct the payment of an ex-gratia lump sum amount of                
 

--- Page 647 ---
 
6 
 
Rs. 5 lakhs to each one of the serving Respondents, as also to 
the widow/ legal representative  of Sh. P. Muthaiah , who stood 
superannuated, payable within a period of four weeks, to be 
remitted directly into their respective bank accounts. 
(D) All claims of the respondents shall stand settled , and no 
amount paid in excess, if any, shall be recovered from them.  
14. The appeals stand allowed and disposed of in the aforesaid 
terms. 
 
 
..........................J. 
(SANJAY KAROL) 
 
 
 
  
 
                       .............................J. 
   (AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH)

May 29, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
 
CIVIL APPEAL NO(s). 3675-3676  OF 2012  
 
SMT.MEENAKSHAMMA                                       APPELLANT(S)
                              VERSUS 
C.N. GOPALKRISHNA RAJU & ORS.                        RESPONDENT(S)
 
O R D E R  
 
1. These appeals arise out of the common judgment and order
dated 15.09.2010 passed by the High Court of Karnataka at
Bangalore in R.F.A. No.1587 of 2003 and R.F.A. No.202 of
2004, whereby the High Court partly modified the judgment
and decree dated 11.09.2003 passed by the Trial Court in
O.S. No.3329 of 1994.
2. The suit was instituted by the plaintiff seeking partition
and separate possession of her share in the properties
belonging to the joint family of late Shri S. Narayana Raju,
who died intestate on 15.05.1992. He was survived by his
wife, Smt. Chandramma (Defendant No.1), four sons (Defendant
Nos.2  to  5),  and  three  daughters  (the  plaintiff  and
Defendant Nos.6 and 7).
1

--- Page 661 ---
3. The suit properties were described under five schedules.
Schedule A consisted of three immovable properties situated
in Bangalore. Schedule B consisted of three commercial
properties standing in the name of Defendant No.1, Smt.
Chandramma.  Schedule  C  consisted  of  six  residential
properties standing in the names of Defendant Nos.3 to 5.
Schedule D related to a bank locker and Schedule E consisted
of certain movable properties such as gold, silver and cash.
4. The plaintiff also sought a declaration that the Release
Deed  dated  24.05.1993,  executed  by  her  in  respect  of
Schedule A properties, was null and void on the ground of
fraud, coercion and undue influence.
5. The Trial Court held that the properties comprised in
Schedules A, B and C were joint family properties. It,
however, partly decreed the suit by granting the plaintiff a
share only in Schedules B and C, while declining relief in
respect of Schedules D and E on the ground that the same
were not proved to be in existence.
6. Aggrieved thereby, appeals were preferred before the High
Court. The High Court, while affirming the rejection of the
claim in respect of Schedules D and E, modified the decree
by holding that the plaintiff was not entitled to any share
in Schedule C properties. The High Court further upheld the
validity of the Release Deed dated 24.05.1993 and re-
determined the shares in Schedules A and B in view of the
2

--- Page 662 ---
death  of  Defendant  No.1,  Smt.  Chandramma,  during  the
pendency of the proceedings. 
7. The controversy before us is now substantially confined to
the claim of the plaintiff in respect of the properties
described in Schedule C.
8. Having heard learned counsel for the parties and upon
careful consideration of the material on record, we find
that the properties included in Schedule C were acquired by
Defendant Nos.3 to 5 in their individual capacity. The
evidence on record establishes that these properties were
purchased from their own earnings, including properties
allotted by the Karnataka Housing Board on a lease-cum-sale
basis. No evidence has been led to show the existence of a
joint  family  nucleus  or  that  joint  family  funds  were
utilised for such acquisitions.
9. We also find no error in the conclusion of the High Court
with regard to the validity of the Release Deed dated
24.05.1993. The plaintiff, despite being well-educated, did
not step into the witness box to substantiate her plea of
fraud or coercion, and instead examined her husband as a
power of attorney holder, who admittedly had no personal
knowledge of the execution of the document. The burden of
proof having not been discharged, the finding of the High
Court warrants no interference.
10.  In view of the above, we find no legal infirmity in the
impugned judgment. The High Court has rightly excluded
3

--- Page 663 ---
Schedule C properties from partition and correctly affirmed
the rejection of the claim in respect of Schedules D and E.
11.  Consequently, the appeals are dismissed. The judgment and
decree passed by the High Court of Karnataka are affirmed.
All pending applications, if any, stand disposed of.
 
…………………………………………………………………. J. 
 (SANJAY KAROL) 
…………………………………………………………………. J. 
 (AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH) 
 
New Delhi 
28th January, 2026

Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.8529/2026
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.            /2026
[@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.
8529/2026]
SHOYAB SHABBIR SHAIKH                 Appellant(s)
                  
       VERSUS
THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRA & ANR.      Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This  appeal  arises  from  an  order  dated
18.09.2025  in  Criminal  Appeal  No.100  of  2025
passed by the Bombay High Court rejecting the
regular bail prayer of the appellant in connection
with FIR being C.R. No.103 of 2015 registered at
Shirdi Police Station, District Ahmednagar.
1

--- Page 667 ---
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.8529/2026
4. At the outset, the learned counsel for the
appellant submits that the appellant has suffered
pending trial incarceration of well over 11 years.
It  is  stated  that  he  is  in  custody  since
23.05.2015.
5. The aforesaid fact is not disputed.
6. Having  regard  to  the  long  period  of
incarceration, pending trial, without expressing
any opinion on the merits of the appeal, we are of
the view that the appellant is entitled to be
released on bail at this stage.
7. The  appeal  is,  accordingly,  allowed.  The
order passed by the High Court rejecting the bail
prayer of the appellant is set aside.
8. The appellant shall be released on bail on
such terms and conditions as the trial court may
deem fit to impose in the facts and circumstances
of the case. One of the conditions of bail shall
be that appellant shall not threaten the witnesses
and in case he threatens the witnesses it shall be
open  for  the  Trial  Court  to  deal  with  an
application for cancellation of bail.
2

--- Page 668 ---
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.8529/2026
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEALS NO(S).         OF 2026
(@SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION(S) (C) NO(S).  18305-18306/2026)
BHAVESH SURESH KATARIA                            APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
KATARIA INSURANCE BROKERS PVT. LTD                RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
Issue notice to the respondent.
Learned counsel for the respondent - Caveator
accepts notice.
Leave granted.
We have heard learned counsel for the respective
parties.
We have perused the material on record.
During the course of submissions, it was brought
to our notice that the respondent did not contest
Interim  Application  No.1663  of  2021  filed  by  the
appellant herein in its suit viz., Commercial IP Suit
No.215 of 2021. However, the order dated 08.12.2025
granting  temporary  injunction  in  favour  of  the
appellant herein was assailed before the Division Bench
of the High Court on merits. The High Court has not
appreciated the fact that the respondent herein had not
1

--- Page 672 ---
contested the aforesaid application and had simply
filed the appeals even without seeking vacating of the
temporary injunction granted by the learned single
Judge on 08.12.2025. However, the Division Bench of the
High Court has set aside the order dated 08.12.2025 on
merits  without  appreciating  the  fact  that  the
respondent  herein  had  not  contested  the  Interim
Application filed by the appellant herein before the
learned Single Judge.
On that short ground alone, we set aside the
impugned judgment of the Division Bench of the High
Court and remand the matter before the learned Single
Judge in order to enable the respondent herein to file
an  application  seeking  vacating  of  the  order  of
temporary injunction granted by the learned single
Judge on 08.12.2025. 
Should an application for vacating the order of
temporary injunction be made by the respondent herein,
the same shall be considered by the learned Single
Judge as expeditiously as possible and in accordance
with law.
In view of the nature of the order passed above,
all contentions on both sides are left open to be
2

--- Page 673 ---
advanced  before  the  learned  Single  Judge  on  the
application to be filed by the respondent seeking
vacating of the interim order dated 08.12.2025.
Appeals  are  allowed  and  disposed  of  in  the
aforesaid terms.
No costs.
………………………………………………………, J
    (B.V. NAGARATHNA)
…………………………………………………………, J
  (UJJAL BHUYAN)
NEW DELHI
MAY 25, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.10786 OF 2014
DEEP P PATHAK & ORS.                                 APPELLANTS
                                VERSUS
STATE OF GUJARAT & ORS.                             RESPONDENTS
WITH
CIVIL APPEAL NO.10788 OF 2014
O R D E R
Learned counsel for respondent Nos.4 and 5 brought
to our notice the directions issued by the High Court
in paragraph 61 of the impugned order dated 23.08.2012.
For  ease  of  reference,  the  said  directions  are
extracted as under:
“61) In view of what is stated above, present
petitions are allowed and it is held that,
i)  The  settlement  arrived  at  between  the
Consortium of Self- financing Colleges and the
State Government in Special Civil Application No.
18234 of 2011 is held to be of no consequences as
it is in violation of the provisions of the Gujarat
Professional  Medical  Educational  Colleges  or
Institutions (Regulations of Admission and Fixation
of Fees) Act, 2007.
ii) The admission to the Postgraduate Dental
Courses shall be governed in the ratio of 75:25
which includes 15% NRI seats.
iii)  The  Gujarat  Professional  Medical
Educational Colleges or Institutions (Regulations
1

--- Page 677 ---
of Admission and Fixation of Fees) Act, 2007 the
Dental Council of India Revised MDS prevails over
Regulation, 2007 in connection with seat sharing
ratio and the seat sharing ratio in the Gujarat
Professional  Medical  Educational  Colleges  or
Institutions (Regulations of Admission and Fixation
of Fees) Act, 2007 governs the field.
iv) The respondents are directed to prepare
revised merit list on the basis of the observations
made in this judgment in connection with giving
admission in the PG Dental Courses in the colleges
affiliated to the respondent Gujarat University.
The respondents are directed to act accordingly as
per the observations made in this judgment.
v) The management quota is to be treated as 25%
of total seats which includes NRI quota in the
same.”
Learned  counsel  for  respondent  Nos.4  and  5
submitted that at this point of time the correctness or
otherwise of the aforesaid directions need not be gone
into  having  regard  to  the  lapse  of  time  and  the
students no longer being interested in the issues which
arise in these appeals.
In  the  circumstances,  it  was  submitted  that
appropriate orders may be made as the appeals have been
rendered infructuous.
We take note of the aforesaid submissions and we
find at this point of time, the consideration of the
correctness or otherwise of the aforesaid directions
2

--- Page 678 ---
issued by the High Court in the impugned order is
wholly  academic  insofar  as  these  petitioners  are
concerned.
In the circumstances, we dispose of these Civil
Appeals in the aforesaid terms.
………………………………………………………, J
    (B.V. NAGARATHNA)
…………………………………………………………, J
  (UJJAL BHUYAN)
NEW DELHI
MAY 27, 2026

2

--- Page 693 ---
                   Mr. Devashish Bharukha, Adv.
                   Ms. Shradha Deshmukh, Adv.
                   Ms. Ameyavikrama Thanvi, Adv.
                   
                   Mr. Raj Bahadur Yadav, AOR
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. We have heard learned counsel appearing for the parties. The
previous proceedings in the instant case would indicate that the
respondent-contemnor having assured this Court to keep the child
present has surreptitiously removed the child from the jurisdiction
of this country.
2. This court by order dated 12.08.2024 having formed an opinion
that  there  is  a  contempt  of  this  Court  constituted  by  the
respondent, for framing the charge had directed the respondent to
remain present on the next date of hearing and the matter stood
adjourned. This Court by order dated 15.05.2025 had noticed as
under:
“In spite of this Court’s order dated 21.10.2019 in
Crl.A. No.1607-1608/2019, order dated 27.07.2021 passed in
M.A. Nos. 254-255 of 2020 and orders dated 09.09.2024 and
13.11.2024 passed in these contempt petitions, where we
had directed the respondent – Manish Chokar to be present
in Court, and the fact that his Passport is in the custody
of the Registrar of this Court, the respondent has left
the  country  for  United  States,  without  taking  prior
permission from this Court and without proper travel
documents to our knowledge. 
We have heard Ms. Anagha S. Desai, learned counsel for
the petitioner as well as Mr. Tushar Mathur, learned
counsel appearing for the respondent – contemnor. 
In our considered view, this is clearly a Contempt of
Court, which, prima facie, the respondent has committed
twice, initially by violating the order of this court
dated 21.10.2019 and now by his action of having fled the
jurisdiction of this Court, despite orders of restraint.
The respondent is now practically an absconder and there
3

--- Page 694 ---
is no possibility for him to be present in the court while
the  charges  are  being  framed.  Therefore,  we  are
constrained to frame the following charges in the presence
of his counsel, against the respondent :-
“a)    You, Manish Chokar, are
hereby being charged for having
violated  the   orders  of  this
Court  and  having  been  absent
from  the  court  proceedings
despite knowing  the fact  that
you were to be present  in
Court on every proceeding. 
 b)  You  have  also  left  the
country without prior permission
from this Court, in spite of the
fact that your Passport is in
the custody of Registrar of this
Court.” 
The respondent shall file an affidavit explaining as
to why he should not be punished for Contempt of Court for
the charges framed above. 
Re-list the matter on 15.07.2025”
3. A perusal of the aforesaid order would indicate, this Court has
prima facie opined that respondent is an absconder and the charge
came to be framed, however, extended liberty to the contemnor to
file an affidavit explaining as to why he should not be punished. A
subsequent reply filed was not accepted by this Court by order
dated 15.07.2025.
4. This Court after having noticed that status of the contemnor has
changed to that of an accused, opined that requisition of the
accused/contemnor through the aegis of the Ministry of Home Affairs
by forwarding summons to be served on the accused, was warranted
and accordingly the Ministry of Home Affairs was directed to take
steps and file a report. Pursuant to same a report dated 11.11.2025
was placed on record by Union of India, which disclosed that
respondent-husband has been duly intimated about the pendency of
4

--- Page 695 ---
the present proceedings, thereafter, the matter has been adjourned
from time to time and orders have been passed for permitting the
complainant-wife to have video conference with the child. However,
today learned counsel for the petitioner-wife submits that such
video conferences have taken place only five times and thereafter
it did not continue.
5. Notwithstanding the pendency of the proceedings, we direct that
there shall be a video conference of the child and the mother every
week between 05:00 pm to 07:00 pm (time of New Jersey).
6. Learned counsel appearing for the respondent submits that on
account of an issue regarding the guardianship is pending before
the jurisdictional court of USA and it is ordered to be listed on
11.06.2026, the accused is not in a position to commit himself as
on date on which he would be appearing before this Court.
7. In the light of the said submission made, we direct Ms.
Aishwarya Bahti, learned ASG to take steps to cancel the passport
of the accused and we direct the respondent to be present before
this Court on the next date of hearing.
8. Registry is directed to furnish photocopy of the passport of the
respondent to the learned ASG, as the original passport is said to
have been deposited by respondent before this Court and same is
said to be in the custody of the Court.
9. List on 20.07.2026.
  
(RASHI GUPTA)                                   (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.        OF 2026
[arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 6173 OF 2026]
AMLESH SAH @ AMLESH                            APPELLANT
                         VERSUS
STATE OF BIHAR                                RESPONDENT
 
O R D E R 
1.   Leave granted.
2. By the impugned judgment and order dated 11 th November,
2025,  the  High  Court  of  Judicature  at  Patna  has  rejected  the
appellant’s prayer for bail in anticipation of arrest.  
3. Appellant figures as an accused in FIR No. 013 dated 17 th
January,  2025  registered  with  Police  Station  Majorganj,  District-
Sitamarhi. It is alleged in the FIR that the appellant has committed
offence(s) punishable under Section(s) 126(2), 329(3), 115(2), 109,
352, 351(2), and 3(5) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023.
4. We have heard learned counsel appearing for the appellant
as well as the respondent.

--- Page 697 ---
2
5. It is not in dispute that the appellant, in terms of an earlier
order  dated  1 st April,  2026,  has  joined  the  investigation  and
cooperated with the investigating officer. That apart, from the FIR,
we do not find any specific role  having been attributed to the
appellant.
6. Having regard to the facts and circumstances of the present
case and in view of the fact that no medical report is brought on
record in the counter affidavit of the respondent-State, we are of
the considered opinion that custodial interrogation of the appellant
is  not  necessary  and  that  there  is  sufficient  ground  for  the
appellant to be admitted to an order for grant of bail in anticipation
of arrest.
7. Accordingly, the impugned judgment and order stands set
aside.
8. It is directed that in the event of the appellant being arrested
in connection with proceedings arising out of the aforesaid FIR, he
shall be released on bail by the arresting/investigating officer/trial
court on terms and conditions to be fixed by the trial court.
9. It is made clear that if the appellant breaches any of the
terms  and conditions  imposed by  it, the  trial court shall be  at
liberty to cancel the bail of the appellant.
10. Needless  to  observe,  the  appellant  shall  not,  directly  or

--- Page 698 ---
3
indirectly, by making inducement, threat or promise, dissuade any
person acquainted with the facts of the case from disclosing such
facts to any police officer or to the court.
11. Additionally,  should  the  investigating  officer  require  the
appellant to face further interrogation, the latter will be under an
obligation to respond failing which any omission in this behalf may
be brought to the notice of the trial court for cancellation of bail.  
12. We clarify that the observations made in this order and grant
of bail will not be treated as findings on the merits of the case.
13. The appeal is allowed on the above terms.
14.  Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
………………..……………………...J.
[DIPANKAR DATTA]  
 ………...……………………………..J.
[SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA]
New Delhi;
May 29, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.248/2025
RITESH KAPAHI                          APPELLANT
VERSUS
STATE OF HARYANA & ANR.                          RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. In  course  of  hearing  of  this  transfer  petition,  the  petitioner-
husband  ( Ritesh  Kapahi )  and  the  respondent  no.2-wife  (Gunjan
Kapahi) were referred to the Delhi High Court Mediation Centre.
2. The  process  of  mediation  has  produced  positive  results.  The
parties have agreed to resolve the inter se disputes and differences
amicably. It has also been decided by them to apply before this Court
seeking dissolution of their marriage by mutual consent under Article
142 of the Constitution of India.
3. Petitioner  has  filed  an  application  under  Article  142  of  the
Constitution  of  India,  being  I.A.  No.160173/2026,  i n  view  of  such
settlement. The prayers in the application under Article 142 read as
follows:
“(a) Allow the present application;
(b) grant  decree  of  divorce  by  dissolving  the  marriage
between the petitioner and respondent no.2 under Article
142 of the Constitution of India,1950;
(c) to quash/dispose of all civil/criminal cases between the
parties listed in above para 4 of the present application;
(d) Pass such other and further orders as this Hon’ble Court
may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of

--- Page 702 ---
the case and in the interest of justice.”
 
4. During the course of the dispute, the parties filed various cases
against each other. List of cases pending by and between the parties
before various courts is as under:
“i). Case  bearing  no.CHI  No.4555/2023,  titled  as
"State of Haryana Vs. Ritesh" pending before the Ld. Court of
Judicial Magistrate-1st Class, Faridabad District Court, Haryana.
ii). Case bearing No.MC/8/2023 titled as 'Gunjan Chabra v.
Ritesh Kapahi & Ors. pending adjudication before court of Ld.
JM1C-02-MC, West District, Tis Hazari Court, Delhi.
iii). Case bearing no.MC/197/2023, titled as 'Neera Kapahi v.
Gunjan Kapahi, pending adjudication before court of Ld. JM1C-02-
MC, West District, Tis Hazari Court, Delhi.
iv). T wo Applications u/s 31 of the PWDV Act, 2005 filed by
Smt. Neera Kapahi pending adjudication before court of Ld. JM1C-
02-MC, West District, Tis Hazari Court, Delhi.
v). Case bearing no. HMA/862/2023, titled as Ritesh Kapahi
v.  Gunjan  Kapahi  pending  adjudication  before  court  of  Ld.
Principal  Judge  Family  Court,  West  District,  Tis  Hazari  Court,
Delhi.”
5. We have interacted with the petitioner and the respondent no.2,
who  are  present  in  Court.  They  have  separately  submitted,  while
responding to our query, that they have agreed to part ways without
there being any threat, force, coercion or  undue influence and that
this Court may dissolve the marital tie by passing an appropriate
order,  as  prayed  in  the  application  under  Article  142  of  the
Constitution. 
6. The parties having amicably resolved the  inter se  disputes and
differences,  the  terms  of  settlement  which  are  contained  in  the
settlement forming part of the application under Article 142 of the
Constitution shall form part of this order.

--- Page 703 ---
7. Parties agree that payment in terms of the settlement has been
made over to the respondent by the petitioner inclusive of the sum
towards a cot (palank). However,  a sum of Rs.23,00,000/- (Rupees
twenty-three lakh) only is lying with the Registry of this Court which
needs to be released in favour of the respondent no.2.
8. Accordingly, in exercise of power conferred on us by Article 142
of the Constitution  of India , we dissolve the marriage between the
parties by mutual consent by granting an order in terms of prayer (b)
of the application under Article 142 of the Constitution and also allow
the same. There shall be an order in terms of prayer (c) too. Decree
be drawn up, accordingly.
9. Respondent no.2 shall be entitled to reside in the accommodation
where she is presently residing for a period of a fortnight from date,
whereafter she shall vacate and deliver peaceful possession of such
accommodation to the petitioner together with the keys thereof and
the wardrobe, if any.
10. In  the  event,  the  respondent  no.2  does  not  deliver  vacant
possession and also does not hand over the keys after a fortnight, as
agreed to by her before us, the petitioner shall be at liberty to replace
the lock with his own lock and bring such breach to our notice for
appropriate order.
11. Petitioner shall be entitled to retain the television set whereas
the  respondent  no.2  shall  be  entitled  to  retain  possession  of  the
scooter (Activa). He  shall sign the  necessary documents/forms for
transfer of ownership of the scooter in favour of the respondent no.2.
12. As a result of this order, nothing survives for decision on the
transfer petition. The transfer petition stands disposed of.

--- Page 704 ---
13. As indicated in the office report, a sum of Rs.23,00,000/- (Rupees
twenty-three lakh) only is lying with the Registry of this Court. The
same shall be released together with accrued interest in favour of the
respondent no.2.
14. The  relevant  courts/police  stations  shall  mark  closure  of  the
cases/proceedings/complaints that are pending/lodged before it, once
any party approaches it with a copy of this order.
15. We appreciate the efforts of the mediator in assisting the parties
to resolve their disputes amicably.
16. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
............................................J.
(DIPANKAR DATTA)
............................................J.
(SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA)
New Delhi;
May 29, 2026.

--- Page 714 ---
M.A.No.  530/2022 
in W.P.(C) No. 56/2004
2. The Miscellaneous Application is disposed of in terms of the
signed reportable judgment.
3. The relevant part of the signed reportable judgment is as
under:-
“That all the States/UTs notify “recognised welfare
institutions or organisations” in accordance with the
explanation to Section 15(6A) ITPA.
That all States/UTs prepare a State-wide list of social
welfare workers, with sufficient representation from all
districts, who may be eligible to form a part of the
non-official advisory body under Section 13(3)(b) ITPA. 
That all States/UTs designate the ADGP level police
officer heading the Anti-Trafficking Bureau in their
respective States/UTs as the Police Nodal Officers for
the purpose of performing the functions as delineated by
us in the Victim Protection Plan. 
That  all  States/UTs  shall  designate  the  Secretary,
Department of Women and Child Development in their
respective States/UTs as the Government Nodal Officers
for  the  purpose  of  performing  the  functions  as
delineated by us in the Victim Protection Plan.
It shall be the responsibility of the respondent no. 1
to  ensure  that  the  aforesaid  directions  are  duly
complied with within a period of three months from the
date of this judgment. 
For  the  purpose  of  reporting  compliance  with  the
additional directions mentioned in Paragraph No. 456,
the  Registry  shall  notify  this  matter  once  again
sometime in September, 2026.
Tackling human trafficking for CSE, with its hidden
nature and multifarious forms, is indeed a very daunting
task. However, there is recognition from all corners
that it is an evil which needs urgent and consistent
attention. Various measures have been undertaken by the
multitude of stakeholders involved. However, throughout
this judgment, we have noted that these measures have
4

--- Page 715 ---
M.A.No.  530/2022 
in W.P.(C) No. 56/2004
not yielded concrete positive results. Thus, there is no
hiding away from the fact that much more needs to be
done.  By  formulating  a  victim  protection  plan,  we
believe we have taken a major step in that direction. We
hope this effort will have a domino effect, leading to
much more sustained and coordinated action from all
stakeholders.  
We are aware that a single plan will not be the answer
to  all  the  trials  and  tribulations  victims  face.
However, our effort here should reflect more than just
the  formulation  of  a  plan.  It  should  reflect  an
endeavour  to  give  the  victim  centre  stage  and  to
prioritise their needs. It should reflect an attempt to
view victims not as passive beings but as agents in need
of empowerment. Lastly, but most importantly, it should
reflect a commitment to the victims that this Court will
accord them the dignity that was always theirs.
We direct the Registry to send one copy of the judgment
to:
a. All the High Courts;
b. Home Secretary, Ministry of Home Affairs, 
Union of India
c. Principal Secretary, Ministry of Women and 
Child Development, Union of India.  
(CHANDRESH)                                     (POOJA SHARMA)

1 
 
  IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
CIVIL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026  
(Arising out of SLP (C) No.16566 of 2026) 
 
 
MANI MANGLA & ORS.                       APPELLANT(s) 
 
                       VERSUS 
 
ABHYUDAYA PALIWAL @ BANU & ORS.         RESPONDENT(s) 
 
 
 O R D E R 
 
Leave granted. 
2. This appeal is directed against the final judgment 
and order dated 09.09.2025 passed by the High Court of 
Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh in FAO No. 3843/2025, 
which upheld the award of the Motor Accident Claims 
Tribunal (MACT), Palwal, dated 27.08.2024, an d 
dismissed the appellants' plea for enhancement of 
compensation. 

--- Page 717 ---
2 
 
3. The brief facts are that on 21.07.2019, the 
deceased, Arun Kumar Mangla, aged 57 years, was fatally 
injured when a speeding white Creta car (Registration 
No. HR-30S-0716) struck his scooter while he was en 
route to his factory. An FIR (No. 0462/2019) was 
registered at Police Station Camp Palwal under Sections 
279, 304A, 336, and 337 of the Indian Penal Code. 
4. The appellants, being the legal heirs of the 
deceased, challenged the method of income assessment 
used by the lower courts. The Motor Accident Claims 
Tribunal (MACT), Palwal, while adjudicating the claim, 
declined to assess the deceased’s income based on his 
Income Tax Returns, which reflected a gross total 
income of Rs. 6,97,287/ - per annum. Instead, the 
Tribunal notionally reduced his monthly income to Rs. 
30,000/-, observing that since his son was assisting 
in the business and continued to run it after his 
father's death, the entire income could not be 
attributed solely to the deceased. Consequently, the 
Tribunal awarded a to tal compensation of Rs. 
27,57,700/- along with interest. 

--- Page 718 ---
3 
 
5. In the impugned judgment, the High Court affirmed 
the Tribunal’s findings, holding that the overall 
compensation awarded was just and proper. The High 
Court upheld the hypothetical approach of assessing 
only a notional managerial contribution of the 
deceased, reasoning that the continuation of the sole 
proprietorship business by the son justified the 
reduced assessment of income. 
6. The appellants have challenged these findings, 
contending that the lower courts erred in law by 
arbitrarily splitting the sole proprietor's income and 
assessing a notional figure despite established ITR 
records. It is further contended that the mere 
inheritance or continuation of a business by a legal 
heir does not negate the loss of dependency, which must 
be assessed with reference to the deceased's earning 
capacity on the date of the accident. 
7. Having heard learned counsel for the parties and 
upon perusal of the material on record, including the 
Income Tax Returns and financial documents of the 
deceased, we are of the considered view that the 

--- Page 719 ---
4 
 
assessment of compensation warrants enhancement in 
order to meet the ends of justice. Interest of justice 
would be best served by further enhancing the 
compensation. Taking a holistic view of the matter, we 
deem it appropriate to award an additional lump s um 
amount. 
8. Respondent No. 3 – HDFC Ergo General Insurance 
Company Limited is hereby directed to pay a lump sum 
amount of Rs. 10,00,000/- (Rupees Ten Lakh only) to the 
appellants. 
9. The aforementioned amount of Rs. 10,00,000/- shall 
be paid in addition to the compensation that already 
stands awarded by the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal, 
Palwal, and affirmed by the High Court. 
10. The Respondent Insurance Company shall directly 
deposit the said lump sum amount into the bank accounts 
of the claimants/appellants in the same proportion as 
directed in the original award dated 27.08.2024, within 
a period of six weeks from the date of this order. The 
appellants shall furnish the requisite bank account 

--- Page 720 ---
5 
 
particulars to learned counsel for the respondents 
without delay. 
11. In the event of default, the appellant(s) shall be 
entitled to interest on the outstanding amount at the 
rate awarded by the Labour Court/Tribunal. 
12. The appeal is disposed of in the above terms. 
13. Pending applications, including I.A. No. 
115620/2026 (Exemption from filing O.T.) and I.A. No. 
115619/2026 (Permission to file additional documents), 
shall stand disposed of. 
 
    ..........................J. 
  (SANJAY KAROL) 
  
 
                   
............................J. 
     (AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH)

MAY 25, 2026

1. For  alleged  non-compliance  of  the  Order  dated
08.01.2025, the present Contempt Petition has been filed.

said that the attendance register of certain period is
not available, therefore, the State is not agreeable to
make payment for the said period. In that regard, liberty

--- Page 723 ---
3
had  already  been  granted  in  the  main  order  dated
08.01.2025, for taking recourse.
2. After hearing learned counsel for the parties, we are
of the view that, in respect to the disputed period, the
amount may be withheld and appropriate recourse before
the High Court may be taken. However, for the remaining
period, the amount shall be paid to the petitioner in
terms of the order passed by the University and agreed by
the State Government. Accordingly, the Contempt Petition
is disposed of with liberty to the parties to approach
the High Court by filing an appropriate petition.
3. Let the said exercise be completed within six weeks
from today and compliance be reported to the Registry of
this Court.
4. List on 18.08.2026.

terms of para 9 of the said affidavit. The said objection
is taken on record.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEALS NO(S).         OF 2026
(@SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION(S) (C) NO(S).  18305-18306/2026)
BHAVESH SURESH KATARIA                            APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
KATARIA INSURANCE BROKERS PVT. LTD                RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
Issue notice to the respondent.
Learned counsel for the respondent - Caveator
accepts notice.
Leave granted.
We have heard learned counsel for the respective
parties.
We have perused the material on record.
During the course of submissions, it was brought
to our notice that the respondent did not contest
Interim  Application  No.1663  of  2021  filed  by  the
appellant herein in its suit viz., Commercial IP Suit
No.215 of 2021. However, the order dated 08.12.2025
granting  temporary  injunction  in  favour  of  the
appellant herein was assailed before the Division Bench
of the High Court on merits. The High Court has not
appreciated the fact that the respondent herein had not
1

--- Page 727 ---
contested the aforesaid application and had simply
filed the appeals even without seeking vacating of the
temporary injunction granted by the learned single
Judge on 08.12.2025. However, the Division Bench of the
High Court has set aside the order dated 08.12.2025 on
merits  without  appreciating  the  fact  that  the
respondent  herein  had  not  contested  the  Interim
Application filed by the appellant herein before the
learned Single Judge.
On that short ground alone, we set aside the
impugned judgment of the Division Bench of the High
Court and remand the matter before the learned Single
Judge in order to enable the respondent herein to file
an  application  seeking  vacating  of  the  order  of
temporary injunction granted by the learned single
Judge on 08.12.2025. 
Should an application for vacating the order of
temporary injunction be made by the respondent herein,
the same shall be considered by the learned Single
Judge as expeditiously as possible and in accordance
with law.
In view of the nature of the order passed above,
all contentions on both sides are left open to be
2

--- Page 728 ---
advanced  before  the  learned  Single  Judge  on  the
application to be filed by the respondent seeking
vacating of the interim order dated 08.12.2025.
Appeals  are  allowed  and  disposed  of  in  the
aforesaid terms.
No costs.

    (B.V. NAGARATHNA)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.10786 OF 2014
DEEP P PATHAK & ORS.                                 APPELLANTS
                                VERSUS
STATE OF GUJARAT & ORS.                             RESPONDENTS
WITH
CIVIL APPEAL NO.10788 OF 2014
O R D E R
Learned counsel for respondent Nos.4 and 5 brought
to our notice the directions issued by the High Court
in paragraph 61 of the impugned order dated 23.08.2012.
For  ease  of  reference,  the  said  directions  are
extracted as under:
“61) In view of what is stated above, present
petitions are allowed and it is held that,
i)  The  settlement  arrived  at  between  the
Consortium of Self- financing Colleges and the
State Government in Special Civil Application No.
18234 of 2011 is held to be of no consequences as
it is in violation of the provisions of the Gujarat
Professional  Medical  Educational  Colleges  or
Institutions (Regulations of Admission and Fixation
of Fees) Act, 2007.
ii) The admission to the Postgraduate Dental
Courses shall be governed in the ratio of 75:25
which includes 15% NRI seats.
iii)  The  Gujarat  Professional  Medical
Educational Colleges or Institutions (Regulations
1

--- Page 732 ---
of Admission and Fixation of Fees) Act, 2007 the
Dental Council of India Revised MDS prevails over
Regulation, 2007 in connection with seat sharing
ratio and the seat sharing ratio in the Gujarat
Professional  Medical  Educational  Colleges  or
Institutions (Regulations of Admission and Fixation
of Fees) Act, 2007 governs the field.
iv) The respondents are directed to prepare
revised merit list on the basis of the observations
made in this judgment in connection with giving
admission in the PG Dental Courses in the colleges
affiliated to the respondent Gujarat University.
The respondents are directed to act accordingly as
per the observations made in this judgment.
v) The management quota is to be treated as 25%
of total seats which includes NRI quota in the
same.”
Learned  counsel  for  respondent  Nos.4  and  5
submitted that at this point of time the correctness or
otherwise of the aforesaid directions need not be gone
into  having  regard  to  the  lapse  of  time  and  the
students no longer being interested in the issues which
arise in these appeals.
In  the  circumstances,  it  was  submitted  that
appropriate orders may be made as the appeals have been
rendered infructuous.
We take note of the aforesaid submissions and we
find at this point of time, the consideration of the
correctness or otherwise of the aforesaid directions
2

--- Page 733 ---
issued by the High Court in the impugned order is
wholly  academic  insofar  as  these  petitioners  are
concerned.
In the circumstances, we dispose of these Civil
Appeals in the aforesaid terms.
………………………………………………………, J
    (B.V. NAGARATHNA)

  (UJJAL BHUYAN)

2

--- Page 748 ---
                   Mr. Devashish Bharukha, Adv.
                   Ms. Shradha Deshmukh, Adv.
                   Ms. Ameyavikrama Thanvi, Adv.
                   
                   Mr. Raj Bahadur Yadav, AOR
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. We have heard learned counsel appearing for the parties. The
previous proceedings in the instant case would indicate that the
respondent-contemnor having assured this Court to keep the child
present has surreptitiously removed the child from the jurisdiction
of this country.
2. This court by order dated 12.08.2024 having formed an opinion
that  there  is  a  contempt  of  this  Court  constituted  by  the
respondent, for framing the charge had directed the respondent to
remain present on the next date of hearing and the matter stood
adjourned. This Court by order dated 15.05.2025 had noticed as
under:
“In spite of this Court’s order dated 21.10.2019 in
Crl.A. No.1607-1608/2019, order dated 27.07.2021 passed in
M.A. Nos. 254-255 of 2020 and orders dated 09.09.2024 and
13.11.2024 passed in these contempt petitions, where we
had directed the respondent – Manish Chokar to be present
in Court, and the fact that his Passport is in the custody
of the Registrar of this Court, the respondent has left
the  country  for  United  States,  without  taking  prior
permission from this Court and without proper travel
documents to our knowledge. 
We have heard Ms. Anagha S. Desai, learned counsel for
the petitioner as well as Mr. Tushar Mathur, learned
counsel appearing for the respondent – contemnor. 
In our considered view, this is clearly a Contempt of
Court, which, prima facie, the respondent has committed
twice, initially by violating the order of this court
dated 21.10.2019 and now by his action of having fled the
jurisdiction of this Court, despite orders of restraint.
The respondent is now practically an absconder and there
3

--- Page 749 ---
is no possibility for him to be present in the court while
the  charges  are  being  framed.  Therefore,  we  are
constrained to frame the following charges in the presence
of his counsel, against the respondent :-
“a)    You, Manish Chokar, are
hereby being charged for having
violated  the   orders  of  this
Court  and  having  been  absent
from  the  court  proceedings
despite knowing  the fact  that
you were to be present  in
Court on every proceeding. 
 b)  You  have  also  left  the
country without prior permission
from this Court, in spite of the
fact that your Passport is in
the custody of Registrar of this
Court.” 
The respondent shall file an affidavit explaining as
to why he should not be punished for Contempt of Court for
the charges framed above. 
Re-list the matter on 15.07.2025”
3. A perusal of the aforesaid order would indicate, this Court has
prima facie opined that respondent is an absconder and the charge
came to be framed, however, extended liberty to the contemnor to
file an affidavit explaining as to why he should not be punished. A
subsequent reply filed was not accepted by this Court by order
dated 15.07.2025.
4. This Court after having noticed that status of the contemnor has
changed to that of an accused, opined that requisition of the
accused/contemnor through the aegis of the Ministry of Home Affairs
by forwarding summons to be served on the accused, was warranted
and accordingly the Ministry of Home Affairs was directed to take
steps and file a report. Pursuant to same a report dated 11.11.2025
was placed on record by Union of India, which disclosed that
respondent-husband has been duly intimated about the pendency of
4

--- Page 750 ---
the present proceedings, thereafter, the matter has been adjourned
from time to time and orders have been passed for permitting the
complainant-wife to have video conference with the child. However,
today learned counsel for the petitioner-wife submits that such
video conferences have taken place only five times and thereafter
it did not continue.
5. Notwithstanding the pendency of the proceedings, we direct that
there shall be a video conference of the child and the mother every
week between 05:00 pm to 07:00 pm (time of New Jersey).
6. Learned counsel appearing for the respondent submits that on
account of an issue regarding the guardianship is pending before
the jurisdictional court of USA and it is ordered to be listed on
11.06.2026, the accused is not in a position to commit himself as
on date on which he would be appearing before this Court.
7. In the light of the said submission made, we direct Ms.
Aishwarya Bahti, learned ASG to take steps to cancel the passport
of the accused and we direct the respondent to be present before
this Court on the next date of hearing.
8. Registry is directed to furnish photocopy of the passport of the
respondent to the learned ASG, as the original passport is said to
have been deposited by respondent before this Court and same is
said to be in the custody of the Court.
9. List on 20.07.2026.
  
(RASHI GUPTA)                                   (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
5

--- Page 751 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.        OF 2026
[arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 6173 OF 2026]
AMLESH SAH @ AMLESH                            APPELLANT
                         VERSUS
STATE OF BIHAR                                RESPONDENT
 
O R D E R 
1.   Leave granted.
2. By the impugned judgment and order dated 11 th November,
2025,  the  High  Court  of  Judicature  at  Patna  has  rejected  the
appellant’s prayer for bail in anticipation of arrest.  
3. Appellant figures as an accused in FIR No. 013 dated 17 th
January,  2025  registered  with  Police  Station  Majorganj,  District-
Sitamarhi. It is alleged in the FIR that the appellant has committed
offence(s) punishable under Section(s) 126(2), 329(3), 115(2), 109,
352, 351(2), and 3(5) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023.
4. We have heard learned counsel appearing for the appellant
as well as the respondent.

--- Page 752 ---
2
5. It is not in dispute that the appellant, in terms of an earlier
order  dated  1 st April,  2026,  has  joined  the  investigation  and
cooperated with the investigating officer. That apart, from the FIR,
we do not find any specific role  having been attributed to the
appellant.
6. Having regard to the facts and circumstances of the present
case and in view of the fact that no medical report is brought on
record in the counter affidavit of the respondent-State, we are of
the considered opinion that custodial interrogation of the appellant
is  not  necessary  and  that  there  is  sufficient  ground  for  the
appellant to be admitted to an order for grant of bail in anticipation
of arrest.
7. Accordingly, the impugned judgment and order stands set
aside.
8. It is directed that in the event of the appellant being arrested
in connection with proceedings arising out of the aforesaid FIR, he
shall be released on bail by the arresting/investigating officer/trial
court on terms and conditions to be fixed by the trial court.
9. It is made clear that if the appellant breaches any of the
terms  and conditions  imposed by  it, the  trial court shall be  at
liberty to cancel the bail of the appellant.
10. Needless  to  observe,  the  appellant  shall  not,  directly  or

--- Page 753 ---
3
indirectly, by making inducement, threat or promise, dissuade any
person acquainted with the facts of the case from disclosing such
facts to any police officer or to the court.
11. Additionally,  should  the  investigating  officer  require  the
appellant to face further interrogation, the latter will be under an
obligation to respond failing which any omission in this behalf may
be brought to the notice of the trial court for cancellation of bail.  
12. We clarify that the observations made in this order and grant
of bail will not be treated as findings on the merits of the case.
13. The appeal is allowed on the above terms.
14.  Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
………………..……………………...J.
[DIPANKAR DATTA]  
 ………...……………………………..J.
[SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA]
New Delhi;
May 29, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.248/2025
RITESH KAPAHI                          APPELLANT
VERSUS
STATE OF HARYANA & ANR.                          RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. In  course  of  hearing  of  this  transfer  petition,  the  petitioner-
husband  ( Ritesh  Kapahi )  and  the  respondent  no.2-wife  (Gunjan
Kapahi) were referred to the Delhi High Court Mediation Centre.
2. The  process  of  mediation  has  produced  positive  results.  The
parties have agreed to resolve the inter se disputes and differences
amicably. It has also been decided by them to apply before this Court
seeking dissolution of their marriage by mutual consent under Article
142 of the Constitution of India.
3. Petitioner  has  filed  an  application  under  Article  142  of  the
Constitution  of  India,  being  I.A.  No.160173/2026,  i n  view  of  such
settlement. The prayers in the application under Article 142 read as
follows:
“(a) Allow the present application;
(b) grant  decree  of  divorce  by  dissolving  the  marriage
between the petitioner and respondent no.2 under Article
142 of the Constitution of India,1950;
(c) to quash/dispose of all civil/criminal cases between the
parties listed in above para 4 of the present application;
(d) Pass such other and further orders as this Hon’ble Court
may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of

--- Page 757 ---
the case and in the interest of justice.”
 
4. During the course of the dispute, the parties filed various cases
against each other. List of cases pending by and between the parties
before various courts is as under:
“i). Case  bearing  no.CHI  No.4555/2023,  titled  as
"State of Haryana Vs. Ritesh" pending before the Ld. Court of
Judicial Magistrate-1st Class, Faridabad District Court, Haryana.
ii). Case bearing No.MC/8/2023 titled as 'Gunjan Chabra v.
Ritesh Kapahi & Ors. pending adjudication before court of Ld.
JM1C-02-MC, West District, Tis Hazari Court, Delhi.
iii). Case bearing no.MC/197/2023, titled as 'Neera Kapahi v.
Gunjan Kapahi, pending adjudication before court of Ld. JM1C-02-
MC, West District, Tis Hazari Court, Delhi.
iv). T wo Applications u/s 31 of the PWDV Act, 2005 filed by
Smt. Neera Kapahi pending adjudication before court of Ld. JM1C-
02-MC, West District, Tis Hazari Court, Delhi.
v). Case bearing no. HMA/862/2023, titled as Ritesh Kapahi
v.  Gunjan  Kapahi  pending  adjudication  before  court  of  Ld.
Principal  Judge  Family  Court,  West  District,  Tis  Hazari  Court,
Delhi.”
5. We have interacted with the petitioner and the respondent no.2,
who  are  present  in  Court.  They  have  separately  submitted,  while
responding to our query, that they have agreed to part ways without
there being any threat, force, coercion or  undue influence and that
this Court may dissolve the marital tie by passing an appropriate
order,  as  prayed  in  the  application  under  Article  142  of  the
Constitution. 
6. The parties having amicably resolved the  inter se  disputes and
differences,  the  terms  of  settlement  which  are  contained  in  the
settlement forming part of the application under Article 142 of the
Constitution shall form part of this order.

--- Page 758 ---
7. Parties agree that payment in terms of the settlement has been
made over to the respondent by the petitioner inclusive of the sum
towards a cot (palank). However,  a sum of Rs.23,00,000/- (Rupees
twenty-three lakh) only is lying with the Registry of this Court which
needs to be released in favour of the respondent no.2.
8. Accordingly, in exercise of power conferred on us by Article 142
of the Constitution  of India , we dissolve the marriage between the
parties by mutual consent by granting an order in terms of prayer (b)
of the application under Article 142 of the Constitution and also allow
the same. There shall be an order in terms of prayer (c) too. Decree
be drawn up, accordingly.
9. Respondent no.2 shall be entitled to reside in the accommodation
where she is presently residing for a period of a fortnight from date,
whereafter she shall vacate and deliver peaceful possession of such
accommodation to the petitioner together with the keys thereof and
the wardrobe, if any.
10. In  the  event,  the  respondent  no.2  does  not  deliver  vacant
possession and also does not hand over the keys after a fortnight, as
agreed to by her before us, the petitioner shall be at liberty to replace
the lock with his own lock and bring such breach to our notice for
appropriate order.
11. Petitioner shall be entitled to retain the television set whereas
the  respondent  no.2  shall  be  entitled  to  retain  possession  of  the
scooter (Activa). He  shall sign the  necessary documents/forms for
transfer of ownership of the scooter in favour of the respondent no.2.
12. As a result of this order, nothing survives for decision on the
transfer petition. The transfer petition stands disposed of.

--- Page 759 ---
13. As indicated in the office report, a sum of Rs.23,00,000/- (Rupees
twenty-three lakh) only is lying with the Registry of this Court. The
same shall be released together with accrued interest in favour of the
respondent no.2.
14. The  relevant  courts/police  stations  shall  mark  closure  of  the
cases/proceedings/complaints that are pending/lodged before it, once
any party approaches it with a copy of this order.
15. We appreciate the efforts of the mediator in assisting the parties
to resolve their disputes amicably.
16. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
............................................J.
(DIPANKAR DATTA)
............................................J.
(SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA)
New Delhi;
May 29, 2026.
