M.A.No.  530/2022 
in W.P.(C) No. 56/2004
2. The Miscellaneous Application is disposed of in terms of the
signed reportable judgment.
3. The relevant part of the signed reportable judgment is as
under:-
“That all the States/UTs notify “recognised welfare
institutions or organisations” in accordance with the
explanation to Section 15(6A) ITPA.
That all States/UTs prepare a State-wide list of social
welfare workers, with sufficient representation from all
districts, who may be eligible to form a part of the
non-official advisory body under Section 13(3)(b) ITPA. 
That all States/UTs designate the ADGP level police
officer heading the Anti-Trafficking Bureau in their
respective States/UTs as the Police Nodal Officers for
the purpose of performing the functions as delineated by
us in the Victim Protection Plan. 
That  all  States/UTs  shall  designate  the  Secretary,
Department of Women and Child Development in their
respective States/UTs as the Government Nodal Officers
for  the  purpose  of  performing  the  functions  as
delineated by us in the Victim Protection Plan.
It shall be the responsibility of the respondent no. 1
to  ensure  that  the  aforesaid  directions  are  duly
complied with within a period of three months from the
date of this judgment. 
For  the  purpose  of  reporting  compliance  with  the
additional directions mentioned in Paragraph No. 456,
the  Registry  shall  notify  this  matter  once  again
sometime in September, 2026.
Tackling human trafficking for CSE, with its hidden
nature and multifarious forms, is indeed a very daunting
task. However, there is recognition from all corners
that it is an evil which needs urgent and consistent
attention. Various measures have been undertaken by the
multitude of stakeholders involved. However, throughout
this judgment, we have noted that these measures have
4

--- Page 765 ---
M.A.No.  530/2022 
in W.P.(C) No. 56/2004
not yielded concrete positive results. Thus, there is no
hiding away from the fact that much more needs to be
done.  By  formulating  a  victim  protection  plan,  we
believe we have taken a major step in that direction. We
hope this effort will have a domino effect, leading to
much more sustained and coordinated action from all
stakeholders.  
We are aware that a single plan will not be the answer
to  all  the  trials  and  tribulations  victims  face.
However, our effort here should reflect more than just
the  formulation  of  a  plan.  It  should  reflect  an
endeavour  to  give  the  victim  centre  stage  and  to
prioritise their needs. It should reflect an attempt to
view victims not as passive beings but as agents in need
of empowerment. Lastly, but most importantly, it should
reflect a commitment to the victims that this Court will
accord them the dignity that was always theirs.
We direct the Registry to send one copy of the judgment
to:
a. All the High Courts;
b. Home Secretary, Ministry of Home Affairs, 
Union of India
c. Principal Secretary, Ministry of Women and 
Child Development, Union of India.  
(CHANDRESH)                                     (POOJA SHARMA)

CONTEMPT PETITION(CIVIL)Diary No(s).45647/2025(ITEM NO.54)
1. For  alleged  non-compliance  of  the  Order  dated
08.01.2025, the present Contempt Petition has been filed.
In the counter affidavit filed by the State, it has been
said that the attendance register of certain period is
not available, therefore, the State is not agreeable to
make payment for the said period. In that regard, liberty

--- Page 768 ---
3
had  already  been  granted  in  the  main  order  dated
08.01.2025, for taking recourse.
2. After hearing learned counsel for the parties, we are
of the view that, in respect to the disputed period, the
amount may be withheld and appropriate recourse before
the High Court may be taken. However, for the remaining
period, the amount shall be paid to the petitioner in
terms of the order passed by the University and agreed by
the State Government. Accordingly, the Contempt Petition
is disposed of with liberty to the parties to approach
the High Court by filing an appropriate petition.
3. Let the said exercise be completed within six weeks
from today and compliance be reported to the Registry of
this Court.
4. List on 18.08.2026.
5. Learned counsel for the petitioner objected to the
terms of para 9 of the said affidavit. The said objection
is taken on record.

NOS.54.1 & 54.2)

--- Page 771 ---
1 
 
  IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
CIVIL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026  
(Arising out of SLP (C) No.16566 of 2026) 
 
 
MANI MANGLA & ORS.                       APPELLANT(s) 
 
                       VERSUS 
 
ABHYUDAYA PALIWAL @ BANU & ORS.         RESPONDENT(s) 
 
 
 O R D E R 
 
Leave granted. 
2. This appeal is directed against the final judgment 
and order dated 09.09.2025 passed by the High Court of 
Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh in FAO No. 3843/2025, 
which upheld the award of the Motor Accident Claims 
Tribunal (MACT), Palwal, dated 27.08.2024, an d 
dismissed the appellants' plea for enhancement of 
compensation. 

--- Page 772 ---
2 
 
3. The brief facts are that on 21.07.2019, the 
deceased, Arun Kumar Mangla, aged 57 years, was fatally 
injured when a speeding white Creta car (Registration 
No. HR-30S-0716) struck his scooter while he was en 
route to his factory. An FIR (No. 0462/2019) was 
registered at Police Station Camp Palwal under Sections 
279, 304A, 336, and 337 of the Indian Penal Code. 
4. The appellants, being the legal heirs of the 
deceased, challenged the method of income assessment 
used by the lower courts. The Motor Accident Claims 
Tribunal (MACT), Palwal, while adjudicating the claim, 
declined to assess the deceased’s income based on his 
Income Tax Returns, which reflected a gross total 
income of Rs. 6,97,287/ - per annum. Instead, the 
Tribunal notionally reduced his monthly income to Rs. 
30,000/-, observing that since his son was assisting 
in the business and continued to run it after his 
father's death, the entire income could not be 
attributed solely to the deceased. Consequently, the 
Tribunal awarded a to tal compensation of Rs. 
27,57,700/- along with interest. 

--- Page 773 ---
3 
 
5. In the impugned judgment, the High Court affirmed 
the Tribunal’s findings, holding that the overall 
compensation awarded was just and proper. The High 
Court upheld the hypothetical approach of assessing 
only a notional managerial contribution of the 
deceased, reasoning that the continuation of the sole 
proprietorship business by the son justified the 
reduced assessment of income. 
6. The appellants have challenged these findings, 
contending that the lower courts erred in law by 
arbitrarily splitting the sole proprietor's income and 
assessing a notional figure despite established ITR 
records. It is further contended that the mere 
inheritance or continuation of a business by a legal 
heir does not negate the loss of dependency, which must 
be assessed with reference to the deceased's earning 
capacity on the date of the accident. 
7. Having heard learned counsel for the parties and 
upon perusal of the material on record, including the 
Income Tax Returns and financial documents of the 
deceased, we are of the considered view that the 

--- Page 774 ---
4 
 
assessment of compensation warrants enhancement in 
order to meet the ends of justice. Interest of justice 
would be best served by further enhancing the 
compensation. Taking a holistic view of the matter, we 
deem it appropriate to award an additional lump s um 
amount. 
8. Respondent No. 3 – HDFC Ergo General Insurance 
Company Limited is hereby directed to pay a lump sum 
amount of Rs. 10,00,000/- (Rupees Ten Lakh only) to the 
appellants. 
9. The aforementioned amount of Rs. 10,00,000/- shall 
be paid in addition to the compensation that already 
stands awarded by the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal, 
Palwal, and affirmed by the High Court. 
10. The Respondent Insurance Company shall directly 
deposit the said lump sum amount into the bank accounts 
of the claimants/appellants in the same proportion as 
directed in the original award dated 27.08.2024, within 
a period of six weeks from the date of this order. The 
appellants shall furnish the requisite bank account 

--- Page 775 ---
5 
 
particulars to learned counsel for the respondents 
without delay. 
11. In the event of default, the appellant(s) shall be 
entitled to interest on the outstanding amount at the 
rate awarded by the Labour Court/Tribunal.

1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This  appeal  arises  from  an  order  dated
18.09.2025  in  Criminal  Appeal  No.100  of  2025
passed by the Bombay High Court rejecting the
regular bail prayer of the appellant in connection
with FIR being C.R. No.103 of 2015 registered at
Shirdi Police Station, District Ahmednagar.
1

--- Page 782 ---
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.8529/2026
4. At the outset, the learned counsel for the
appellant submits that the appellant has suffered
pending trial incarceration of well over 11 years.
It  is  stated  that  he  is  in  custody  since
23.05.2015.
5. The aforesaid fact is not disputed.
6. Having  regard  to  the  long  period  of
incarceration, pending trial, without expressing
any opinion on the merits of the appeal, we are of
the view that the appellant is entitled to be
released on bail at this stage.
7. The  appeal  is,  accordingly,  allowed.  The
order passed by the High Court rejecting the bail
prayer of the appellant is set aside.
8. The appellant shall be released on bail on
such terms and conditions as the trial court may
deem fit to impose in the facts and circumstances
of the case. One of the conditions of bail shall
be that appellant shall not threaten the witnesses
and in case he threatens the witnesses it shall be
open  for  the  Trial  Court  to  deal  with  an
application for cancellation of bail.
2

Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.8529/2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.     of 2026
[ARISING OUT OF SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRL.) NO.7351/2026]
RAJENDRA PRATAP SINGH & ANR.                 Appellant(s)
                          VERSUS
STATE OF U.P & ANR.                          Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 23.03.2026,
passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Judicature  at  Allahabad
rejecting the anticipatory bail prayer of the appellants in
connection with First Information Report (FIR) No.416/2024
registered at P.S. Cantt, District Varanasi.
4. After considering the submission of learned counsel
for the appellants, on 29.04.2026, the following interim
protection order was passed:
“1. Issue notice, returnable in four weeks.
2.  Notice  on  behalf  of  State  has  been
accepted  by  Mr.  Garvesh  Kabra,  learned
Advocate-on-Record.
3. In the meantime, it is provided that in
the event the petitioners are arrested in
connection  with  FIR  No.0416/2024  dated
22.10.2024  registered  at  Police  Station
Cantt.,  District–Varuna  (Commissionerate
1

--- Page 795 ---
Varanasi),  they  shall  be  released  on  a
personal bond of Rs. 25,000/- subject to an
undertaking that they shall cooperate in the
investigation and make themselves available
for interrogation as and when required by
the investigating agency. They shall also
submit  undertaking  that  they  will  not
threaten  the  witnesses  or  tamper  the
evidence”
 
5. On service of notice, the respondent is represented.
6. We have heard learned counsel for the parties.
7. We do not find a good reason to discharge the interim
protection granted earlier.  Consequently, we deem it
appropriate to dispose of the appeal by making the interim
order absolute subject to the following two conditions.
(A) The appellants shall co-operate in the investigation
and make themselves available for interrogation as and
when required by the investigating agency.
(B) Within three weeks from today, they shall submit bail
bonds to the satisfaction of the Trial Court concerned
along with an undertaking that they shall not threaten
the witnesses or tamper the evidence.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.     of 2026
[ARISING OUT OF SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRL.) NO.7110/2026]
N. SAI ALIAS N. SAI KUMAR                     Appellant(s)
                           VERSUS
THE STATE OF ANDHRA PRADESH                  Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 11.02.2026,
passed by the High Court of Andhra Pradesh at Amaravati
rejecting the anticipatory bail prayer of the appellant in
connection with Crime No.126 of 2025 registered at Alipiri
Police Station, Tirupati District.
4. After considering the submission of learned counsel
for the appellant, on 27.04.2026, the following interim
protection order was passed:
“1. Issue notice, returnable in four weeks.
2. Notice on behalf of the respondent has
been accepted by Mr Guntur Pramod Kumar,
AOR. 1 3. In the meantime, it is provided
that in the event the Petitioner is arrested
in  connection  with  Crime  No.  126/2025,
registered  at  P.S.  Alipiri,  District
1

--- Page 803 ---
Tirupati, he shall be released on a personal
bond  of  Rs.  25,000/-  subject  to  an
undertaking that he shall cooperate in the
investigation and make himself available for
interrogation as and when required by the
investigating agency. He shall also submit
an undertaking that he will not threaten the
witnesses or tamper the evidence.”
 
5. On service of notice, the respondent is represented.
6. We have heard learned counsel for the parties.
7. We do not find a good reason to discharge the interim
protection granted earlier.  Consequently, we deem it
appropriate to dispose of the appeal by making the interim
order absolute subject to the following two conditions.
(A) The appellant shall co-operate in the investigation
and make himself available for interrogation as and
when required by the investigating agency.
(B) Within three weeks from today, he shall submit bail
bonds to the satisfaction of the Trial Court concerned
along with an undertaking that he shall not threaten
the witnesses or tamper the evidence.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
WRIT PETITION (C) NO(S). 365/2012
MINOR C.V.GAYATHRI, REP. BY HER FATHER AND 
NATURAL GUARDIAN S. VAITHEESWARAN & ORS.    PETITIONER(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF TAMIL NADU & OTHERS        RESPONDENT(s)
WITH
WRIT PETITION (C) NO.533/2015
WRIT PETITION (C) NO.618/2015 
 O R D E R
1. We have heard learned senior counsel and learned
counsel for the respective parties.
2. The petitioners herein were students who had filed
these Writ Petitions under Article 32 of the Constitution
of India. It is not in dispute that these students have
completed their medical course (MBBS).
3. In the circumstances, we do not find any reason to
consider these Writ Petitions any further on merits as the
petitioner students having completed their MBBS course
would not have any further locus to continue to agitate
the issues which they have raised in these Writ Petitions.
1

--- Page 811 ---
4. In  the  circumstances,  we  dispose  of  these  Writ
Petitions as having been rendered infructuous insofar as
these petitioners are concerned.
5. We say so for the reason that the issues which have
been  raised  by  these  petitioners  have  been  rendered
academic insofar as these petitioners are concerned.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
…………………………………………………J.
                                     ( B.V. NAGARATHNA )
 
…………………………………………………J.
                                      ( UJJAL BHUYAN )   
NEW DELHI; 
MAY 27, 2026

                   Applicant-in-person, AOR                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Learned senior counsel appearing on behalf of the National
Medical Commission (NMC) has filed a chart stating thereunder the
status  of  payment  of  stipend  made  to  interns/junior
residents/senior residents by the respective medical colleges.
3

--- Page 821 ---
2. It is stated therein that out of 756 medical colleges of under
graduates there is no dispute with regard to payment of stipend by
573 colleges and 176 medical colleges have been established in the
recent past and as such the question of payment of stipend would
not arise in those 176 medical colleges. He would also submit that
7 medical colleges have not being paying stipend for which the NMC
has already taken steps by issuing show cause notices for levying
of penalty and on receipt of reply further steps would be taken by
them. He would also add that one medical college has remained
closed and there are no interns in the said college. He further
elaborates his submission by contending that 562 colleges are
running post graduate courses and they are paying the stipend to
the interns and only two medical colleges do not have any interns
and as such question of payment of stipend by these two (2)
colleges would not arise. His submission is placed on record.
3. Ms. Charu Mathur, learned counsel, is appointed as a nodal
counsel and the learned advocates appearing for the parties are
requested to furnish synopsis, charts and pleadings to her and a
convenience compilation shall be prepared and be furnished to the
Court for issuing further directions in these matters wherever
necessary, by the next date of hearing.
4. List on 24.08.2026. 
(RASHI GUPTA)                                   (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

INFRATECH LTD & ORS.   ...Respondent(s)
WITH
CIVIL APPEAL NO. 4691 OF 2026
         
        
 O R D E R
Civil Appeal No.4696 of 2026
1. We heard Mr. Navin Pahwa, the learned senior counsel
appearing for the appellant (intervenor) and Mr. S.Niranjan
Reddy and Mr.  Dama Seshadri Naidu, the learned senior
counsel appearing for the respondent No.1.
2. We find no error not to speak of any error of law in
the impugned judgment and order passed by the National
Company Law Appellate Tribunal (for short, “the NCLAT”),
Principal Bench, New Delhi.  
3. However, it shall be open for the appellant to take
appropriate  steps  available  to  it  for  revival  of  the
proceedings in accordance with law.
1

--- Page 823 ---
4. With  the  aforesaid,  the  present  appeal  stands
disposed of.
Civil Appeal No.4691 of 2026
5. We heard Mr. Balbir Singh, the learned senior counsel
appearing for the appellant and Mr. S.Niranjan Reddy and Mr.
Dama Seshadri Naidu, the learned senior counsel appearing
for the respondent No.1.
6. We find no error not to speak of any error of law in
the impugned judgment and order passed by the NCLAT.
7. However, in the peculiar facts and circumstances of
this case, we set aside that part of the order by which the
NCLAT imposed cost of Rs.10,00,000/- (Rupees ten lakhs).
8. It is needless to clarify that none of the findings
recorded  by  the  NCLAT  shall  come  in  the  way  of  the
assessment proceedings before the GST authorities.
9. With  the  aforesaid,  the  present  appeal  stands
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.        OF 2026
[arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 7058 OF 2026]
RAM MOHAN KUMAR @ MOHAN KUMAR               APPELLANT
                            VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR                          RESPONDENT
 
O R D E R 
1.   Leave granted.
2. By  the  impugned  judgment  and order  dated 11 th February,
2026  the  High  Court  of  Judicature  at  Patna  has  rejected  the
appellant’s prayer for bail in anticipation of arrest.  
3. Appellant figures as an accused in FIR No. 0131/2025 dated
14th June,  2025,  registered  with  Police  Station-T ariyani  Chowk,
District-Seohar.  It  is  alleged  in  the  FIR  that  the  appellant  has
committed offence(s) punishable under Section(s) 316(2) and 318(4)
of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023.
4. We have heard learned counsel appearing for the appellant as
well as the respondent-State.

--- Page 827 ---
2
5. It is not in dispute that the appellant, in terms of an earlier
order, has joined the investigation.  In such view of the matter and
considering the nature of allegations coupled with the fact that the
offences are magistrate triable, we are of the considered opinion
that custodial interrogation of the appellant is not necessary and
that there is sufficient ground for the appellant to be admitted to an
order for grant of bail in anticipation of arrest.
6. Accordingly,  the  impugned  judgment  and  order  stands  set
aside.
7. It is directed that in the event of the appellant being arrested
in connection with proceedings arising out of the aforesaid FIR, he
shall be released on bail by the arresting/investigating officer/trial
court on terms and conditions to be fixed by the trial court.
8. It is made clear that if the appellant breaches any of the terms
and conditions imposed by it, the trial court shall be at liberty to
cancel the bail of the appellant.
9. Needless  to  observe,  the  appellant  shall  not,  directly  or
indirectly, by making inducement, threat or promise, dissuade any
person acquainted with the facts of the case from disclosing such
facts to any police officer or to the court.
10. Additionally,  should  the  investigating  officer  require  the
appellant to face further interrogation, the latter will be under an
obligation to respond failing which any omission in this behalf may

--- Page 828 ---
3
be brought to the notice of the trial court for cancellation of bail.  
11. We clarify that the observations made in this order and grant
of bail will not be treated as findings on the merits of the case.
12. The appeal is allowed on the above terms.
13.  Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
………………..……………………...J.
[DIPANKAR DATTA]  
 ………...……………………………..J.
[SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA]
New Delhi;
May 29, 2026.

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 04-02-2026
in SCRA(D) No.512/2026, 04-02-2026 in CRMA No.1/2026, 05-05-2026 in

                                VERSUS
STATE OF GUJARAT & ORS.                            Respondents
I.A. No.160459/2026-CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
I.A. No.160455/2026-EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
I.A. No.160457/2026-EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE DIPANKAR DATTA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA
For Petitioner(s) : Ms. Pinky Behera, AOR
                    Mr. Davies, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : Ms. Swati Ghildiyal, AOR
Mr. Sankalp Suman, Adv. 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Petitioner  has suffered 21  years  of actual incarceration pursuant to a
conviction recorded by the trial court under Section 302 of the Indian Penal
Code, 1860. 
3. He applied for premature release/parole, which was not considered by the
authorities. This resulted in filing of a special criminal application 1 before the
1  Special Criminal Application (Direction) No.512 of 2026
1

--- Page 863 ---
High Court. The same is pending.
4. By filing these special leave petitions, an order is prayed for expediting
the decision on such special criminal application. 
5. We find that the High Court has fixed 25 th June, 2026 for hearing the
special criminal application. 
6. In such view of the matter, we request the High Court to decide the
application  upon  hearing  the  parties  on  25 th June,  2026,  or  immediately
thereafter.  
7. If the respondent-State has not yet filed but wishes to file any counter-
affidavit, it may do so by 15 th June, 2026. Rejoinder thereto, if any, may be filed
by the petitioner by 22nd June, 2026.
8. The special leave petitions stand disposed of on the above terms.
9. All points and contentions on merits are kept open. 
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
    (MANIK KUMAR)                           (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)
SENIOR PERSONAL ASSISTANT                     COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA  
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ALOK ARADHE
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. M.P.Siddiqui, Adv.
                   Mr. Chand Qureshi, AOR
                   Mr. Mohd Azam, Adv.
                   Ms. Shabnoor, Adv.
                   Ms. Shazia Parveen, Adv.
                   Ms. Farha Siddiqui, Adv.
                   Mr. Md Ashraf Siddiqui, Adv.
                   Mr. Ankur Rana, Adv.
                   Mr. Chandan Kumar Mandal, Adv.
                   Mr. Roshan Lal, Adv.
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Siddhant Sharma, AOR
                   Mr. Vikram Choudhary, Adv.
                                     
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The instant Special Leave Petition is against the judgment and
order dated 29.03.2023 passed by the High Court of Punjab and
Haryana in COCP No. 3538 of 2018 dismissing the Contempt Petition
filed by the petitioner herein. 
1 of 4

--- Page 867 ---
2. The short facts necessary for disposal of the present Special
Leave Petition are that the petitioner was arrested on 19.08.2017.
He alleges that he was produced before the learned JMFC Court,
Ludhiana, Punjab on 20.08.2017, when he was said to have made oral
complaint about “ill treatment” by the police. However, it is
admitted that no formal complaint was made. 
3. On 09.09.2017 and 13.09.2017, he made formal applications for
medical examination, based on which the learned JMFC Court, vide
order dated 19.09.2017, directed that the petitioner should be sent
for medical examination at the Civil Hospital, Ludhiana. The
grievance of the petitioner is that the said order was not complied
with. He filed Contempt Petition No. 3538 of 2018 before the High
Court of Punjab and Haryana, which came to be dismissed by the
order impugned before us on 29.09.2023. 
4. In the meanwhile, the criminal proceedings culminated in an
order of acquittal on 05.01.2022.
5. In the Special Leave Petition against the dismissal of the
Contempt Petition by the High Court, this Court issued notice on
08.12.2023. When the matter came up for hearing before this Bench
on 29.04.2026, we passed the following order:
“1.  The  Jail  Superintendent,  Central  Jail,
Ludhiana, who was to carry out the order passed
by JMIC dated 19.09.2017 in case arising out of
FIR No. 343 dated 19.08.2017, shall file an
affidavit explaining the circumstances in which
the order was not complied with. 
2. The State shall ensure that the requisite
2 of 4

--- Page 868 ---
affidavit is filed by the Jail Superintendent
within a period of two weeks from today.
 
3. Re-list the matter on 22.05.2026.” 
6. Following our direction, Mr. Surinder Pal Khanna, retired AIF
(Prisons)-Cum-Superintendent, Central Jail, Patiala, Punjab filed a
detailed affidavit explaining the circumstances in which the order
could not be complied with. As indicated earlier, the circumstance
relate to events that occurred way back in 2017. In any event of
the matter, as the issue concerns human rights, we wanted to know
the actual position and relevant facts before taking any decision
on  the  Contempt  Petition.  After  explaining  the  facts  and
circumstances, Mr. Surinder Pal Khanna has explained the position
in his affidavit, the relevant portion is as follows:
"20. That it is also pertinent to refer here
that while deciding the application for medical
examination  on  dated  19.09.2017,  no  prior
response or status report was sought from the
jail authorities by the Ld. Trial Court, which
is generally followed. Further, a perusal of
the record would show that the said application
was argued in person by Smt. Anupam Thakur, the
wife  of  accused  Mukesh  Thakur,  and  there
appears to be a bona fide mistake at the
subordinate level due to ignorance, whereby the
staff and medical officer proceeded with the
examination of Mukesh Thakur alone. While it
was incumbent upon the subordinate staff to
comply with the order in its entirety, the
specific circumstances of the wife's appearance
for  one  accused  likely  contributed  to  the
administrative oversight without any malafide
intent on the part of the deponent.
21. That it is respectfully submitted that the
deponent has maintained a distinguished and
unblemished  service  record  throughout  his
3 of 4

--- Page 869 ---
professional  career,  during  which  he  was
honored  with  the  President's  Award  for
Meritorious Services in 2006 and the Tinka-
Tinka National Award in 2019. The deponent has
consistently held the majesty of the law and
judicial mandates in the highest esteem and has
always ensured strict compliance with court
orders. However, if this Hon'ble Court reaches
the conclusion that any mistake has occurred on
the part of the deponent, or that a lapse
transpired at the subordinate level resulting
in any non-compliance, the deponent tenders his
most sincere, unconditional, and unqualified
apology for the same."
7. It is evident from the above that Mr. Surinder Pal Khanna,
while explaining the circumstances, has also stated that if his
conduct is considered to be violative of the order of the Court, he
tenders unconditional and unqualified apology.
8.  We are satisfied with the explanation. The learned counsel
for the petitioner has gracefully agreed for the Court to close the
contempt proceedings. Having considered the affidavit in detail, we
are inclined to accept the unconditional and unqualified apology
and close the contempt proceedings. 
9. The Special Leave Petition is accordingly disposed of. 
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
(KAPIL TANDON)                                  (NIDHI WASON)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR
4 of 4

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO. 1745 OF 2026
IN
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 6620 OF 2026
AMIYA KUMAR SAHOO & ORS. … PETITIONERS
VERSUS
STATE OF ODISHA & ORS. … RESPONDENTS
WITH
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO. 1746 OF 2026
IN
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 6638 OF 2026
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO. 1747 OF 2026
IN
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 6615 OF 2026
AND
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO. 1748 OF 2026
IN
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 6604 OF 2026
O R D E R
1. Seeking modification in the common order dated 13.02.2026
passed by this Court in the captioned special leave petitions
these miscellaneous applications have been filed. Learned counsel
for the applicants contends that the special leave petitions
filed by the State Government on the same issue have been
dismissed. The consequential effect of the disposal of the

--- Page 883 ---
2
special leave petitions filed by the Applicants who were private
parties is that they would derive the benefits if they are found
eligible. We take the said fact and the order of this Court in
the case of Muslemin Mahammad & ors. vs. State of Odisha & Ors.
[Civil Appeal 3904 of 2026 decided on 25.03.2026] on record.
2. In  the  light  of  the  dismissal  of  the  special  leave
petitions  filed  by  the  State  government,  the  case  of  the
Applicants is required to be considered in right earnest in terms
of the order in the aforementioned civil appeal if the Applicants
herein are eligible.
3. With the aforesaid clarification, the present Miscellaneous
Applications stand disposed of.

CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.         OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 7042 of 2026)
SATYENDRA PATHAK @ SATYENDRA PANDIT   … APPELLANT
VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH & ANR.   … RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1) Leave granted. 
2) The appellant has approached this Court praying
for quashing of FIR and entire chargesheet in respect
of Crime No. 135/2025 dated 29.09.2025 registered at
Police  Station  –  Balrampur,  Distt.  Balrampur-
Ramanujganj (C.G.) for the offences punishable under
Section 64(2)(m), 332(b) of BNS and under Section
3(2)(v)  of  Schedule  Caste  &  Schedule  Tribes
(Prevention of Atrocities) Act and under Section 4 &
6 of Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act,
2012.
3) While issuing notice on 27.04.2026, it was noted
that when the appellant had approached this Court for
grant of pre-arrest bail in connection with the same
case in SLP (Crl.) No. 2121/2026, the victim appeared
on  video  conferencing  from  the  office  of  Legal
1

--- Page 887 ---
Services Authority, Vidhik Sahyak Kendra, Bagicha,
District  Jashpur  and  stated  that  she  gave  an
affidavit in favour of the appellant and wished that
he be granted anticipatory bail. Accordingly, this
Court granted anticipatory bail to the appellant.
4) In similar terms, the victim was noticed in the
present appeal, whereafter she again appeared through
video conferencing on 25.05.2026 and took the same
stand  in  favour  of  the  appellant.  However,  with
intent  to  bring  her  intention  on  record,  her
statement was directed to be recorded before the
competent Sessions Court on 26.05.2026. Pursuant to
the order her statement was recorded on 26.05.2026 as
under: –
“1. A report was registered depicting me as the
complainant in Crime No. 135/2025 under Section
64(2)(m), 332(b) of the B.N.S. at Police Station
Balrampur. Neither any report was lodged by me
against accused Satyendra Pathak at Police Station
Balrampur (C.G.) nor was any written complaint
submitted by me prior to that at Police Station
Bagicha, District Jashpur (C.G.). I had dispute
with Satyendra Pathak regarding the construction of
a house; apart from this, the accused has not
committed any incident with me. The accused has not
committed any incident of rape with me.
2.Earlier in this matter, it was stated by me that
I have no objection on anticipatory bail being
granted to accused. I do not want any action
against accused Satyendra Pathak and I want the
case  registered  against  the  accused  Satyendra
Pathak at Police Station Balrampur bearing Crime
No. 135/2025 under Section 64(2)(m), 332(b) of the
B.N.S. be quashed.”
2

--- Page 888 ---
5) On perusal, we are of the considered opinion that
since she does not wish to pursue the case any
further against accused, therefore without expressing
anything  otherwise  as  per  willingness  of  the
complainant-victim, we deem it appropriate to quash
the  aforementioned  FIR  and  criminal  proceedings
pending against the appellant. 
6) Accordingly, the present appeal stands allowed
and  the  FIR  No.  135  of  2025  dated  29.09.2025
registered  at  Police  Station  –  Balrampur,  Distt.
Balrampur-Ramanujganj (C.G.) and criminal proceedings
against  the  appellant  are  hereby  quashed.  The
proceedings pending before the Court shall also be
quashed and be consigned to record.  
7) Pending applications, if any, stand disposed of. 
……………………………………………………………,J.
        [J.K. MAHESHWARI]
……………………………………………………………,J.
 [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
New Delhi;
May 27, 2026.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
SLP (C) Nos. 9419-9423/2024
SLP(C) No. 13884-13885/2025 
SLP(C) No. 10064-10065/2026
1) In these cases, the issue as involved is regarding
2

--- Page 892 ---
posting/assignment  of  the  post  of  HoD  in  Pharmacology
Department.  The petitioners relied upon Regulation 3.10 of
the  Teachers  Eligibility  Qualifications  in  Medical
Institutions Regulations, 2022. The High Court decided that
the post of HoD would be filled by rotation and not by
seniority, as such, held against the petitioners.  The said
order is under challenge. 
2) While issuing notice, vide order dated 29.04.2024 in
SLP (C)Nos. 9419-9423/2024, order dated 14.05.2025 in SLP
(C)Nos. 13884-13885/2025 and order dated 13.03.2026 in SLP
(C)No. 10064-10065/2026, operation of the order of the High
Court  was  stayed.  Meaning  thereby,  the  petitioners  are
continuing on the post of HoDs.  
3) During  the  pendency  of  the  present  special  leave
petitions,  the  Medical  Institutions  (Qualifications  of
Faculty) Regulations, 2025, has come into force. The said
Regulations throw some light to deal with the issue to hold
the post of HoD.  
4) In  SLP  (C)  Nos.  9419-9423/2024,  Mr.Siddharth
Bhatnagar,  learned  senior  counsel  appearing  for  the
petitioner submits that the said Regulations does not apply
to his case while Mr.Devadatt Kamat, learned senior counsel
for respondent Nos. 2 and 3, Mr. Shailesh Madiyal, learned
senior counsel for respondent Nos. 4 and 5 and Mr. Avishkar
Singhvi, learned counsel for respondent-State, submits that
these Regulations are having application.  
3

--- Page 893 ---
5) In the light of 2022 or 2025 Regulation, the issue of
holding the post of HoD is required to be looked into on
merit.  However,  on  applicability  of  2025  Regulation
controverted by both sides, it is required to be examined in
detail by the authority.  In the interregnum, we deem it
appropriate how the autonomous body by its wisdom thinks on
the applicability of 2025 Regulation, may be brought on
record.  Since stay is in operation and due to paucity of
time, hearing could not be possible, therefore, prior to
next date of hearing, in our view, the adjudication by the
autonomous  body  or  its  committee  on  applicability  of
regulations may be brought on record.  On placing the claim
by parties within two weeks from the date of uploading, the
college concerned shall constitute a committee which shall
decide the issue as indicated and its findings be placed
before next date of hearing.  The above directions shall
also apply to SLP (C)Nos. 13884-13885/2025 and SLP(C) No.
10064-10065/2026.
6) List on 11th August, 2026.  
(NIDHI AHUJA)                   (NAND KISHOR)
    DEPUTY REGISTRAR             ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
4

--- Page 894 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
WRIT PETITION (C) NO(S). 365/2012
MINOR C.V.GAYATHRI, REP. BY HER FATHER AND 
NATURAL GUARDIAN S. VAITHEESWARAN & ORS.    PETITIONER(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF TAMIL NADU & OTHERS        RESPONDENT(s)
WITH
WRIT PETITION (C) NO.533/2015
WRIT PETITION (C) NO.618/2015 
 O R D E R
1. We have heard learned senior counsel and learned
counsel for the respective parties.
2. The petitioners herein were students who had filed
these Writ Petitions under Article 32 of the Constitution
of India. It is not in dispute that these students have
completed their medical course (MBBS).
3. In the circumstances, we do not find any reason to
consider these Writ Petitions any further on merits as the
petitioner students having completed their MBBS course
would not have any further locus to continue to agitate
the issues which they have raised in these Writ Petitions.
1

--- Page 895 ---
4. In  the  circumstances,  we  dispose  of  these  Writ
Petitions as having been rendered infructuous insofar as
these petitioners are concerned.
5. We say so for the reason that the issues which have
been  raised  by  these  petitioners  have  been  rendered
academic insofar as these petitioners are concerned.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
…………………………………………………J.
                                     ( B.V. NAGARATHNA )
 
…………………………………………………J.
                                      ( UJJAL BHUYAN )

MAY 27, 2026

                   Mr. Augustine Peter, Adv.
                   Ms. Lakshmi Sree P., Adv.
                   Ms. Lebina Baby, Adv.
                   
                   Applicant-in-person, AOR                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Learned senior counsel appearing on behalf of the National
Medical Commission (NMC) has filed a chart stating thereunder the
status  of  payment  of  stipend  made  to  interns/junior
residents/senior residents by the respective medical colleges.
3

--- Page 905 ---
2. It is stated therein that out of 756 medical colleges of under
graduates there is no dispute with regard to payment of stipend by
573 colleges and 176 medical colleges have been established in the
recent past and as such the question of payment of stipend would
not arise in those 176 medical colleges. He would also submit that
7 medical colleges have not being paying stipend for which the NMC
has already taken steps by issuing show cause notices for levying
of penalty and on receipt of reply further steps would be taken by
them. He would also add that one medical college has remained
closed and there are no interns in the said college. He further
elaborates his submission by contending that 562 colleges are
running post graduate courses and they are paying the stipend to
the interns and only two medical colleges do not have any interns
and as such question of payment of stipend by these two (2)
colleges would not arise. His submission is placed on record.
3. Ms. Charu Mathur, learned counsel, is appointed as a nodal
counsel and the learned advocates appearing for the parties are
requested to furnish synopsis, charts and pleadings to her and a
convenience compilation shall be prepared and be furnished to the
Court for issuing further directions in these matters wherever
necessary, by the next date of hearing.
4. List on 24.08.2026. 
(RASHI GUPTA)                                   (AVGV RAMU)

CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.4696   OF 2026
BENGAL COLD ROLLERS PVT LTD   ...Appellant(s)
                  Vs.
ATTLURU SREENIVASULU REDDY, 
SUSPENDED DIRECTOR OF KLSR 
INFRATECH LTD & ORS.   ...Respondent(s)
WITH
CIVIL APPEAL NO. 4691 OF 2026
         
        
 O R D E R
Civil Appeal No.4696 of 2026
1. We heard Mr. Navin Pahwa, the learned senior counsel
appearing for the appellant (intervenor) and Mr. S.Niranjan
Reddy and Mr.  Dama Seshadri Naidu, the learned senior
counsel appearing for the respondent No.1.
2. We find no error not to speak of any error of law in
the impugned judgment and order passed by the National
Company Law Appellate Tribunal (for short, “the NCLAT”),
Principal Bench, New Delhi.  
3. However, it shall be open for the appellant to take
appropriate  steps  available  to  it  for  revival  of  the
proceedings in accordance with law.
1

--- Page 907 ---
4. With  the  aforesaid,  the  present  appeal  stands
disposed of.
Civil Appeal No.4691 of 2026
5. We heard Mr. Balbir Singh, the learned senior counsel
appearing for the appellant and Mr. S.Niranjan Reddy and Mr.
Dama Seshadri Naidu, the learned senior counsel appearing
for the respondent No.1.
6. We find no error not to speak of any error of law in
the impugned judgment and order passed by the NCLAT.
7. However, in the peculiar facts and circumstances of
this case, we set aside that part of the order by which the
NCLAT imposed cost of Rs.10,00,000/- (Rupees ten lakhs).
8. It is needless to clarify that none of the findings
recorded  by  the  NCLAT  shall  come  in  the  way  of  the
assessment proceedings before the GST authorities.
9. With  the  aforesaid,  the  present  appeal  stands
disposed of.

1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.        OF 2026
[arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 7058 OF 2026]
RAM MOHAN KUMAR @ MOHAN KUMAR               APPELLANT
                            VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR                          RESPONDENT
 
O R D E R 
1.   Leave granted.
2. By  the  impugned  judgment  and order  dated 11 th February,
2026  the  High  Court  of  Judicature  at  Patna  has  rejected  the
appellant’s prayer for bail in anticipation of arrest.  
3. Appellant figures as an accused in FIR No. 0131/2025 dated
14th June,  2025,  registered  with  Police  Station-T ariyani  Chowk,
District-Seohar.  It  is  alleged  in  the  FIR  that  the  appellant  has
committed offence(s) punishable under Section(s) 316(2) and 318(4)
of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023.
4. We have heard learned counsel appearing for the appellant as
well as the respondent-State.

--- Page 911 ---
2
5. It is not in dispute that the appellant, in terms of an earlier
order, has joined the investigation.  In such view of the matter and
considering the nature of allegations coupled with the fact that the
offences are magistrate triable, we are of the considered opinion
that custodial interrogation of the appellant is not necessary and
that there is sufficient ground for the appellant to be admitted to an
order for grant of bail in anticipation of arrest.
6. Accordingly,  the  impugned  judgment  and  order  stands  set
aside.
7. It is directed that in the event of the appellant being arrested
in connection with proceedings arising out of the aforesaid FIR, he
shall be released on bail by the arresting/investigating officer/trial
court on terms and conditions to be fixed by the trial court.
8. It is made clear that if the appellant breaches any of the terms
and conditions imposed by it, the trial court shall be at liberty to
cancel the bail of the appellant.
9. Needless  to  observe,  the  appellant  shall  not,  directly  or
indirectly, by making inducement, threat or promise, dissuade any
person acquainted with the facts of the case from disclosing such
facts to any police officer or to the court.
10. Additionally,  should  the  investigating  officer  require  the
appellant to face further interrogation, the latter will be under an
obligation to respond failing which any omission in this behalf may

--- Page 912 ---
3
be brought to the notice of the trial court for cancellation of bail.  
11. We clarify that the observations made in this order and grant
of bail will not be treated as findings on the merits of the case.
12. The appeal is allowed on the above terms.
13.  Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
………………..……………………...J.
[DIPANKAR DATTA]  
 ………...……………………………..J.
[SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA]
New Delhi;
May 29, 2026.

--- Page 913 ---

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
SLP (C) Nos. 9419-9423/2024
SLP(C) No. 13884-13885/2025 
SLP(C) No. 10064-10065/2026
1) In these cases, the issue as involved is regarding
2

--- Page 932 ---
posting/assignment  of  the  post  of  HoD  in  Pharmacology
Department.  The petitioners relied upon Regulation 3.10 of
the  Teachers  Eligibility  Qualifications  in  Medical
Institutions Regulations, 2022. The High Court decided that
the post of HoD would be filled by rotation and not by
seniority, as such, held against the petitioners.  The said
order is under challenge. 
2) While issuing notice, vide order dated 29.04.2024 in
SLP (C)Nos. 9419-9423/2024, order dated 14.05.2025 in SLP
(C)Nos. 13884-13885/2025 and order dated 13.03.2026 in SLP
(C)No. 10064-10065/2026, operation of the order of the High
Court  was  stayed.  Meaning  thereby,  the  petitioners  are
continuing on the post of HoDs.  
3) During  the  pendency  of  the  present  special  leave
petitions,  the  Medical  Institutions  (Qualifications  of
Faculty) Regulations, 2025, has come into force. The said
Regulations throw some light to deal with the issue to hold
the post of HoD.  
4) In  SLP  (C)  Nos.  9419-9423/2024,  Mr.Siddharth
Bhatnagar,  learned  senior  counsel  appearing  for  the
petitioner submits that the said Regulations does not apply
to his case while Mr.Devadatt Kamat, learned senior counsel
for respondent Nos. 2 and 3, Mr. Shailesh Madiyal, learned
senior counsel for respondent Nos. 4 and 5 and Mr. Avishkar
Singhvi, learned counsel for respondent-State, submits that
these Regulations are having application.  
3

--- Page 933 ---
5) In the light of 2022 or 2025 Regulation, the issue of
holding the post of HoD is required to be looked into on
merit.  However,  on  applicability  of  2025  Regulation
controverted by both sides, it is required to be examined in
detail by the authority.  In the interregnum, we deem it
appropriate how the autonomous body by its wisdom thinks on
the applicability of 2025 Regulation, may be brought on
record.  Since stay is in operation and due to paucity of
time, hearing could not be possible, therefore, prior to
next date of hearing, in our view, the adjudication by the
autonomous  body  or  its  committee  on  applicability  of
regulations may be brought on record.  On placing the claim
by parties within two weeks from the date of uploading, the
college concerned shall constitute a committee which shall
decide the issue as indicated and its findings be placed
before next date of hearing.  The above directions shall
also apply to SLP (C)Nos. 13884-13885/2025 and SLP(C) No.
10064-10065/2026.
6) List on 11th August, 2026.  
(NIDHI AHUJA)                   (NAND KISHOR)
    DEPUTY REGISTRAR             ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
4

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.         OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 7042 of 2026)
SATYENDRA PATHAK @ SATYENDRA PANDIT   … APPELLANT
VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH & ANR.   … RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1) Leave granted. 
2) The appellant has approached this Court praying
for quashing of FIR and entire chargesheet in respect
of Crime No. 135/2025 dated 29.09.2025 registered at
Police  Station  –  Balrampur,  Distt.  Balrampur-
Ramanujganj (C.G.) for the offences punishable under
Section 64(2)(m), 332(b) of BNS and under Section
3(2)(v)  of  Schedule  Caste  &  Schedule  Tribes
(Prevention of Atrocities) Act and under Section 4 &
6 of Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act,
2012.
3) While issuing notice on 27.04.2026, it was noted
that when the appellant had approached this Court for
grant of pre-arrest bail in connection with the same
case in SLP (Crl.) No. 2121/2026, the victim appeared
on  video  conferencing  from  the  office  of  Legal
1

--- Page 935 ---
Services Authority, Vidhik Sahyak Kendra, Bagicha,
District  Jashpur  and  stated  that  she  gave  an
affidavit in favour of the appellant and wished that
he be granted anticipatory bail. Accordingly, this
Court granted anticipatory bail to the appellant.
4) In similar terms, the victim was noticed in the
present appeal, whereafter she again appeared through
video conferencing on 25.05.2026 and took the same
stand  in  favour  of  the  appellant.  However,  with
intent  to  bring  her  intention  on  record,  her
statement was directed to be recorded before the
competent Sessions Court on 26.05.2026. Pursuant to
the order her statement was recorded on 26.05.2026 as
under: –
“1. A report was registered depicting me as the
complainant in Crime No. 135/2025 under Section
64(2)(m), 332(b) of the B.N.S. at Police Station
Balrampur. Neither any report was lodged by me
against accused Satyendra Pathak at Police Station
Balrampur (C.G.) nor was any written complaint
submitted by me prior to that at Police Station
Bagicha, District Jashpur (C.G.). I had dispute
with Satyendra Pathak regarding the construction of
a house; apart from this, the accused has not
committed any incident with me. The accused has not
committed any incident of rape with me.
2.Earlier in this matter, it was stated by me that
I have no objection on anticipatory bail being
granted to accused. I do not want any action
against accused Satyendra Pathak and I want the
case  registered  against  the  accused  Satyendra
Pathak at Police Station Balrampur bearing Crime
No. 135/2025 under Section 64(2)(m), 332(b) of the
B.N.S. be quashed.”
2

--- Page 936 ---
5) On perusal, we are of the considered opinion that
since she does not wish to pursue the case any
further against accused, therefore without expressing
anything  otherwise  as  per  willingness  of  the
complainant-victim, we deem it appropriate to quash
the  aforementioned  FIR  and  criminal  proceedings
pending against the appellant. 
6) Accordingly, the present appeal stands allowed
and  the  FIR  No.  135  of  2025  dated  29.09.2025
registered  at  Police  Station  –  Balrampur,  Distt.
Balrampur-Ramanujganj (C.G.) and criminal proceedings
against  the  appellant  are  hereby  quashed.  The
proceedings pending before the Court shall also be
quashed and be consigned to record.  
7) Pending applications, if any, stand disposed of. 
……………………………………………………………,J.
        [J.K. MAHESHWARI]

IN

O R D E R
1. Seeking modification in the common order dated 13.02.2026
passed by this Court in the captioned special leave petitions
these miscellaneous applications have been filed. Learned counsel
for the applicants contends that the special leave petitions
filed by the State Government on the same issue have been
dismissed. The consequential effect of the disposal of the

--- Page 947 ---
2
special leave petitions filed by the Applicants who were private
parties is that they would derive the benefits if they are found
eligible. We take the said fact and the order of this Court in
the case of Muslemin Mahammad & ors. vs. State of Odisha & Ors.
[Civil Appeal 3904 of 2026 decided on 25.03.2026] on record.
2. In  the  light  of  the  dismissal  of  the  special  leave
petitions  filed  by  the  State  government,  the  case  of  the
Applicants is required to be considered in right earnest in terms
of the order in the aforementioned civil appeal if the Applicants
herein are eligible.
3. With the aforesaid clarification, the present Miscellaneous
Applications stand disposed of.

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  27433/2023
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  29-03-2023
in COCP No. 3538/2018 passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana 
at Chandigarh]
DR. LUCKY @ JAI HIND GUPTA                         Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
DR. G.S. BHINDER & ANR.                            Respondent(s)
 
Date : 22-05-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA  
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ALOK ARADHE
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. M.P.Siddiqui, Adv.
                   Mr. Chand Qureshi, AOR
                   Mr. Mohd Azam, Adv.
                   Ms. Shabnoor, Adv.
                   Ms. Shazia Parveen, Adv.
                   Ms. Farha Siddiqui, Adv.
                   Mr. Md Ashraf Siddiqui, Adv.
                   Mr. Ankur Rana, Adv.
                   Mr. Chandan Kumar Mandal, Adv.
                   Mr. Roshan Lal, Adv.
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Siddhant Sharma, AOR
                   Mr. Vikram Choudhary, Adv.
                                     
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The instant Special Leave Petition is against the judgment and
order dated 29.03.2023 passed by the High Court of Punjab and
Haryana in COCP No. 3538 of 2018 dismissing the Contempt Petition
filed by the petitioner herein. 
1 of 4

--- Page 951 ---
2. The short facts necessary for disposal of the present Special
Leave Petition are that the petitioner was arrested on 19.08.2017.
He alleges that he was produced before the learned JMFC Court,
Ludhiana, Punjab on 20.08.2017, when he was said to have made oral
complaint about “ill treatment” by the police. However, it is
admitted that no formal complaint was made. 
3. On 09.09.2017 and 13.09.2017, he made formal applications for
medical examination, based on which the learned JMFC Court, vide
order dated 19.09.2017, directed that the petitioner should be sent
for medical examination at the Civil Hospital, Ludhiana. The
grievance of the petitioner is that the said order was not complied
with. He filed Contempt Petition No. 3538 of 2018 before the High
Court of Punjab and Haryana, which came to be dismissed by the
order impugned before us on 29.09.2023. 
4. In the meanwhile, the criminal proceedings culminated in an
order of acquittal on 05.01.2022.
5. In the Special Leave Petition against the dismissal of the
Contempt Petition by the High Court, this Court issued notice on
08.12.2023. When the matter came up for hearing before this Bench
on 29.04.2026, we passed the following order:
“1.  The  Jail  Superintendent,  Central  Jail,
Ludhiana, who was to carry out the order passed
by JMIC dated 19.09.2017 in case arising out of
FIR No. 343 dated 19.08.2017, shall file an
affidavit explaining the circumstances in which
the order was not complied with. 
2. The State shall ensure that the requisite
2 of 4

--- Page 952 ---
affidavit is filed by the Jail Superintendent
within a period of two weeks from today.
 
3. Re-list the matter on 22.05.2026.” 
6. Following our direction, Mr. Surinder Pal Khanna, retired AIF
(Prisons)-Cum-Superintendent, Central Jail, Patiala, Punjab filed a
detailed affidavit explaining the circumstances in which the order
could not be complied with. As indicated earlier, the circumstance
relate to events that occurred way back in 2017. In any event of
the matter, as the issue concerns human rights, we wanted to know
the actual position and relevant facts before taking any decision
on  the  Contempt  Petition.  After  explaining  the  facts  and
circumstances, Mr. Surinder Pal Khanna has explained the position
in his affidavit, the relevant portion is as follows:
"20. That it is also pertinent to refer here
that while deciding the application for medical
examination  on  dated  19.09.2017,  no  prior
response or status report was sought from the
jail authorities by the Ld. Trial Court, which
is generally followed. Further, a perusal of
the record would show that the said application
was argued in person by Smt. Anupam Thakur, the
wife  of  accused  Mukesh  Thakur,  and  there
appears to be a bona fide mistake at the
subordinate level due to ignorance, whereby the
staff and medical officer proceeded with the
examination of Mukesh Thakur alone. While it
was incumbent upon the subordinate staff to
comply with the order in its entirety, the
specific circumstances of the wife's appearance
for  one  accused  likely  contributed  to  the
administrative oversight without any malafide
intent on the part of the deponent.
21. That it is respectfully submitted that the
deponent has maintained a distinguished and
unblemished  service  record  throughout  his
3 of 4

--- Page 953 ---
professional  career,  during  which  he  was
honored  with  the  President's  Award  for
Meritorious Services in 2006 and the Tinka-
Tinka National Award in 2019. The deponent has
consistently held the majesty of the law and
judicial mandates in the highest esteem and has
always ensured strict compliance with court
orders. However, if this Hon'ble Court reaches
the conclusion that any mistake has occurred on
the part of the deponent, or that a lapse
transpired at the subordinate level resulting
in any non-compliance, the deponent tenders his
most sincere, unconditional, and unqualified
apology for the same."
7. It is evident from the above that Mr. Surinder Pal Khanna,
while explaining the circumstances, has also stated that if his
conduct is considered to be violative of the order of the Court, he
tenders unconditional and unqualified apology.
8.  We are satisfied with the explanation. The learned counsel
for the petitioner has gracefully agreed for the Court to close the
contempt proceedings. Having considered the affidavit in detail, we
are inclined to accept the unconditional and unqualified apology
and close the contempt proceedings. 
9. The Special Leave Petition is accordingly disposed of. 
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
(KAPIL TANDON)                                  (NIDHI WASON)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR
4 of 4

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.     of 2026
[ARISING OUT OF SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRL.) NO.7351/2026]
RAJENDRA PRATAP SINGH & ANR.                 Appellant(s)
                          VERSUS
STATE OF U.P & ANR.                          Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 23.03.2026,
passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Judicature  at  Allahabad
rejecting the anticipatory bail prayer of the appellants in
connection with First Information Report (FIR) No.416/2024
registered at P.S. Cantt, District Varanasi.
4. After considering the submission of learned counsel
for the appellants, on 29.04.2026, the following interim
protection order was passed:
“1. Issue notice, returnable in four weeks.
2.  Notice  on  behalf  of  State  has  been
accepted  by  Mr.  Garvesh  Kabra,  learned
Advocate-on-Record.
3. In the meantime, it is provided that in
the event the petitioners are arrested in
connection  with  FIR  No.0416/2024  dated
22.10.2024  registered  at  Police  Station
Cantt.,  District–Varuna  (Commissionerate
1

--- Page 975 ---
Varanasi),  they  shall  be  released  on  a
personal bond of Rs. 25,000/- subject to an
undertaking that they shall cooperate in the
investigation and make themselves available
for interrogation as and when required by
the investigating agency. They shall also
submit  undertaking  that  they  will  not
threaten  the  witnesses  or  tamper  the
evidence”
 
5. On service of notice, the respondent is represented.
6. We have heard learned counsel for the parties.
7. We do not find a good reason to discharge the interim
protection granted earlier.  Consequently, we deem it
appropriate to dispose of the appeal by making the interim
order absolute subject to the following two conditions.
(A) The appellants shall co-operate in the investigation
and make themselves available for interrogation as and
when required by the investigating agency.
(B) Within three weeks from today, they shall submit bail
bonds to the satisfaction of the Trial Court concerned
along with an undertaking that they shall not threaten
the witnesses or tamper the evidence.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
……………………………………………………………………………J
                              [MANOJ MISRA]
……………………………………………………………………………J
                              [MANMOHAN]
New Delhi
May 29, 2026

     IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

Transfer Petition(s)(Civil)  No(s).  2471/2025
ARCHANA KUMARI SINGH                               Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
JITESH KUMAR                                       Respondent(s)
 O R D E R
1. By way of this petition, the petitioner is
seeking transfer of Matrimonial (Divorce) Suit
No.497/2025,  titled  “JITESH  KUMAR  Vs.  ARCHANA
KUMARI SINGH” from the Court of Principal Judge,
Family Court, Patna to the Family Court, Howrah,
West Bengal.
2. Heard  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
parties.
3. In the facts and circumstances of the case,
and the material available before us on record,
the petitioner has made out a ground for transfer
of the present petition and accordingly, the same
is allowed.
4. Hence, as prayed for, Matrimonial (Divorce)
Suit No.497/2025 is directed to be transferred
from the Court of Principal Judge, Family Court,

--- Page 987 ---
2
Patna to the Family Court, Howrah, West Bengal.
5. The entire record pertaining to Matrimonial
(Divorce) Suit No.497/2025, shall be transmitted
forthwith  by  the  transferor  Court  to  the
transferee Court.
6. The  transfer  petition  is,  accordingly,
allowed.
7. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  stand
closed.
......................J.
  (PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (C) NO.2446 OF 2025
ANKITA SIKDAR (MAJUMDER)                      PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
JIBAK MAJUMDER @ JISHNU MAJUMDER              RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. This petition is filed under Section 25 of the Code of
Civil Procedure, 1908, seeking transfer of a case bearing MAT
Suit No. 86 of 2023 titled “ Jibak Majumder @ Jishnu Majumder
vs. Ankita Sikdar (Majumder) , pending before the Additional
District and Sessions Judge, Fast Track Court, Cooch Behar,
West Bengal to the Family Court, Dhubri, Assam.
2. The grounds raised by the petitioner for seeking the
transfer are that she does not have any source of income; she
has to take care of her widow mother; and also on account of
long  distance  she  is  unable  to  effectively  defend  the
proceedings at Cooch Behar.
3. On such consideration, we are of the view that transfer
deserves to be allowed. Consequently, the Transfer Petition is
allowed.  
4. Accordingly, case bearing MAT Suit No. 86 of 2023 titled
“Jibak Majumder @ Jishnu Majumder vs. Ankita Sikdar (Majumder) ,
pending before the Additional District and Sessions Judge, Fast
Track Court, Cooch Behar, West Bengal  is transferred  to the
Family Court, Dhubri, Assam.
1

--- Page 998 ---
5. The transferor Court shall forward the records pertaining
to the said case to the transferee Court, as expeditiously as
possible.
6. Parties are directed to appear before the concerned Court
on 13th July, 2026.
7. The  respondent-husband  is  permitted  to  attend  the
proceedings virtually, unless his presence is required by the
trial Court. 
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
...............................J.
(SANJAY KAROL)          
...............................J.
  (SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA)    
NEW DELHI;
MAY 21, 2026
2

1. By this petition, petitioner-wife seeks
transfer of HMP No. 3003/2025 titled as “Singh
Rajankumar vs. Pragati Singh”, pending before
Family Court Judge at Ahmedabad, Gujarat to the
Family Court at Durg, Chhattisgarh.

parties and have perused the record.
3. Upon consideration of the submissions and
perusal of materials available on record, we
are of the view that this Transfer Petition
deserves to be allowed. Accordingly, HMP No.
3003/2025  titled  as  “Singh  Rajankumar  vs.
Pragati Singh”, pending before Family Court
Judge at Ahmedabad, Gujarat is transferred to
the Family Court at Durg, Chhattisgarh. The
1

--- Page 1004 ---
transferor  Court  shall  transmit  the  entire
record of the case forthwith to the transferee
Court.

it appropriate to observe that it will be open
to  the  parties  to  appear  through  video
conferencing platform except on occasions where
their physical presence is necessary. 
5. The Transfer Petition is allowed in the
above terms. 
6. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall
stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.564/2026
SUBIR ROY & ORS.     PETITIONERS
                                VERSUS
DEVI RAM     RESPONDENT
WITH
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO. 237/2026
O R D E R
1. The present petitions have been filed under Section 25 of the
Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (for short “CPC”) and Section 406 of
the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 by the petitioners seeking
transfer of Recovery Suit bearing CS No.633/2025, titled as "Devi
Ram Vs. Subir Roy & Ors.", pending before the Additional Civil
Jude, Senior Division, Dera Bassi to the Principal Civil Judge at
Rewa, Madhya Pradesh and Maintenance Case bearing MNT125/51/2025,
titled as “Devi Ram Vs. Sonia”, pending before the Principal Judge,
Family Court, Dera Bassi to the Principal Judge, Family Court,
Rewa, Madhya Pradesh. 
2. Learned counsel appearing for the respondent – father has
vehemently opposed the transfer by contending that he is 69 years
old and he is unable to be physically present or travel and also
suffering from dementia.
3. Having gone through the material available on record and also
1

--- Page 1013 ---
the facts of the case, we are of the view that these Transfer
Petitions deserve to be allowed. Accordingly, CS No.633/2025,
titled as "Devi Ram Vs. Subir Roy & Ors.", pending before the
Additional Civil Jude, Senior Division, Dera Bassi is transferred
to  the  Principal  Civil  Judge  at  Rewa,  Madhya  Pradesh  and
Maintenance Case bearing MNT125/51/2025, titled as “Devi Ram Vs.
Sonia”, pending before the Principal Judge, Family Court, Dera
Bassi is transferred to the Principal Judge, Family Court, Rewa,
Madhya Pradesh.
4. Records shall be sent by the Court where proceedings are
pending to the transferee Court promptly.
5. A copy of this order be sent to the concerned courts for
compliance.
6. The Transfer Petitions stand allowed in the aforesaid terms.
7. In the light of abovesaid submission made by the learned
counsel appearing for the respondent, this Court permits the
respondent – father to appear virtually before the transferee
Court. 
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.  
.................J.
  (ARAVIND KUMAR)
.................J.
(PRASANNA B. VARALE)
NEW DELHI;
MAY 29, 2026.

                   Mr. Sanidhya Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Ankit Handa,Adv.
                   
1

--- Page 1019 ---
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. The present petitions arise from a common order
passed by a Division Bench of the High Court dismissing
Appeal No.45 of 2023 (filed by the petitioner-Municipal
Corporation of Greater Mumbai) and Appeal No.347 of 2019
(filed by respondent No.2-The Empire Building Occupants
Welfare Association) respectively and thereby affirming
the order passed by a Single Judge in a suit instituted
by the respondent No.1 on Notice of Motion No.1115 of
2005  along  with  Notice  of  Motion  No.1320  of  2013
respectively.
3. The  High  Court  while  dismissing  the  appeals
observed in paragraphs 80, 81 and 82 respectively as
under:
“80. For all the above reasons, we dismiss
this appeal.  However, we once again clarify that
nothing in the impugned order or this order
prevents the MCGM or any other party, if so
entitled, from taking recourse to the due process
of law as against the Plaintiff.
81. Further, we clarify that the observations
or findings in the impugned order or this order
are only prima facie.  They should not influence
the Court when deciding the suit finally.  The
suit should be disposed of in accordance with the
law and on its own merits after  considering the
2

--- Page 1020 ---
evidence that the parties might present therein
and the law on the subject.
82. Both  the  Appeals  and  any  interim
applications therein are disposed of without any
order for costs.”
4. We  heard  Mr.  Siddharth  Bhatnagar,  the  learned
senior counsel appearing for the petitioner-Corporation,
Mr. Balbir Singh, the learned senior counsel appearing
for the respondent No.1/original plaintiff and Mr. Amit
Sibbal,  the  learned  senior  counsel  appearing  for
respondent Nos. 12 to 18 respectively.
5. We find no error not to speak of any error of law
in the impugned order passed by the High Court.
6. We take notice of the fact that the Suit is of the
year 2004.  We are informed that in the recent past,
issues have been framed. Since the Suit is now almost 22
years old, we request the High Court to start with the
recording of the oral evidence and try to complete the
Suit within a period of one year from today.
7. With the aforesaid, we dispose of the Special Leave
Petitions.
8. Pending application shall also stand disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                         (POOJA SHARMA)
   AR-CUM-PS                              COURT MASTER
3

For Petitioner(s) :

For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. We heard Ms. Madhavi Agrawal, the learned amicus
curiae.
2. Delay condoned. 
3. The learned amicus gave us a fair idea as regards
the  subject matter  of this  litigation and  also the
grievance which the petitioner seeks to redress before
1

--- Page 1022 ---
this Court.
4. We take notice of paragraph 8 of the impugned
order.  The same reads thus:
“8.Petitioner  will  swear/affirm  affidavit
verifying  execution  of  the  authority  letter
executed by Kishanlal and deposit it in the
district Court, Lucknow, accompanied by website
copy of this order.  We request the District
Judge  to  provide  petitioner  with  appropriate
acknowledgment of the deposit to be made as
required under clause-(a) in section 4 of Powers
of Attorney Act, 1882.  Petitioner then will
apply for restoration of the appeal before the
State  Commission  on  producing  acknowledgment
regarding the authority letter already disclosed
in the appeal.  We direct further, petitioner may
adopt same procedure in respect of other persons,
from whom he has obtained power of attorney for
pursuing their claims.”
5. We are of the view that the High Court has taken
due care to protect the interest of all those persons
whose claims have been rejected by the Insurance Company.
The  procedure  as  prescribed  in  paragraph  8  of  the
impugned  order  referred  to  above  will  have  to  be
undertaken.  Even after undertaking the entire exercise,
as prescribed in paragraph 8 of the impugned order, if
nothing is done by the authority concerned, it shall be
open for the petitioner to once again go before the High
Court by filing an appropriate Writ Petition.
2

--- Page 1023 ---
6. Once  the  entire  procedure  is  completed,  the
concerned District Judge shall see to it that appropriate
orders are passed expeditiously. The matter shall be
decided on its own merits.
7. With the aforesaid, this Special Leave Petition and
the pending applications stand disposed of.
8. We  are  grateful  to  Ms.  Madhavi  Agrawal  for
rendering her services as amicus curiae.

For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                        O R D E R
Learned counsel for the petitioner submits
that with respect to the same premises in the other
matter preferred by the brother of the petitioner,
this Court while entertaining the SLP(C) bearing
Diary No. 32859 of 2026 titled ‘ M/s.Ram Suroop Ram
Niwas Vs. Bimla Devi’  has granted interim protection
vide  order dated 27 th May, 2026, at an enhanced rate
of rent. She, therefore, submits that the present
1

--- Page 1040 ---
matter  may also be tagged with the said matter which
is likely to be listed on 15 th July, 2026 and  till
then similar protection may also be provided to the
petitioner in the present case.
It has  also been informed that the order
dated 27th May, 2026 has still not uploaded.  
It  is  further  submitted  that  as  per  the
protection provided by the High Court, the petitioner
has to vacate the premises on 1 st June, 2026 i.e.
today.
Learned counsel further  informs that this
Court while passing the order dated 27 th May, 2026,
had provided protection to the petitioner in the said
matter,  subject to  payment  of  enhanced rent  of
 1,70,000/- (One Lakh and Seventy Thousand Only) per₹
month with effect from 1st June, 2026.
On  the  statement  of  the  learned  counsel
appearing for the petitioner, we direct that this
matter be tagged with  Diary No. 32859 of 2026 titled
‘M/s.Ram Suroop Ram Niwas Vs. Bimla Devi’.
Notice be issued to the respondent, returnable
on 15th July, 2026.
On the said date, both the matter to be
listed.
2

--- Page 1041 ---
In  the  meanwhile,  interim  protection  is
provided to the petitioner to the extent that the
time granted by the High Court  up to 1 st June, 2026
is extended.
The petitioner may not vacate the premises
till 15th July, 2026 subject to payment of enhanced
rent of  1,70,000/- (One Lakh and Seventy Thousand₹
Only) per month to be paid to the respondent on or
before 10th of every month in advance. 
List on 15th July, 2026.
On the next date, proof of advance rent be
also filed by the petitioner.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner before us happens to be the father of the
deceased.
2. He is aggrieved by the order passed by the High Court dated
10.02.2026 releasing the respondent no.1 (original accused) on
bail in a serious offence like murder. He wants that the
impugned order passed by the High Court be set aside, the bail
be cancelled, and the accused be taken back in custody.
3. We heard Mr. Nagendra Singh, learned counsel appearing for
the petitioner, Mr. Jasdeep Singh Dhillon, the learned counsel
appearing for the respondent no.1-accused, and Mr. Abhishek
Saket, the learned counsel appearing for the State.
4. We are informed that the trial is practically at the fag
end. Although, we do not agree at all with the line of reasoning
2

--- Page 1056 ---
SLP(Crl.)  No. 5750/2026
assigned  by  the  High  Court  while  granting  bail,  more
particularly, when the High Court says that one of the grounds
which appealed was, over crowding of jails in the State of Uttar
Pradesh, we would like to observe the progress of the trial.
5. Post this matter for further hearing on 16.07.2026. Trial
to proceed further expeditiously without any hindrance. 
6. We direct that the Cross Case arising from a cross F.I.R
shall also be concluded within a period of two months from
today.
7. Registry shall forward one copy of this order at the
earliest to the concerned Trial Court, i.e., Additional District
Judge/Regular, Bijnor, U.P.

--- Page 1058 ---

5. We want the parties to once again go for fresh mediation.

counsel to act as a Mediator between the parties and try to
bring around some settlement so as to see that the lives of the
two minor children is not effected in any manner.
7. We impressed upon both Sandeep and Sangeeta to save the
marriage at least for the sake of their two minor children. 
2

--- Page 1059 ---
T. P. (Civil)  No. 1449/2025
8. Sandeep  should  speaks  to  his  daughter,  Samridhi,
periodically and Sangeeta shall to see to it that Sandeep is
able to speak to his daughter. In the same manner, Sangeeta
should also speak to her son namely, Samarathya periodically and
Sandeep shall see to it that Samarathya talks with his mother on
phone periodically.
9. The parties shall get in touch with Mr.S.K. Rungta at the
earliest. 
10. Registry to inform about this order to Mr.S.K. Rungta, the
learned Mediator at the earliest.
11. Post this matter for further hearing on 22.07.2026 at 3.00
